growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: The shift this course is about
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
The tools in this course — PostHog, Hogsend, a Meta pixel — are the easy part. You can
install all three in an afternoon. The hard part, and the thing that actually moves the
business, is a change in how you *operate*: you stop treating growth as a thing the
marketing person does over there, and you start treating it as a system your whole team
builds, measures, and improves — the same way you build, measure, and improve the
product itself.

That is what "product-led growth team" means in this course. Not a job title, not a
department, not a SaaS category you have to belong to. It's a way of running a startup
where **the product and its usage data are the engine of growth**, and where growth is an
engineering discipline — instrumented, measured, iterated — rather than a budget you hand
to an agency and hope.

The rest of the course is the concrete version of that idea: measure honestly, keep the
users you earn, then grow into an audience you own, and run the whole thing on a cadence.
This chapter is the *why* underneath all of it.

This operating model has a reputation for being
a big-company luxury — something you adopt once you have a growth team to staff it. The
evidence runs the other way: the smaller the team, the more leverage the model has,
because a four-person company can't afford to spend a quarter building things nobody
uses or pouring money into ads that don't return. The loop this chapter describes is
cheapest exactly when you're small. The most instructive recent case is Lovable —
[covered at length on Lenny's Podcast](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6qAB6aUMIeA) —
which reached $200M in ARR within a year of launch with a team in the dozens, running
precisely this playbook: obsessive instrumentation, activation work before spend, and
growth decisions made by the people building the product. So if you're wondering
whether you're too small for this — you're not. If anything, you're the ideal size
for it.

growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: What \
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
The term was popularised around 2016 by the VC firm OpenView (Blake Bartlett gets the
credit for the label —
[their canonical explainer](https://openviewpartners.com/product-led-growth/) is still
the reference). The strict definition: **the product itself is the primary driver
of acquisition, activation, retention, and expansion** — people find it, try it, get
value, and upgrade largely on their own, without a salesperson in the loop. Slack, Figma,
Notion, Calendly, Zoom, Dropbox are the textbook cases: a free or low-friction entry, a
fast "aha", and expansion that happens bottom-up as more of a team adopts it.

[Wes Bush's definition](https://productled.com/blog/product-led-growth-definition) — he
wrote [the book with the term on the cover](https://productled.com/book) — adds the
useful sharp edge: PLG is a **go-to-market strategy**, a choice about how you acquire
and convert customers, not a product-quality aspiration. "We care about the product" is
not PLG; "the product does the selling" is.

Set against it are the other go-to-market motions:

* **Sales-led** — a rep drives the deal; the product is demoed, not self-served. Normal
  for high-price, complex B2B.
* **Marketing-led** — demand-gen and content fill a pipeline that humans then convert.
* **Hybrid** — most real companies. A self-serve bottom that feeds a sales-assisted top
  (this is what Slack and Figma actually became at scale). The pattern is now so
  standard that [a16z describes the modern enterprise motion](https://a16z.com/growthsales-the-new-era-of-enterprise-go-to-market/)
  as PLG and sales working the same funnel from opposite ends: the product generates
  and qualifies the demand, sales harvests the accounts where usage is dense. Elena
  Verna — who ran growth at Miro, Dropbox and Amplitude — has the practical version:
  [you add sales *on top of* a working self-serve motion](https://www.elenaverna.com/p/why-when-and-how-to-add-sales-to),
  to expand and enterprise-ify accounts the product already won. Sales added to a
  product that can't activate users just industrialises the leak.

Does the strategy work when it fits? The honest answer is that the public-market
evidence says yes, with selection-bias caveats:
[OpenView's PLG Index](https://openviewpartners.com/product-led-growth-index/) has
tracked product-led public companies trading at a premium to the broader SaaS index —
the market pays more for growth that compounds through the product than for growth
bought with a sales budget. Treat that as directional, not destiny; an index of
successful PLG companies can't count the ones the motion didn't suit.

Which is the honest part, because this course won't sell you a religion: **not every
business should be purely product-led.** If you sell a $100k platform to banks, a
self-serve free tier is not your growth engine, and pretending otherwise rarely ends
well. PLG-as-a-go-to-market is a *choice* that depends on your price, your buyer, and
your product's time-to-value.

But there is a second, broader thing the same words can mean — and it's the one this
course is built on. The next piece is that broader idea.

**What to take from it:**

* The strict definition still holds — the product does the selling — but AI has lowered
  the cost of building the "aha" that a product-led motion depends on.
* PLG-as-strategy is a *choice* tied to your price, buyer, and time-to-value; it isn't a
  moral stance about caring how good the product is.

growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: Product-led as an operating model
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
Strip away the SaaS specifics and "product-led" describes a *way of working* that applies
to almost any startup, **whatever it is**:

> You instrument what users do, you read those signals honestly, you form hypotheses
> about where you're losing people, you ship changes — in the product *and* in how you
> message users — and you measure whether the change actually moved the metric. Then you
> do it again.

That loop doesn't care whether you're self-serve or sales-assisted. A marketplace, a
consumer app, a dev tool, a Discord-native game, an e-commerce brand, even a productised
service — all of them have users doing measurable things and a relationship that can be
deepened. All of them can be run by a team that treats growth as a measurable system
instead of a vibe.

Reforge's framing of [growth as a system of product, process, and team](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-system)
makes the same point from the other direction: sustainable growth isn't a person you
hire or a channel you find, it's the *combination* of a product worth retaining, a
process for learning fast, and a team wired to act on what it learns. Miss any leg and
the stool tips — a great process on a product nobody wants measures the failure very
precisely; a great product with no process grows by accident and can't repeat it.

So when this course says "treat your startup as a product-led growth team," it means:
adopt the *operating model* — the instrumentation, the honesty about metrics, the
build-measure-learn loop, the ownership — even if your go-to-market is hybrid or
sales-assisted. You don't have to be a PLG *company* to work like a PLG *team*.

| Your startup is… | The product-led move that still applies |
|---|---|
| A self-serve SaaS | The classic case — free entry, fast aha, bottom-up expansion |
| A marketplace | Instrument both sides; activation = first successful match/transaction |
| A consumer app | Retention *is* the business; lifecycle messaging is the whole game |
| A dev tool | Content + community + a product that proves itself; paid ads are weak |
| A community / web3 game | Identity-linking for perks *is* your activation and your moat |
| E-commerce | Behavioural flows (cart, browse, post-purchase) over batch blasts |
| A productised service | Even here: measure where leads stall, automate the follow-up, own the list |

The tactics shift by type; the operating model doesn't.

growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: Growth is an engineering discipline
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
The single biggest mindset change is this: **growth work looks like engineering work.**

A growth team doesn't "do marketing." It runs experiments. The unit of work is a
hypothesis — *"if we email users who haven't created a project within 48 hours, more of
them will activate"* — and the deliverable is a measured result: it lifted activation by
N points, or it didn't, and you keep or kill it on the evidence. That is the same loop as
shipping a feature behind a flag and reading the metric. (The experiments chapter turns
this into a full experimentation program — a scored backlog, a weekly cadence, and the
honesty rules that keep a high tempo from manufacturing false wins.)

It's worth pausing on the anatomy of that hypothesis, because you'll end up writing
dozens in this format:
**if we \[specific change], \[specific metric] will move \[direction], because
\[a reason grounded in something you observed]**. Each clause does work. The change is
specific enough to ship. The metric is named before you look, so you can't declare
victory on whatever moved. And the *because* forces the hypothesis to come from your
data — a replay you watched, a funnel step that leaks — rather than from a tactic you
saw on Twitter. A backlog of these is a growth program; a backlog of "try referral
program?" is a wish list.

It's worth saying what this discipline is *not*, because the word "growth" drags a
decade of baggage behind it. It is not a bag of tricks. Elena Verna's
["10 growth tactics that never work"](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/10-growth-tactics-that-never-work-elena-verna)
is the best available list of the anti-patterns — copying a competitor's onboarding,
redesigning the pricing page on instinct, launching a referral program before anyone
loves the product, hiring a growth lead to fix a product problem. What they share:
each substitutes *activity* for the loop. The move that works is boring and repeatable
— find the constraint in the data, form a hypothesis, ship, measure, keep or kill.

**What to take from it:**

* Most "growth tactics" fail because they substitute activity for the loop — they aren't
  aimed at a constraint anyone found in the data.
* The durable pattern is boring and repeatable: find the constraint, form a hypothesis,
  ship, measure, keep or kill. Borrowed tactics can work, but only by luck.

Three consequences follow, and they shape everything later in the course:

* **Every feature ships with instrumentation.** A feature you can't observe isn't
  quite finished — events are part of "done" (the instrumentation chapter).
* **Decisions cite the funnel, not opinions.** "I think users are confused" becomes "37%
  drop between signup and first project; here are five session replays of it happening"
  (the daily-dashboard chapter). The funnel is the shared source of truth.
* **The messaging layer is code, owned by the people who build.** The emails and Discord
  pings that pull users back to value are journeys in your repo, version-controlled and
  code-reviewed — not a config someone edits in a SaaS UI that no engineer ever sees
  (the lifecycle chapters). This is the specific reason the course pairs PostHog with Hogsend.

This is also why a tiny team has an *advantage* here. You don't need a growth department.
You need one loop, instrumented well, run by people who can change both the product and
the messaging. A founder who can read a retention curve and ship a journey is the whole
growth team — and that's enough to start.

growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: Who owns growth on a small team
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
"Treat your startup as a growth team" raises an obvious question on a four-person company:
*who?* The answer is not "hire a growth lead." It's **assign the hat, and make the ritual
cheap.**

* **Name one owner.** One person is accountable for the funnel being instrumented, the
  dashboard being read, and the journeys being live. It can be a founder. It's a hat, not
  a hire.
* **Make growth a standing ritual, not a project.** The cadence: a 2-minute daily glance
  at alerts, a 30-minute weekly read of retention + journeys + ad spend, a monthly review
  of **incremental lift** (the extra conversions a change caused, above what would have
  happened anyway) and **blended CAC** (customer-acquisition cost averaged across every
  channel, paid and organic). A system that runs on a rhythm survives; one that runs on
  heroics dies the first busy week.
* **Give the whole team the shared language.** Everyone should know the **North Star**
  (the single metric that best proxies the value your product delivers) and what
  "activated" means for your product. When the same five numbers mean the same thing to
  everyone, you've already beaten most companies.

The maturity ladder most startups climb: **flying blind** (no data) → **analytics
installed but no one looks** → **someone reads a dashboard** → **the team acts on it
automatically through lifecycle journeys** → **growth compounds through an owned audience
and referrals**. This course walks that ladder rung by rung; the goal is the top of it.

It's worth being honest about which rung you're on. The most common self-deception is
believing you're on rung three because a dashboard *exists* — a fairer test is whether
any decision changed in the last month because of it. If the answer is no, rung two is
closer to the truth, and the measurement chapters are where your leverage is.

growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: The machine you're going to build
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
Here's the whole system on one page, so the chapters that follow have somewhere to hang.
Three layers, one loop:

* **See it — PostHog.** Collect events, identify people, and read the
  funnel, retention, and the daily dashboard. You can't fix a leak you can't see.
* **Act on it — Hogsend.** Code-first lifecycle journeys that reach the
  right user at the right moment — welcome, nudge, win-back, dunning (recovering failed
  payments), referral — triggered by the same events you measure, and fired back as events
  so the loop stays measurable.
* **Own it — identity + ads.** Drive traffic, then capture every visitor
  into a channel you own (verified email, linked Discord/Telegram) on an identity graph,
  so you never pay to reach the same person twice — and so your ads and referrals compound.

The division of labour that keeps it simple no matter how much you add: **PostHog detects,
Hogsend acts, you decide.** Hold that split and the system stays legible.

| Layer | Tool | Chapters | The question it answers |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decide what matters | You (AARRR, North Star) | 0, 2 | What are we even optimising? |
| See it | PostHog | 1, 3, 4 | Where are we losing people? |
| Act on it | Hogsend | 5, 6 | How do we pull them back, automatically? |
| Own & grow it | Ads + Hogsend connectors | 7, 8 | How do we acquire without renting our audience? |
| Run it | The cadence + the plan | 9, 10 | How does this become a habit, not a project? |

Two chapters at the end turn the machine into an operation: the cadence chapter is the
rhythm it runs on (the daily/weekly/monthly cadence and the experimentation program),
and the plan chapter is the **30/60/90/180-day plan** — the whole build sequenced into
phases with interactive checklists, exit criteria, and the anti-patterns per phase. If
you're the kind of person who wants to see the plan before the theory, it's there; it
will just keep pointing you back here.

growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: How teams get this wrong
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
Five failure modes, each one a way of *not* being a growth team. You'll recognise at
least one from a company you've worked at:

* **The vibes startup — no data at all.** Decisions by intuition and the loudest voice in
  the room. Fast at first, blind at scale; you can't tell a real improvement from noise.
  The fix is the measurement chapters.
* **The dashboard graveyard — analytics installed, nobody looks.** PostHog is firing, the
  charts exist, and no decision has ever changed because of them. Measurement without a
  ritual and without *action* is theatre. The fix is the daily cadence plus the
  action layer.
* **The blast list — action without measurement.** A weekly newsletter to everyone, batch
  sends, no behavioural triggers, no **holdouts** (a group deliberately left un-messaged,
  so you can prove the sends changed anything at all). It feels like marketing and proves
  nothing. The fix is triggered journeys and **incrementality testing** (measuring the
  lift against that held-out group).
* **The rented-audience trap — growth you don't own.** All acquisition through paid ads,
  every reach re-bought, nothing captured into a channel you control. The moment you stop
  paying, the pipeline goes dark. The fix is the owned-audience flywheel.

A fifth, subtler than the others because it *looks* like doing everything right:
**the tactic collector.** This team ships growth work constantly — a referral program
one month, gamified onboarding the next, a pricing experiment after that — each copied
from a company it worked for, none derived from this product's own data. It's the
pattern Verna's list catalogues, and the tell is that nobody can say which leak
the current project is supposed to plug. Tactics are the *output* of the loop, never
the input.

The through-line: a growth team **measures honestly, acts automatically, and owns its
audience.** Miss any one of the three and you've got a department, not a system.

growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: The one rule: measure → keep → grow
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
If one idea from this course sticks, let it be the order.

Almost every founder's instinct is to reach for acquisition first — run ads, drive
signups, watch the top number climb. It's an understandable instinct, and usually the
most expensive one, because pouring traffic into a product that can't keep users is
just *buying churn* — paying to acquire users a leaky product immediately loses.
Acquisition is the **last** lever, not the first.

So the sequence is deliberate, and the course follows it exactly:

1. **Measure** what's actually happening — so you're working on the real
   leak, not a guessed one.
2. **Keep** the users you already earn — activation and retention, the
   highest-leverage and cheapest growth you have, because those users are already yours.
3. **Grow** into an audience you own — open the tap only once the bucket
   holds water, and make every drop land somewhere you can reach again for free.

The order flexes with your stage, but it never reverses. Pre-launch, "measure" means
instrumenting before day one so you never fly blind at all. Post-revenue, "keep"
includes dunning — recovering failed payments is the fastest revenue you'll ever ship.
At scale, "grow" becomes a portfolio of channels with a referral loop compounding
underneath. What stays constant is that spending on acquisition *before* you can
measure activation and retention is spending blind.

growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
You can't install a mindset, but there are three things worth settling before you
touch a line of code — each takes minutes, and each saves real money later:

1. **Settle who owns growth** — one person accountable for measure → keep → grow. On a
   solo team that's you; writing it down still helps, because a hat is easier to drop
   than to wear.
2. **Try writing what "activated" means for your product** in one sentence — the
   moment a new user first gets real value. The metrics chapter will make it precise;
   what matters now is committing to the concept.
3. **Talk the order through with your team** — retention before acquisition. It's the
   single most valuable sentence to get everyone nodding at before any money goes into
   ads, because it's the one instinct that pulls the other way.

Then, when you're ready: the PostHog chapter is the tool you'll measure with, and why
measuring is the job at all.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [OpenView — What is product-led growth?](https://openviewpartners.com/product-led-growth/) — the origin source; Blake Bartlett coined the term here in 2016.
* [Wes Bush — the PLG definition](https://productled.com/blog/product-led-growth-definition) — the canonical strict definition, and [the book](https://productled.com/book) behind it.
* [OpenView — the Product-Led Growth Index](https://openviewpartners.com/product-led-growth-index/) — the public-market evidence that product-led compounding gets priced at a premium.
* [Reforge — Growth loops are the new funnels](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-loops) — growth as a compounding system rather than a funnel you refill; the engineering-discipline argument at its sharpest.
* [Reforge — Building growth: product, process, and team](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-system) — why growth is a system with three legs, not a hire or a hack.
* [Elena Verna — Why, when, and how to add sales to a PLG motion](https://www.elenaverna.com/p/why-when-and-how-to-add-sales-to) — the hybrid reality: sales layered on top of a working self-serve engine.
* [a16z — Growth+Sales: the new era of enterprise go-to-market](https://a16z.com/growthsales-the-new-era-of-enterprise-go-to-market/) — the same hybrid pattern from the investor's seat.
* [Lenny's Newsletter — 10 growth tactics that never work](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/10-growth-tactics-that-never-work-elena-verna) — the written companion to the Verna interview above; the anti-pattern catalogue.

growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.title: Start here — Becoming a product-led growth team
growth-with-posthog/00-product-led-growt.text:
> **In this chapter:** what product-led growth means in practice, why it's a way of
> *operating* rather than a tactic you bolt on, how it applies to any startup (not just
> self-serve SaaS), what the evidence says, the five ways teams get it wrong, and the map
> of the machine the rest of this course builds. No setup yet — this is the mindset the
> tools serve.

**How this chapter works.** It's nine short pieces — most a one- to three-minute read,
two with a talk worth watching (read the digest and transcript if you'd rather read).
Work through them in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [The shift this course is about](./01-the-shift.mdx) — growth as a system your team builds, not a thing marketing does.
2. [What "product-led growth" means](./02-what-plg-means.mdx) — the strict definition, and the go-to-market motions around it.
3. [Product-led as an operating model](./03-operating-model.mdx) — the universal part, whatever your startup is.
4. [Growth is an engineering discipline](./04-growth-is-engineering.mdx) — hypotheses, not tactics.
5. [Who owns growth on a small team](./05-who-owns-growth.mdx) — assign the hat, make the ritual cheap.
6. [The machine you're going to build](./06-the-machine.mdx) — three layers, one loop, on one page.
7. [How teams get this wrong](./07-how-teams-get-it-wrong.mdx) — the five failure modes.
8. [The one rule: measure → keep → grow](./08-measure-keep-grow.mdx) — the order that never reverses.
9. [Before you move on](./09-before-you-move-on.mdx) — three things to settle, then check yourself.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter.**

* **North Star** — the single metric that best proxies the value your product delivers; the number the whole team steers by.
* **AARRR** — Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue: the five stages of the growth funnel (the "pirate metrics").
* **CAC / blended CAC** — customer-acquisition cost, what it takes to win one customer; *blended* CAC averages that across every channel, paid and organic.
* **Holdout** — a slice of users deliberately left un-messaged, so you can prove a journey caused the lift rather than assuming it.
* **Incrementality** — the extra outcome an action actually caused, above what would have happened anyway.
* **Identity graph** — the record that ties one person's many identifiers (email, device, Discord) to a single profile, so you never pay to reach them twice.
* **ARR** — annual recurring revenue: the yearly run-rate of a subscription business.
* **Dunning** — the sequence that recovers failed subscription payments (expired cards, retries) before they become churn.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.title: Why measure at all
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.text:
You can run a product on intuition for a while. The problem is that intuition can't
tell you *where* you're losing people. You see the top number — signups, traffic —
and the bottom number — revenue, active users — and everything between them is dark.

Measurement turns that dark middle into a sequence of steps you can see through. When
1,000 people land on your site, you want to know how many sign up, how many reach the
moment the product becomes useful, and how many are still around next week. Each of
those is a place you can lose people, and each is a place you can fix. You cannot fix
a leak you cannot see.

This is the first move of the operating model from the opening chapter. A growth team
doesn't argue about whether onboarding is confusing — it *looks*: 41% of new users drop
between signup and first project, here are the replays. Measurement is what converts opinions into a
shared, checkable picture of the funnel, and it's why the course starts here. Everything
downstream — which journeys you build, where you spend on ads, what you tell the team in
the Monday review — is only as good as the data underneath it.

That is the entire job of a product analytics tool: make the leaks visible so you can
work on the right one. PostHog is the tool this course uses to do it. The next few pieces
are what's actually in it, and later in this chapter comes the honest comparison with
everything else you could pick instead.

The discipline that goes *with* the tool — *what* to measure and *why* — is the **AARRR
framework** (Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue), and it gets its own
chapter. This chapter is about the instrument.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.title: One pipeline, one identity
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.text:
PostHog calls itself an all-in-one platform for building products. In practice it's a
stack of products that all sit on **one event pipeline and one model of who your users
are**. That shared foundation is the entire point, and it's worth dwelling on because it's
the thing the alternatives mostly *don't* have: an error links to the session replay that
links to the user's whole analytics history that links to the feature flag they were in —
because it is all the same data, keyed to the same person, in one place.

For most analytics setups, "join the error to the session to the funnel" is a data-
engineering project. In PostHog it's a click. For a small team, that collapse of five
tools into one is most of the value.

**What to take from it:**

* PostHog exists to answer one question — *what should I build next?* — and it's built so
  answering it doesn't need a data-engineering project first.
* You send events from your product with front-end and back-end SDKs; everything else
  (analytics, replay, flags, error tracking, LLM analytics) reads off that one stream.
* The pitch isn't "best at any one thing" — it's "all of it joined, keyed to the same
  person, plus an AI agent to query it for you."

That last point is the whole reason a small team reaches for PostHog over a pile of
point tools. The next piece is the full list of what's in the box.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.title: The whole PostHog suite
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.text:
You don't need all of it. For this course you'll mainly use **Product Analytics**,
**Web Analytics**, and **Session Replay**, plus **Data Pipelines** later for sending
conversions to ad platforms. Here's the whole suite so you know what's there, and what
each piece does for a growth team specifically:

* **Product Analytics** — event-based analysis: trends, funnels, retention, paths,
  stickiness, lifecycle, and raw SQL ([docs](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics)).
  Includes *autocapture* (it records clicks and pageviews with no code). This is the core
  of the course — it's where you find the leaks.
* **Web Analytics** — a Google-Analytics-style dashboard (visitors, sessions, bounce
  rate, channels, geography) sitting on the same data, with far less setup. The
  marketing-site view, for the acquisition numbers.
* **Session Replay** — watch real user sessions play back, with console logs and
  network requests captured ([docs](https://posthog.com/docs/session-replay)). This is
  how you find the *why* behind a drop-off — the qualitative layer under the quantitative
  funnel. Five minutes of replay beats an hour of staring at a chart.
* **Feature Flags** — turn features on/off for a percentage of users or a targeted
  **cohort** (a saved group of users defined by shared traits or behaviour — everyone on
  the paid plan, say), without deploying ([docs](https://posthog.com/docs/feature-flags)).
  The "ship behind a flag" half of the growth-as-engineering loop.
* **Experiments (A/B testing)** — built on feature flags; measures whether a change
  actually moved a metric, with statistical significance
  ([docs](https://posthog.com/docs/experiments)). This is how a hypothesis becomes a
  *measured* result rather than a hunch — and the instrument behind the experimentation
  program you'll run in the experiments chapter.
* **Surveys** — in-app surveys — NPS (Net Promoter Score) and open feedback — targeted
  at specific users. The cheapest way to get the *why* straight from the user.
* **Error Tracking** — captures exceptions, groups them into issues, and links each
  one to the session where it happened. Production health that's joined to behaviour.
* **LLM Analytics** — PostHog's headline 2026 product: traces of individual LLM calls
  (tokens, latency, cost), per-user cost attribution, and evals. Relevant if you ship
  AI features and need to see what they cost per user.
* **Data Warehouse** — connect external sources (Stripe, HubSpot, your database) and
  join them to your event data with SQL. This is how revenue from Stripe meets behaviour
  from your app without a separate ETL (extract, transform, load) tool.
* **Data Pipelines (CDP, a customer data platform)** — *Sources* (managed ingestion),
  *Destinations* (send your events out to Snowflake, ad platforms, Slack, a CRM), and
  *Transformations* (clean or enrich events in flight). You'll use a Destination in the
  ads chapter to send conversions to Meta.
* **PostHog AI ("Max")** — an in-product assistant that answers questions in plain
  language, writes SQL for you, and builds insights. Useful when you don't yet know
  the query language, which is most people on day one.

> ⚠️ PostHog also ships newer products — **Logs**, **Workflows** (its own messaging
> layer), and **Inbox** — that are evolving quickly. Treat their exact status and
> limits as a moving target and check the docs.

Of that list, two products are where a growth team actually *lives*. The next piece is
three minutes of each one working.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.title: The two demos to actually watch
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.text:
Two products are where a growth team actually *lives*, so it's worth seeing each one work
for three minutes before you move on. Product analytics is the quantitative half — funnels,
trends, retention, increasingly with the AI assistant doing the query-writing:

**What to take from it:**

* The daily loop is *ask a question → get a chart*: funnels, trends, and retention are a
  few clicks, and the AI assistant will write the query when you don't know how.
* You don't need to learn a query language on day one to get real answers — which is
  exactly the point for a small team without a data analyst.

And session replay is the qualitative half — the tape you watch to understand *why* the
number you just saw is what it is:

**What to take from it:**

* Replay is the *why* under the *where*: when a funnel shows a drop, you jump straight from
  that data point to the recordings of the people who dropped.
* Console logs and network requests are captured with the session, so "reproduce the bug"
  becomes "watch it happen" — the same move whether you're chasing a support ticket or a
  drop-off.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.title: Events, persons, and why it isn't GA4
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.text:
Everything in PostHog hangs off **events** (a thing that happened) attached to a
**person** (who it happened to). "User signed up", "project created", "page viewed" —
each is an event with properties, tied to a person
([capture docs](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/capture-events)). Get those
two things right and every chart in the product just works. The instrumentation chapter
is about getting them right.

This sounds obvious, but it's the exact place where **web analytics and product
analytics part ways**, and the reason "we already have Google Analytics" isn't an
answer. GA4 does technically have
[an event model](https://developers.google.com/analytics/devguides/collection/ga4/events)
now, but the product is built to answer *marketing* questions — sessions, channels,
campaign attribution — aggregated, sampled at scale, and reported around anonymous
traffic. What it can't honestly answer is the question every chapter of this course
turns on: *what did this specific user do next, and do users who did X retain better
than users who didn't?* User-level funnels, N-day retention cohorts, and jump-to-the-
replay-of-that-drop-off all require events keyed to durable persons — which is the
whole design of a product analytics tool
([PostHog's side-by-side with GA4](https://posthog.com/blog/posthog-vs-ga4) is candid
about what each is for).

For a growth team, events are also the team's **shared language**. "Activated" stops
being a vibe and becomes `project_created` fired within seven days of signup. When
everyone names the same events the same way, the dashboard, the lifecycle journeys,
and the ad conversions all speak to each other for free. The event taxonomy you set up
in the instrumentation chapter is, quietly, the most important architectural decision in
the whole course.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.title: PostHog Workflows vs Hogsend
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.text:
**Hogsend is a code-first lifecycle & marketing-automation engine.** You write your
onboarding emails, drip campaigns, and Discord/Telegram nudges as TypeScript *journeys*
that live in your repo, run on your own infrastructure, and are triggered by the same
PostHog events you're already capturing. It's what makes PostHog **dramatically better
for growth teams that live in code**: the measurement stays in PostHog, and Hogsend turns
those measurements into action you can version, code-review, and trust.

PostHog has its own messaging product (PostHog Workflows, formerly the open-source
Laudspeaker). It's a no-code canvas for marketers, and it's good at what it does:
batch sends and simple drip campaigns targeted on PostHog data.

This course pairs PostHog with **Hogsend** instead, for the lifecycle layer, because
the audience here is people who build, and because the messaging layer is where the
"owned by the people who build" principle actually bites. The split is
deliberate:

| | PostHog Workflows | Hogsend |
|---|---|---|
| Authoring | Visual no-code canvas | Code-first TypeScript (`defineJourney`) |
| Lives in | PostHog cloud | Your repo, your infra, your email provider |
| Best at | Batch + simple drips, by marketers | Durable multi-step journeys, by developers |
| Versioned | A config in a UI | A dependency you pin and code-review |
| Logic | What the canvas exposes | Anything you can express in code |

The clean division of labour, which the rest of the course follows:

* **PostHog detects** — event collection, identity, cohorts, the analytics you read.
* **Hogsend acts** — the journeys that send the emails and Discord/Telegram messages,
  triggered by events, with the results fired back as events PostHog can measure.

To make "code-first" concrete before the lifecycle chapter builds it properly — this is
a real journey shape, abbreviated:

```ts
defineJourney({
  meta: { id: "activation-nudge", trigger: { event: "user.signed_up" } },
  run: async (user, ctx) => {
    await sendEmail({ to: user, template: "welcome" });
    const { timedOut } = await ctx.waitForEvent({
      event: "project.created",
      timeout: hours(48),
    });
    if (timedOut) await sendEmail({ to: user, template: "activation-nudge" });
  },
});
```

Read what that buys you: the branching is ordinary TypeScript (`if`), the "wait up to
48 hours for the activation event" is one line, and the whole thing lives in a pull
request a colleague reviews. Now picture maintaining the same logic as boxes on a
canvas — then picture the version with three more branches. That divergence is the
whole argument, and it compounds with every condition you add.

You don't have to choose one forever. The point is that the *measurement* lives in
PostHog and the *code-first lifecycle automation* lives in Hogsend — and the lifecycle
chapters are entirely about why a code-first journey beats a flowchart once your logic
gets real (behavioural branching, exact-once sends, holdouts you can prove).

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.title: US cloud or EU cloud
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.text:
PostHog Cloud runs in two independent regions:

* **US Cloud** — data hosted in the US (AWS, Virginia).
* **EU Cloud** — data hosted in the EU (AWS, Frankfurt). New EU projects disable IP
  capture by default for a stronger privacy posture
  ([the EU cloud announcement](https://posthog.com/blog/posthog-cloud-eu) has the
  background).

You pick one at signup. This matters because **data does not move between regions and
you cannot merge across them.** If most of your users (or your compliance obligations)
are in Europe, start on EU Cloud. If you guess wrong, fixing it later means a
migration, not a setting.

There's also a **self-hosted** open-source version. It's a real infrastructure
commitment (it runs on ClickHouse) and PostHog actively steers small teams toward
Cloud unless data-residency rules force self-hosting. For this course, Cloud is the
sensible choice — the free tier (next piece) means there's no cost argument for
self-hosting at your stage, and the operational cost of running ClickHouse yourself is
real.

> Hogsend, by contrast, is built to be self-hosted — your database, your email
> provider, your deploy. So a common setup is *PostHog Cloud for analytics +
> self-hosted Hogsend for messaging*, which keeps the customer data and the sending
> on your own infrastructure. The asymmetry is deliberate: analytics is read-heavy and
> fine to delegate; the messaging spine and the **identity graph** (the profile that ties
> one person's many identifiers — email, device, Discord — into a single record) are
> assets you want to own.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.title: What PostHog costs
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.text:
PostHog is **usage-based and free until you're at real scale.** The model:

* Every product has a **monthly free tier that resets each month.** You only pay,
  per-product, once you exceed it.
* There's **no charge for storing historical events** — you're billed once when an
  event is captured, not for keeping it.
* PostHog says roughly **90% of companies use it for free**, and most early-stage
  startups run free for 6–12 months.

Approximate monthly free tiers (these change — treat as a guide, confirm on
[posthog.com/pricing](https://posthog.com/pricing) before relying on them):

| Product | Free per month (approx.) |
|---|---|
| Product Analytics | ~1M events |
| Session Replay | ~5,000 recordings |
| Feature Flags | ~1M requests |
| Surveys | ~1,500 responses |
| Error Tracking | ~100K exceptions |
| Data Warehouse | ~1M rows |

> ⚠️ Two things to verify rather than take on faith, because they move and they bite:
> the **exact free-tier numbers and per-unit prices** (they drift), and **Group
> Analytics** — it's reportedly a paid add-on, and enabling it can cause *all*
> identified events in the project to bill, not just grouped ones. If you're a B2B
> product tempted by company-level rollups, check the current pricing
> page first.

The practical takeaway: you will not pay anything to follow this course, and you'll
have a long runway before cost is a question. When it becomes one, the lever is event
volume — which is exactly why the instrumentation chapter tells you to be deliberate
about what you track rather than capturing everything that moves. The single biggest
cost lever, the anonymous-vs-identified setting, is covered there too; set it right on
day one and your bill can be ~4× smaller.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.title: Why PostHog vs the alternatives
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/0.text:
A growth team should be able to say *why* it chose its tools, not just *that* it did.
PostHog is the course's default, but it is not the only honest choice — here's the real
landscape and where PostHog wins and loses.

* **Mixpanel / Amplitude** — the incumbents of product analytics, and genuinely
  excellent at the analytics itself (Amplitude's behavioural cohorts and Mixpanel's
  reports are mature; on several individual analysis features they remain ahead). What
  they're *not* is all-in-one: no session replay, no feature flags, no CDP, no error
  tracking in the same bill — you'd stitch those from other tools, each with its own
  identity model to reconcile ([PostHog's own comparison](https://posthog.com/blog/posthog-vs-mixpanel)
  is more even-handed than you'd expect from a vendor, and worth reading for the
  feature-by-feature truth). **Choose them over PostHog if** deep, polished analytics is
  the only thing you need and you already have replay/flags elsewhere. **Choose PostHog
  if** you want one pipeline under everything, which is most small teams.
* **Google Analytics 4 (GA4)** — free and ubiquitous, but it's *web* analytics, not
  product analytics: aggregated, sampled at scale, session-centric, and not built for
  user-level funnels or retention cohorts (the events-and-persons piece covered why the
  data model itself is the difference). It will tell you traffic by channel; it will not
  honestly answer "do users who create a project in week one retain better." **Keeping
  GA4 around for marketing-channel reporting is fine — asking it to run your activation
  funnel is the part that won't work.**
* **Segment / RudderStack** — these are a *pipe*, not an analytics tool. They collect
  events once and fan them out to many destinations (including PostHog). Complementary,
  not competitive — and for a small team, often overkill: PostHog's own CDP and a
  direct server-side capture usually cover it. **Reach for a dedicated CDP
  when** you have many destinations and want one canonical event schema feeding all of
  them. RudderStack is the warehouse-first, open-source-leaning option here.
* **Heap** — autocapture-first, like PostHog's autocapture taken as the whole product.
  Good if you never want to define events; weaker on the breadth PostHog gives you.
* **Statsig / LaunchDarkly** — best-in-class experimentation and flags, but single-
  purpose. If A/B testing is your core discipline they're stronger than PostHog's
  experiments; you'd pair them with an analytics tool.
* **Plausible / Fathom** — privacy-first, cookieless *web* analytics. Lovely for a
  content site or a landing page where all you want is traffic and referrers without a
  consent banner. They are not product analytics at all — no persons, no funnels, no
  retention — so they answer a different question than this course asks. Fine to keep
  one running on the marketing site alongside PostHog.
* **The build-your-own stack** — Snowflake/BigQuery + dbt + a BI tool (Looker, Metabase)
  fed by Segment. This is where bigger data teams end up, and it's genuinely more
  powerful and flexible than any packaged tool. It's also a team and a budget you don't
  have yet. **The honest rule:** if you already live in a warehouse-native stack, PostHog
  is a duplicate and you should send events to your warehouse instead. If you don't,
  PostHog gives you 80% of that stack's value this afternoon, for free.

The one-line summary: **PostHog wins on "one event pipeline under everything plus a
generous free tier," which is exactly the shape a small growth team needs.** It loses to
single-purpose tools on depth-in-one-area, and to a warehouse stack on ultimate
flexibility. For the team this course is written for, the trade is clearly worth it.

### A note for your kind of startup

The choice barely changes by business type — PostHog fits a marketplace, a consumer app,
a dev tool, or an e-commerce brand equally — but the *emphasis* does. **Consumer apps**
lean hardest on retention and replay. **B2B/marketplaces** care about Group Analytics
(roll events up to the account/company), which is the one place to check pricing first.
**Dev tools** get unusual value from autocapture on docs + the warehouse join to track
the docs-to-signup path. **E-commerce** often keeps GA4 for channel reporting alongside
PostHog for on-site behaviour. Pick the tool once; tune the emphasis to your funnel.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/1.title: Set up your account
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog/1.text:
Four small things are worth doing while this chapter is fresh — together they take
about ten minutes:

1. Create a PostHog account on the **right region** for your users (US or EU) — it's
   the one decision here that's hard to undo (see the US-or-EU piece).
2. Create one project, keeping your marketing site and your app in the *same* one —
   the payoff is seeing the whole journey, from first visit to paying user, in one
   place.
3. Find your **project API key** (it starts with `phc_`) — you'll want it in the
   instrumentation chapter, and it's safe to expose publicly: it can write events but not
   read your data.
4. If you evaluated alternatives, consider **writing one line on why you chose PostHog**
   (or didn't). A growth team can always answer "why this tool" — and the note earns
   its keep when someone asks in six months.

That's all the setup for now. The next chapter is the part most teams skip and later
wish they hadn't: deciding *what* to measure before wiring anything up.

***

Before you go, run the chapter's key ideas — then check yourself.

***

## Go deeper

* [PostHog — Product analytics docs](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics) — the reference for the half of the tool this course lives in.
* [PostHog — the product analytics overview](https://posthog.com/product-analytics) — the shorter framing of the same, with the current feature surface.
* [PostHog vs GA4](https://posthog.com/blog/posthog-vs-ga4) — the candid side-by-side; useful ammunition for the "we already have GA" conversation.
* [Google — the GA4 event model](https://developers.google.com/analytics/devguides/collection/ga4/events) — read Google's own docs to see the difference from a product-analytics event model first-hand.
* [PostHog vs Mixpanel](https://posthog.com/blog/posthog-vs-mixpanel) — the all-in-one vs point-tool trade, feature by feature.
* [PostHog — Session replay docs](https://posthog.com/docs/session-replay) — setup and the debugging workflow you'll use in the daily-dashboard chapter.
* [PostHog — Feature flags docs](https://posthog.com/docs/feature-flags) and [Experiments docs](https://posthog.com/docs/experiments) — the ship-behind-a-flag half of the loop, and the instrument behind the experimentation program.
* [posthog.com/pricing](https://posthog.com/pricing) — the current free-tier numbers; verify before you rely on the table above.

growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog.title: 1 — What PostHog is, and why you want it
growth-with-posthog/01-what-is-posthog.text:
> **In this chapter:** the case for measuring at all, the full PostHog product suite and
> why each piece matters to a growth team, the event-based data model that makes it all
> work, an honest comparison with the alternatives (Mixpanel, Amplitude, GA4, Segment, and
> the build-your-own stack), the US/EU choice, and a realistic read on pricing. By the end
> you'll know what PostHog is for, why it's the default for a small team running the
> operating model from the opening chapter, and you'll have an account on the right cloud.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into ten short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Where a piece has a video, the idea is the same each time: read the
one-line reason it's worth your time, watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then
read the short digest so the point sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you
need:

1. [Why measure at all](./01-why-measure.mdx) — you can't fix a leak you can't see.
2. [One pipeline, one identity](./02-one-pipeline.mdx) — the structural advantage, in one demo.
3. [The whole PostHog suite](./03-the-suite.mdx) — every product, and what each does for growth.
4. [The two demos to actually watch](./04-two-demos.mdx) — product analytics and session replay.
5. [Events, persons, and why it isn't GA4](./05-events-and-persons.mdx) — the data model.
6. [PostHog Workflows vs Hogsend](./06-workflows-vs-hogsend.mdx) — why this course uses both.
7. [US cloud or EU cloud](./07-us-or-eu.mdx) — the one choice you can't easily undo.
8. [What PostHog costs](./08-what-it-costs.mdx) — usage-based, and free for a long time.
9. [Why PostHog vs the alternatives](./09-why-posthog.mdx) — the honest landscape.
10. [Set up your account](./10-set-up-account.mdx) — the ten-minute setup, then a quiz to check yourself.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter.**

* **Event** — a thing that happened, captured with properties ("user signed up", "project created").
* **Person** — who an event happened to; every event is tied to a durable person.
* **Cohort** — a saved group of users defined by shared traits or behaviour (everyone on the paid plan, say).
* **CDP** — customer data platform: collects your events once and fans them out to other tools (a warehouse, ad platforms, a CRM).
* **NPS** — Net Promoter Score, the one-question loyalty survey ("how likely are you to recommend us?").
* **ETL** — extract, transform, load: the plumbing that moves data between systems and reshapes it on the way.
* **AARRR** — Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue: the five stages of the growth funnel.
* **Identity graph** — the profile that ties one person's many identifiers (email, device, Discord) into a single record.

growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.title: AARRR: the five metrics that matter
growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.text:
In 2007 Dave McClure — then at PayPal, later the founder of 500 Startups — gave a
five-minute talk called *Startup Metrics for Pirates*, and it still frames how growth
teams think about a business. His argument was that a startup drowns in metrics, but only
five categories actually decide whether it grows. They spell **AARRR** — **acquisition,
activation, retention, referral, revenue** — which sounds like a pirate growling, hence
the nickname "pirate metrics." Nearly twenty years of blog posts about growth are
downstream of this one talk, so it's worth the five minutes at the source.

**What to take from it:**

* His founder's rule is to pick five metrics, ten at most, and measure them against a
  customer lifecycle you've *hypothesised* — not against a wall of dashboards.
* Don't switch on referral or "viral marketing" until the product is genuinely good; his
  blunt version is to wait until you're not shipping something people would warn their
  friends about, the same retention-before-scaling logic as the leaky bucket.
* His go-to retention lever is automated lifecycle email timed to the user's journey — on
  join, then +3, +7, +30 days — the exact playbook the back half of this course builds
  with Hogsend.

Nearly two decades on it's still the cleanest map of a business there is, and it's the
thing that turns "growth" from a vague ambition into five owned, measurable numbers — the
shared language a growth team actually runs on. The discipline that grew up around it has
its own primary source too: Sean Ellis coined "growth hacker" in
[a 2010 post](https://www.startup-marketing.com/where-are-all-the-growth-hackers/) to
describe "a person whose true north is growth," working through measurable experiments —
exactly the person this course trains you to be. The term later got debased into "growth
hacks"; the original meaning is just the operating model this course is built around.

The five stages read most cleanly each as a question, with what you'd measure underneath
and a single worked example — 4,000 visitors becoming 22 paying customers — carried right
through them:

### Acquisition — *how do people find you?*

The top of the funnel: people arriving and signing up. You watch visitors by channel,
signups, the signup conversion rate, and **CAC** (customer-acquisition cost — what you pay
to win one customer) broken out by channel. Say 4,000 visitors land this month — 2,200
from organic search, 1,000 from a launch post, 800 from ads — and 260 of them sign up, a
6.5% conversion. Already you can see which channel is cheap and which is vanity.

### Activation — *do new users reach the value?*

The first successful experience — the moment the product clicks — and it is *not* the same
as acquisition: acquisition is that they showed up, activation is that they had a good
first visit. McClure's own words were to "turn the drive-by visitors into users who are
somehow enrolled." You measure the activation rate (the share who complete the key first
action), time-to-value, and onboarding completion. Of those 260 signups, 95 create their
first project in week one — 37%. That 37% is the single most important number here, and
the activation atom later in this chapter is about why.

### Retention — *do they come back?*

Activated users returning and keeping the habit — the engine of compounding growth, and
the stage the rest of the framework exists to feed. You measure N-day retention (D1/D7/D30
— the share still active one, seven, and thirty days after signup), the **DAU/MAU ratio**
(daily active users over monthly active users, a measure of stickiness), churn rate, and
where the retention curve flattens. Of the 95 activated users, 60 are still active at day
30 — and crucially the curve *flattens* there rather than sliding to zero, which is the
signal you have a real product (the retention curve gets its own treatment later in this
chapter).

### Referral — *do they bring others?*

Users liking the product enough to invite people. You measure invites sent per user,
invite acceptance rate, the viral coefficient — the **K-factor** (how many new users each
existing user brings in) — and what fraction of new users arrived by referral. Say 60
retained users send 48 invites and 18 convert: a K-factor of 0.3. Below 1 that's a
discount on CAC, not viral growth; above 1 it compounds on its own.

### Revenue — *do they pay, and how much?*

The business finally capturing value. You measure **MRR and ARR** (monthly and annual
recurring revenue), conversion to paid, **LTV** (lifetime value — the total a customer
pays before they leave), and the **LTV:CAC ratio**, the single number that tells you
whether growth is profitable. Of the retained users, 22 upgrade at $29/mo; with a CAC of
$40 and an LTV of $220, the 3:1-plus LTV:CAC says growth here is worth scaling — *once*
the bucket holds.

> A small note that confuses people: some sources list the last two as Revenue then
> Referral. McClure's own slides put **Referral before Revenue**, but it genuinely
> doesn't matter — the acronym is identical (R-R) either way, so it's not worth
> spending energy on.

AARRR is best read as *the five categories of number to watch*, not as a strict
chronological pipeline every user marches through. A user can refer a friend before they
pay; a user can pay before they're really retained. That distinction is what the next
piece is about.

One habit worth building from day one: when the five numbers get written down, put **who
owns each one** next to them. Five numbers with five owners (fine if one person owns three)
is a growth team; five numbers in a doc is a doc.

growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.title: The leaky bucket
growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.text:
**Your business is a bucket.** Water poured in at the top is newly acquired customers.
Water leaking out of holes in the bottom is customers who churn. The water level is your
active customer base. ([Amplitude's explainer](https://amplitude.com/blog/leaky-bucket-problem)
walks the same image with cohort charts — a **cohort** being a group of users tracked
together over time, say everyone who signed up in the same week — if you want it drawn.)

There are only two ways to raise the level: **pour faster** (acquire more) or **plug the
holes** (retain more).

Almost everyone reaches for the tap first. It feels like progress — the signup number goes
up, the chart is up and to the right, the launch looks like a success. But if the bucket
is full of holes, pouring faster mostly reveals the problem: you spend more, the level
barely moves, and you've simply *bought churn*. That's the honest way to say it — if the
product cannot retain, paid acquisition is just a way of purchasing churn at scale.

The mobile world makes this brutal. Data popularised by Andrew Chen found the average
app **loses about 77% of its daily users within three days, and about 90% within 30
days** ([his write-up of the dataset](https://andrewchen.com/new-data-shows-why-losing-80-of-your-mobile-users-is-normal/)).
Run the math on that: acquire 100 users at $5 each, lose 77 of them in three
days, and you've effectively set $385 on fire — immediately, before you've done
anything wrong except scale a leaky bucket. *(Directional: the 77%/90% figures come
from one widely-cited dataset; treat them as illustrative of the shape, not gospel.)*
Chen's [four-scenario framing](https://andrewchen.com/is-your-website-a-leaky-bucket-4-scenarios-for-user-retention/)
of the same idea is worth internalising: acquisition and retention multiply, so the
same traffic produces wildly different businesses depending on the leak rate.

**This is why the course fixes things in this order: measure → keep → grow.** Plug the
biggest holes (activation and retention) *first*, because retention is the multiplier
on every acquisition dollar. A user who stays is worth more, refers more, and pays
back their acquisition cost — [Brian Balfour's retention essays](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/category/Retention)
make the compounding case in full: retention improvements cascade into every other
AARRR stage, which is why he calls retention the one metric that multiplies rather
than adds. Only once the bucket holds water do you open the tap. That's the whole
argument behind "retention before acquisition" — not a slogan, but a claim about where
the leverage actually sits.

### RARRA: the same point, sharpened

Around 2017, Thomas Petit and Gabor Papp reordered the letters to **RARRA** —
Retention, Activation, Referral, Revenue, Acquisition — specifically to break the
habit of starting with acquisition
([Mind the Product's comparison](https://www.mindtheproduct.com/aarrr-vs-rarra-pirate-metrics-explained/)
covers both orderings). Their blunt version: *"If you don't have retention, you have
nothing."* AARRR is the map of a user's journey; RARRA is the order of *priorities* —
what to fix first for the most leverage.

### The honest counterpoint

So you don't over-rotate: the leaky-bucket idea has a credible critique. The
Ehrenberg-Bass Institute [argues it's over-applied](https://marketingscience.info/the-hole-in-the-leaky-bucket-theory/)
— for many categories, acquiring new and light buyers drives growth *more* than
plugging churn, and obsessing only over retention can cap your ceiling. The balanced
reading: **retention-first is the right default for an early-stage product**, but
"retention is the only thing that matters" is an oversimplification. Fix the bucket,
then absolutely turn on the tap — which is what the later growth chapters, on driving
traffic and building an owned audience, are for. The skill is knowing which phase you're
in, and the retention curve, which gets its own treatment later in this chapter, is how
you tell.

Numbers make the multiplier concrete. The level a bucket settles at is roughly arrivals
divided by the leak rate, so a few points of monthly retention move the whole base — far
more than the same effort spent widening the top. Put your own figures in:

growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.title: Activation and the aha moment
growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.text:
Activation is the highest-leverage stage for most early products, so it's worth going
deep. The **aha moment** is the point a new user first feels the product's value. The
**activation metric** is a measurable stand-in for it: a specific action, or a count of
actions in a time window, that *predicts* whether someone sticks around.

The famous examples:

| Company | The "magic number" |
|---|---|
| **Facebook** | 7 friends in 10 days |
| **Twitter** | follow ~30 accounts |
| **Slack** | a team sends 2,000 messages |
| **Dropbox** | put one file in one folder |

These are real and they're useful — but they're also the single most misread idea in
growth, and misreading them is expensive.

### The magic number is a rally cry, not a law

It's tempting to copy "7 friends" and chase it — and it's the one move here worth
resisting. As Mixpanel put it,
[*"magic numbers are an illusion — a very useful illusion, but an illusion
nevertheless."*](https://mixpanel.com/blog/magic-numbers-are-an-illusion/)
Andrew Chen, talking about Facebook's number, said it *"could have been '10 friends in
12 days' or even 'five friends in one day'"* — it was a **round number picked from the
middle of a range**. Facebook weren't trying to make it precise.

Three things worth understanding about these numbers:

* **They describe an average, not an individual.** One user is hooked after 3 friends;
  another churns after 12. The number is a population-level signal.
* **Their real job is organisational.** A magic number is a mantra that gets every team
  pointed at the same goal. That alignment is most of the value — the same "shared
  language" a growth team runs on.
* **Some are shakier than others.** Slack's 2,000 is a *team*-level number, not
  per-user. Dropbox's "one file in one folder" is repeated everywhere but hard to
  trace to a primary source — Dropbox's *solid, documented* story is its referral
  program (which the owned-audience chapter returns to), not its activation number.

### How to find *your* activation metric (the method that matters)

Rather than borrowing someone else's number, you can find your own — it's a three-step
method (from [Lenny Rachitsky's guide](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/how-to-determine-your-activation)):

1. **Brainstorm candidate aha moments** from your product-usage data — actions that
   plausibly mark "they got it."
2. **Run a correlation/regression analysis** to see whether retention jumps for users
   who hit each candidate. This surfaces patterns.
3. **Run an experiment** — deliberately push more new users to hit the candidate
   moment, and see if retention actually rises.

Step 3 is the one teams skip, and it's the one that counts. The crucial caveat: *"a
good activation metric is causal for your retention, not just correlative."* Steps 1–2
only show correlation — maybe engaged users do X *because* they were already going to
stick. Only an experiment proves that *causing* more X *causes* more retention. Without
it, you can spend months optimising a number users hit on their way out the door.

### Worked example: finding it for a real-ish product

Say you run a design-collaboration tool. You walk the method:

1. **Candidates** from your data: *invited a teammate*, *created 3+ files*, *left a
   comment*, *connected Figma*. All plausible "aha"s.
2. **Correlation:** in PostHog, build a retention insight split by each candidate. Users
   who *invited a teammate in week one* retain at 64% to day 30; everyone else at 19%.
   That's a huge gap — but it might just be that engaged teams invite *and* retain.
3. **Experiment:** you put a "+ invite a teammate" prompt in onboarding for half of new
   signups (a feature flag). If the prompted half retains meaningfully better, you've
   shown *causing* the invite *causes* retention. Now "invite a teammate in week one" is
   your activation metric, and onboarding's whole job is to drive it.

That's the work, and it's the same correlation-vs-causation discipline you'll use again
for lifecycle holdouts in the lifecycle-messaging chapter and, in full, in the chapter on
running experiments. Twitter's team is the model: they found "follow 30 accounts"
correlated with retention — then dug further and found a *better*, more causal signal:
what fraction of those 30 **followed back**. They refined a correlation into something
that actually moves the outcome.

The shape of an activation metric is almost always: **\[quantity] of \[key action]
within \[time window]**, for new users. Making sure "\[key action]" is an event you
actually capture is the instrumentation chapter's whole job.

### What "activation" means for your kind of startup

The metric is yours to find, but the *shape* is predictable by business type:

* **Self-serve SaaS** — the first time the core job gets done (project created, report
  shipped, integration connected).
* **Marketplace** — the first *successful match or transaction*; for a two-sided
  marketplace you have two activation metrics (supply and demand), and liquidity is the
  real game.
* **Consumer / social app** — a habit signal: sessions-per-week, or the network action
  (a follow, a post) that predicts the user comes back.
* **Dev tool** — the first successful API call / first green build / first deploy. The
  "aha" is "it worked in my environment."
* **E-commerce** — first purchase, then second purchase (the repeat is the real
  activation; one-time buyers are barely retained).
* **Community / web3** — the link-and-verify-for-a-perk action (which the owned-audience
  chapter builds into an identity graph) is both the activation *and* the start of that
  graph.

You'll refine this into a tracked event when you instrument the product, and hold
yourself to it in the 30-60-90-180-day plan — it saves to your workbook, so it follows
you there.

Why is this worth so much effort? Because activation is a lever on the traffic you
*already* have. A few points of activation turn the same signups into materially more
retained users, at no extra acquisition cost — onboarding is a growth channel that bills
nobody:

growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.title: The North Star Metric
growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.text:
AARRR gives you five categories. A **North Star Metric (NSM)** gives you the *one*
number on top — the single measure that best captures the value your product delivers,
and that the whole team can rally around. It's the metric a growth team pins to the top
of the dashboard and reads first.

A good North Star (criteria from
[Amplitude's North Star Playbook](https://amplitude.com/resources/north-star-playbook),
the framework's main steward):

* **Reflects customer value**, not company vanity. It should rise when users get more
  value, not just when more users exist.
* **Expresses your strategy** — someone should be able to read it and roughly
  understand what your product is for.
* **Is a leading indicator** — an early signal of impact, not a lagging financial
  result.
* **Is not directly movable.** If you can move it with one button, it's not a North
  Star. You move it *through* inputs.

Bad North Stars are vanity or lagging metrics: registered users, downloads, page
views, and — importantly — **revenue itself** (ARR/MRR, annual and monthly recurring
revenue). Revenue is the score at the end of the quarter; by the time it moves, the game
is already played. The North Star is the leading thing that *causes* the revenue.

Examples that capture value:

| Product | North Star |
|---|---|
| Airbnb | nights booked |
| Spotify | time spent listening |
| WhatsApp | messages sent |
| PostHog (their own) | "Discoveries" — a user viewing an insight/replay for 10+ seconds |

PostHog's own choice is instructive: they picked "Discoveries" because it predicted
retention 2.3× better than the alternatives. They chose an *activation-quality* signal
over a vanity count — exactly the lesson from the activation piece earlier in this
chapter.

A North Star sits too high for any one team to move directly, so you operate it through
**3–5 input metrics** that teams *can* move.
[Reforge's treatment](https://www.reforge.com/blog/north-star-metrics) formalises this
as the input/output distinction: outputs — the North Star, revenue — are for *watching*;
inputs are for *working on*. The inputs usually map straight onto AARRR stages:
acquisition volume, activation rate, frequency, referral rate.

When the inputs move, the North Star rises, and that's your early warning *before*
revenue reacts. One headline number on top, a handful of inputs beneath it, each owned by
someone — "you own activation rate, you own weekly frequency." That ownership is how a
small team distributes the growth work without needing a growth department.

A worked input tree, for the design-collaboration tool the activation piece used: North
Star = *weekly files collaborated on* (value delivered, leading, not directly movable).
Inputs: new signups (acquisition), % inviting a teammate in week one (activation — the
metric that piece landed on), files-per-active-team (frequency/depth), % of new signups
arriving via a shared file (a growth loop, unpacked in the other-frameworks piece). Four numbers,
four owners, one story: every input plausibly *causes* the North Star, and the North Star
plausibly causes revenue. If the causal chain won't draw for your candidate NSM, that's
usually the sign to pick again.

growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.title: Other frameworks, and when to reach for them
growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.text:
AARRR is the default, not the only lens. A growth team should know what else exists so it
picks the right tool for the question:

* **Growth loops (Reforge).** The sharpest critique of AARRR:
  [a funnel is *linear*](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-loops) — people enter at
  the top and fall out the bottom, and you're always refilling the top. A **loop** is a
  system where the output of one cycle becomes the input of the next: a new user
  creates content → that content gets indexed/shared → it brings the next user. Loops
  compound; funnels leak. The funnel is the right lens for *seeing* leaks; the loop is
  the right lens for *designing* growth that doesn't need constant refilling. The
  owned-audience flywheel later in the course is a growth loop. **Use loops when** you're
  designing how growth sustains itself, not just measuring conversion.
* **The Sean Ellis PMF survey.** Before you optimise activation, it's worth knowing if
  you have product-market fit at all. Ellis's test, from
  [the original Startup Pyramid essay](https://seanellis.substack.com/p/the-startup-pyramid):
  ask users "how would you feel if you could no longer use this product?" If **40%+ say
  "very disappointed,"** you likely have PMF and should pour into growth; below it, fix
  the product first. A cheap, fast gut-check that complements the retention curve — and
  the gate the 30-60-90-180-day plan puts in front of scaling paid spend.
* **HEART (Google).** Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task-success — a UX-
  quality framework, more granular than AARRR for *product* health. **Use it when** the
  question is "is this feature good," not "is the business growing."
* **The Pirate-metrics critique itself.** Some teams find AARRR too acquisition-shaped
  and prefer to lead with retention (RARRA, sharpened in the leaky-bucket piece) or with
  loops. There's no prize for orthodoxy — the frameworks are lenses, and the honest move
  is to use AARRR to find the leak, loops to design the fix, and the PMF survey to know
  which phase you're even in.

The retention curve deserves one more beat here, because it's the chart that ties the
PMF question to the leaky bucket. Plot the % of a signup **cohort** — everyone who joined
in the same window — still active N days later: every curve falls at first; the question
is whether it **flattens above zero** (some set of users has made your product a habit —
that plateau *is* product-market fit, expressed as data) or decays to nothing (no amount
of acquisition will save it).

**What to take from it:**

* Weebly took ~18 months to reach product-market fit, and the tell was the *shape* of
  the new-users-per-day curve: every press hit (TechCrunch, Newsweek, Time) spiked then
  settled, but kept drifting *down* between spikes — exactly what "no PMF yet" looks like
  as data.
* The metric that matters early is returning usage — the cohort who come back within
  1/3/7/30 days — not signups, which can climb while active users actually shrink.
* His full gut-check is three independent reads: returning usage, an **NPS** (Net
  Promoter Score — a −100 to +100 gauge of how likely users are to recommend you) above
  \~50 (Weebly's was ~80 in the early days), and cohort-based renewal rates over blended
  churn — with the felt sign being customers "beating a path to your door."

What counts as a *good* plateau varies enormously by category — a consumer social app
and an SMB SaaS have utterly different healthy shapes — so before panicking or
celebrating, it's worth calibrating against
[Lenny Rachitsky's operator-surveyed benchmarks](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/what-is-good-retention-issue-29)
for what "good" and "great" retention look like in yours.

The point is not to collect frameworks. It's that a growth team reaches for the lens that
answers the question in front of it, instead of forcing every question through one.

growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.title: From framework to instrument
growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.text:
Every AARRR stage maps to a specific chart you can build in PostHog — the framework
stops being theory the moment each stage has a number on a dashboard:

| AARRR stage | What you build in PostHog |
|---|---|
| Acquisition | A **funnel** from first-touch → signup, split by channel and UTM (the tags on a link that mark where a click came from); CAC per channel |
| Activation | The **activation funnel** (signup → aha threshold); regression to find the aha; experiments to prove it |
| Retention | The **retention curve** (does it flatten above zero?) and **cohort** tables |
| Referral | A funnel of invites sent → accepted → activated; the K-factor |
| Revenue | Free→paid conversion funnel; LTV:CAC by cohort |
| North Star | One headline number pinned to a dashboard, with its input metrics beneath |

The single most important chart in that list is the **retention curve**. If it decays
to zero, you have a leaky bucket and no amount of acquisition will save you. If it
flattens above zero — some cohort of users sticks forever — you have something real to
pour into. It's also how you answer the only strategic question that matters right now:
*am I in the "fix the bucket" phase or the "open the tap" phase?*

You now know what to measure and why. Next, you'll wire PostHog up to measure it.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Sean Ellis — The Startup Pyramid](https://seanellis.substack.com/p/the-startup-pyramid) — the primary source for the 40% "very disappointed" PMF survey.
* [Sean Ellis — Find a growth hacker for your startup (2010)](https://www.startup-marketing.com/where-are-all-the-growth-hackers/) — where "growth hacking" was coined, and what it originally meant.
* [Amplitude — The North Star Playbook](https://amplitude.com/resources/north-star-playbook) — the full framework the North Star piece draws on, with worked input trees.
* [Reforge — How to choose and measure North Star metrics](https://www.reforge.com/blog/north-star-metrics) — the input-vs-output metric distinction that makes an NSM operable.
* [Lenny Rachitsky — What is good retention?](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/what-is-good-retention-issue-29) — operator-surveyed benchmarks by category; calibrate your plateau here.
* [Lenny Rachitsky — How to determine your activation metric](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/how-to-determine-your-activation) — the three-step method from the activation piece, in full.
* [Amplitude — The leaky bucket problem](https://amplitude.com/blog/leaky-bucket-problem) — the bucket, drawn with cohort charts.
* [Andrew Chen — Is your website a leaky bucket?](https://andrewchen.com/is-your-website-a-leaky-bucket-4-scenarios-for-user-retention/) and [losing 80% of mobile users is normal](https://andrewchen.com/new-data-shows-why-losing-80-of-your-mobile-users-is-normal/) — the acquisition×retention multiplication, and the brutal mobile data.
* [Brian Balfour — Retention essays](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/category/Retention) — why retention multiplies every other growth lever.
* [Mixpanel — Magic numbers are an illusion](https://mixpanel.com/blog/magic-numbers-are-an-illusion/) — the useful-illusion argument quoted in the activation piece.
* [Ehrenberg-Bass — The hole in the leaky bucket theory](https://marketingscience.info/the-hole-in-the-leaky-bucket-theory/) — the credible counterpoint, so you don't over-rotate on retention.

growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.title: 2 — AARRR and the leaky bucket
growth-with-posthog/02-aarrr-and-the-lea.text:
> **In this chapter:** the AARRR framework — acquisition, activation, retention,
> referral, revenue — and what each stage really means, with an example per stage; why
> you "fix retention before scaling acquisition," the leaky bucket; how to find *your*
> activation metric the right way, worked end-to-end; how to choose a North Star; and the
> other frameworks (growth loops, HEART, the PMF survey) and when to reach for them. This
> is the *what and why* of measuring; the instrumentation chapter that follows is the *how*.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into six short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Two carry a video: read the one-line reason it's worth your time,
watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then read the short digest so the point
sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [AARRR: the five metrics that matter](./01-aarrr-five-metrics.mdx) — the five owned, measurable numbers a growth team runs on.
2. [The leaky bucket](./02-leaky-bucket.mdx) — why you plug the holes before you pour faster.
3. [Activation and the aha moment](./03-activation-aha.mdx) — find your own magic number, and prove it's causal.
