4. [The North Star Metric](./04-north-star.mdx) — the one number on top, and the inputs beneath it.
5. [Other frameworks](./05-other-frameworks.mdx) — loops, HEART, the PMF survey, and the retention curve.
6. [From framework to instrument](./06-framework-to-instrument.mdx) — each AARRR stage as a PostHog chart, then the quiz.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter.**

* **AARRR** — the five pirate metrics: acquisition, activation, retention, referral, revenue.
* **CAC** — customer-acquisition cost, what you pay to win one customer.
* **LTV** — lifetime value, the total a customer pays before they leave.
* **LTV:CAC** — the ratio of the two; the single number that says whether growth is profitable.
* **MRR / ARR** — monthly and annual recurring revenue.
* **DAU / MAU** — daily and monthly active users; their ratio is a measure of stickiness.
* **K-factor** — the viral coefficient: how many new users each existing user brings in.
* **Cohort** — a group of users tracked together over time, say everyone who signed up in the same week.
* **NSM** — North Star Metric, the one leading number that best captures the value you deliver.
* **PMF** — product-market fit; the Sean Ellis survey and a flattening retention curve are two reads on it.
* **NPS** — Net Promoter Score, a −100 to +100 gauge of how likely users are to recommend you.
* **UTM** — the campaign tags on a link that mark where a click came from.
* **RARRA** — AARRR reordered to put retention first, as a statement of priorities.

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: Ways to install PostHog
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
PostHog has an SDK for wherever your code runs. The useful way to choose is by where
the event actually happens rather than by habit — and most real products end up using
two or three of these together, so this is less "pick one" than "know what each is
for".

**The browser snippet** — paste a `<script>` into your `<head>`. No build step, works on
any site, good for a marketing page or a quick start. Its weakness is the same as its
strength: because it's copy-paste, it tends to drift — different pages get different
versions, and nobody code-reviews a snippet.

**Browser via npm** — `npm install posthog-js`. Better for a real app: types,
tree-shaking, and framework wrappers (`posthog-js/react`, Next.js helpers). This is
the natural home for your product front-end.

```js
import posthog from "posthog-js";

posthog.init("phc_YOUR_PROJECT_KEY", {
  api_host: "https://us.i.posthog.com", // use eu.i.posthog.com on EU Cloud
  person_profiles: "identified_only",   // the cost-sane default — the identify piece explains why
});
```

**Server-side** — `posthog-node`, plus official libraries for Python, Ruby, Go, PHP,
Java, and more. This is where your *important* events belong — signup, payment,
activation — and it's the half most beginner setups skip. A server SDK needs the user's
id passed explicitly, because there's no browser cookie to read it from.

**Mobile** — React Native, iOS (Swift), Android (Kotlin), and Flutter SDKs, with their
own autocapture and session-replay support. If you ship an app, the native SDK is the
better route — piping everything through a webview tends to lose events and replay
quality.

A decision table, since "which SDK" is really "which events live where":

| You're capturing… | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Marketing-site pageviews, UTM landings | Snippet or npm | Anonymous, high-volume, loss-tolerant |
| Product UI interactions, journey steps | npm (`posthog-js`) | Typed, reviewable, framework-aware |
| Signup, subscription, payment, activation | `posthog-node` (server) | Reliable, immune to ad blockers |
| Back-office facts (refunds, fulfilment) | Server SDK or reverse-ETL | They never happen in a browser |

That `phc_` key is your **project API key**. It's safe in client-side code: it can
*write* events but cannot *read* your data. (Reading data needs a separate personal API
key, which you keep secret.) This split surprises engineers regularly — shipping the
`phc_` key in your bundle is by design, not a leak.

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: Autocapture: what you get for free
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
The moment `init()` runs, `posthog-js` starts recording a lot with no further code
([autocapture docs](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/autocapture)):

* **`$pageview`** and **`$pageleave`** — every navigation, with URL, referrer, and **UTM
  parameters** (the `utm_source`/`utm_medium` tags appended to a link that record where a
  click came from).
* **`$autocapture`** — clicks, input changes, and form submissions on `a`, `button`,
  `form`, `input`, `select`, `textarea`, and `label` elements.
* **`$rageclick`** — three-plus clicks in about a second (a frustration signal),
  plus dead clicks and copy/paste events.
* **Automatic properties** on everything — browser, OS, device, viewport, referrer,
  UTMs, and ad-click IDs like `gclid`/`fbclid` (which earn their keep the moment you
  start attributing paid traffic).

Autocapture has sane privacy defaults: it records input *names* and *ids*, never input
*values*, and excludes password fields. You can suppress capture on any element by
giving it the class `ph-no-autocapture`, and narrow what's collected with an element
allowlist if volume becomes a problem.

Autocapture is your safety net — it means you're never *not* collecting data while you
decide what matters. Its limit is that it captures *interface* events ("a button was
clicked"), not *meaning* ("the user activated"). Closing that gap is what custom events
are for, and it's the difference between data and signal.

> **Trade-off worth naming:** autocapture is fast to start and great for exploration, but
> it can get noisy and, at high volume, expensive. The mature pattern is autocapture *on*
> for discovery, plus a deliberate set of named custom events for the funnels you
> actually report on. Some teams turn autocapture off in production once their custom
> events are solid; most leave it on for the replay/debugging value.

**What to take from it:**

* Autocapture roughly *doubles* your event volume — it fires about as often as pageviews,
  and adds rageclicks and web vitals on top — which is why the presenter turns it off on
  anything but a very low-volume site, and why this chapter treats it as discovery, not
  your reporting layer.
* Event names carry real weight: use the object-action shape (`cta_viewed`) and push the
  specifics — the CTA's name — into a *property*, not the event name. That's the same
  naming rule this chapter settles on when it draws up the starter kit.
* The whole payoff is one story per person: a browser event and a later backend/CRM event
  (lead → qualified → info gathered) stitch onto a single profile through a shared distinct
  id — which is exactly why `identify` and server-side capture come next.

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: Custom events: the signal you add yourself
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
Autocapture records that someone clicked *a button*. It does not know that the click
meant "user signed up" or "project created" — the events that define your funnels.
Those you capture explicitly
([capturing events docs](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/capture-events)).

In the browser:

```js
posthog.capture("user_signed_up", {
  plan: "free",
  signup_method: "email",
});
```

On the backend (with `posthog-node`), you must pass the user's id:

```js
client.capture({
  distinctId: "user_123",
  event: "subscription_started",
  properties: { plan: "pro", amount: 49 },
});
```

If you're wondering where a given event should fire, one question settles most cases:
is it money- or decision-critical? **Signup, activation, subscription, purchase — these
belong on your backend, not in the browser.** It's more reliable (no dropped requests),
and because a request fired from your server never reaches the user's browser, ad
blockers can't touch it. UI interactions and journey steps can stay client-side, where
some loss is acceptable.

A worked example of why this matters. The naive place to fire `subscription_started`
is the checkout success page. But users close the tab before the redirect lands, the
success page double-fires on refresh, and an ad blocker may eat the request entirely.
The correct source is the **payment provider's webhook** — Stripe tells your server the
charge succeeded, and your server captures the event:

```js
// In your Stripe webhook handler, after verifying the signature:
if (event.type === "checkout.session.completed") {
  const session = event.data.object;
  posthog.capture({
    distinctId: session.client_reference_id, // your user id, set at checkout
    event: "subscription_started",
    properties: {
      plan: session.metadata.plan,
      amount: session.amount_total / 100,
      interval: session.metadata.interval,
    },
  });
}
```

One webhook, one capture, exactly once, regardless of what the browser did. Every
revenue event in your product has an equivalent server-side source of truth like this —
it's worth taking a minute to find each one and firing from there.

This server-side rule is also the seam where Hogsend lives: the same backend events
that you send to PostHog can be sent to Hogsend's ingest endpoint to trigger lifecycle
journeys. One event, two consumers — PostHog measures it, Hogsend acts on it. Designing
your important events as *server-side* from the start means you get accurate analytics and
ad-blocker-proof journey triggers from the same line of code (the lifecycle chapters
turn those triggers into real journeys).

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: Identifying users (and the cost lever inside it)
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
Out of the box, events are tied to an anonymous id (a random UUID the SDK stores in the
browser). To connect a person's activity to *who they are*, call `identify` — typically
right after login ([identify docs](https://posthog.com/docs/getting-started/identify-users)):

```js
posthog.identify("user_123", {     // your stable user id (DB id or email)
  email: "sam@example.com",
  name: "Sam Rivera",
});
```

This links all the prior anonymous activity from that browser to the identified person,
so the pre-signup and post-signup history becomes one timeline. It works best called
early, with whatever properties you know at the time — each call can add more.

What's actually happening underneath is worth thirty seconds of your attention, because
identity bugs are the hardest analytics bugs to undo. Each browser starts with an
anonymous distinct id. When you `identify`, PostHog *merges* that anonymous person into
the identified one — the anonymous id becomes an alias of `user_123`, and every past
and future event on either id lands on one person. Merges are effectively permanent, so
two mistakes are expensive:

* **Identifying with an unstable id** (a session id, a device id, an email that can
  change) — you'll mint multiple "people" for one human, and their funnel steps will be
  split across them.
* **Identifying two different humans with the same id** (a shared demo account, a
  hardcoded test id that ships to production) — you'll weld strangers into one person,
  and no filter can cleanly separate them again.

**What to take from it:**

* Call `identify` on the front end right after login or signup, with a *stable, unique* id
  (email or your DB id) — a non-unique id welds different people into one profile, and the
  merge pulls all prior anonymous events onto that person.
* Pair it with `reset(true)` on sign-out so the next person on a shared device starts with
  a fresh anonymous id instead of inheriting the last user's identity.
* `alias` is the escape hatch when your front end knows a user by email but your backend
  only has their database id — aliasing keeps both ids pointed at the same person.

**Person properties** come in two flavours, and the difference matters:

* **`$set`** — overwrite every time. Use for things that change: email, plan, company.
* **`$set_once`** — write only if not already set. Use for immutable firsts: signup
  date, first referrer, acquisition channel.

```js
posthog.capture("user_signed_up", {
  $set: { plan: "free" },
  $set_once: { signup_date: "2026-06-29", initial_referrer: document.referrer },
});
```

One subtlety hides inside `$set_once`: it checks whether the key already *exists*, not
which event is newest. So with out-of-order delivery, the first event *ingested* wins —
not the earliest one that actually happened. Reach for it only on facts that are truly
write-once, like a signup date.

### The cost lever: anonymous vs identified

Anonymous events (no person profile) are roughly **4× cheaper** than identified ones.
The `person_profiles` setting controls this:

* **`identified_only`** (recommended) — events stay anonymous until you call
  `identify`. Logged-out marketing-site traffic costs the cheap rate; logged-in users
  get full profiles.
* **`always`** — every event creates a profile. More expensive; only if you genuinely
  need person data on everything.

For a typical SaaS, `identified_only` is the sane default and can cut your bill
significantly. It's the single biggest cost decision in the whole setup — the PostHog
pricing piece lays out the billing model behind it — and it's much easier to set on day
one than to untangle later.

### B2B: group analytics

If you sell to companies, you'll want to roll events up to the *account*, not just the
user, so you can ask "how many *companies* are active" and run account-level funnels:

```js
posthog.group("company", "company_456", { name: "Acme", plan: "enterprise" });
```

Two gotchas worth knowing going in: unlike `identify`, the group has to ride along with
**every** relevant event or you lose the rollup — and group analytics is often a paid
add-on with billing implications, so it's worth a look at the pricing page before
switching it on.

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: The events to track first
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
You don't need a hundred events. A small, deliberately named set is enough to build
every chart in the **AARRR funnel** (Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral,
Revenue — the five stages a growth funnel breaks into), and it's far easier to keep
clean. A good starter kit looks like this:

```
user_signed_up          # Acquisition  — fire server-side
user_logged_in          # Engagement
<your_activation_event>  # Activation   — the "aha" action, e.g. project_created
invite_sent             # Referral
subscription_started    # Revenue      — fire server-side
subscription_cancelled  # Revenue / churn
payment_succeeded       # Revenue      — fire server-side
<your_core_action>      # Retention    — the thing users do repeatedly
```

The one most teams forget is the explicit **activation event** — the "aha" action that
best predicts a user will stick, like `project_created`. Teams that skip firing it
usually end up trying to reverse-engineer it later from autocaptured button clicks, at
which point the activation funnel, retention **cohorts** (users grouped by signup week
and tracked over time), and **lifetime-value (LTV)** analysis are all built on
guesswork. Once you've settled which action is your aha moment, this is the moment to
give it a name and a capture call.

### Naming: the rules that keep this usable

PostHog recommends an `object_action` shape with snake\_case and present-tense verbs —
the same **object-action framework** Segment's data teams
[standardised years ago](https://www.twilio.com/en-us/resource-center/naming-conventions-for-clean-data)
and practitioner tools like [Avo](https://www.avo.app/docs/data-design/best-practices/naming-conventions)
enforce:

* ✅ `project_created`, `invite_sent`, `pricing_page_viewed`
* ❌ `ProjectCreated`, `user-signed-up`, `Sent Invite`

Properties follow `object_adjective`, with `is_`/`has_` for booleans and `_date`/
`_timestamp` for times: `item_price`, `is_subscribed`, `last_login_timestamp`.

The deeper point behind the pedantry —
[Amplitude calls it taxonomy](https://amplitude.com/blog/event-taxonomy),
[RudderStack calls it schema discipline](https://www.rudderstack.com/knowledge-base/data-collection-best-practices/) —
is that events are an API between the people who fire them and the people who query
them. Casing and verb-tense conventions aren't style preferences; they're what makes
`user_signed_up` findable by someone who didn't write it, six months later, in a list
of 80 event types.

If you take one rule from this section, make it this one: **event names are never built
dynamically.** It's the single mistake that most reliably ruins an analytics setup:

```js
// ❌ Don't — this creates thousands of un-queryable event types
posthog.capture(`page_viewed_${pageName}`);

// ✅ Do — one event, the variable goes in a property
posthog.capture("page_viewed", { page_name: pageName });
```

Aim for roughly **10–200 event types** total. Fewer and you can't see your funnels;
more and you've built a dictionary no one can read.

### The tracking plan: a growth team's most boring, most important document

The thing that separates a team's analytics from a mess is a **tracking plan** — a short,
shared spec of every event you fire, its properties, and where it fires. It's a Google
Sheet or a Markdown file in the repo, and it's owned, not vibes. Segment's guide to
[creating a tracking plan](https://www.twilio.com/en-us/resource-center/how-to-create-a-tracking-plan)
frames it exactly right: the plan is a *contract between teams*, not documentation
written after the fact.

| Event | When it fires | Where | Key properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| `user_signed_up` | account created | server | `plan`, `signup_method`, `$set_once: signup_date` |
| `user_logged_in` | session start | client | `login_method` |
| `project_created` | first project saved | server | `template`, `is_first` |
| `invite_sent` | invite email queued | server | `invitee_role` |
| `subscription_started` | checkout success | server (Stripe webhook) | `plan`, `amount`, `interval` |
| `subscription_cancelled` | cancellation effective | server (webhook) | `reason`, `tenure_days` |
| `payment_succeeded` | charge captured | server (webhook) | `amount`, `is_renewal` |
| `page_viewed` | route change | client | `page_name` |

Eight rows. That's a real, working tracking plan for an early-stage product — not a
40-tab spreadsheet, and emphatically not nothing.

Why it earns its keep: it stops two engineers inventing `signup` and `user_signed_up` for
the same thing; it makes "events are part of done" — the operating principle this course
opens with — enforceable in code review; and it's the reference the lifecycle journeys
and the ad-conversion pixels both read from later. Two habits make it stick:

* **The plan lives where code review happens.** A Markdown file in the repo means a new
  event and its plan row land in the same pull request, and a reviewer can reject an
  event that isn't in the plan. A spreadsheet nobody diffs will drift within a month.
* **Every row has the same four columns** — event, trigger, source, properties. If you
  can't fill in "where it fires," you haven't decided yet, and that ambiguity is
  exactly what the plan exists to catch.

Tools exist for this at scale (Avo, Segment Protocols, RudderStack's tracking plans),
but a single shared doc beats nothing by a mile — and it's a perfectly good place to
stay until the doc itself starts hurting.

**What to take from it:**

* Write the plan *before* you instrument: a few hours deciding what matters catches the
  missing event or property you'd otherwise discover months later while building a report
  — and the method is tool-agnostic, so it transfers straight to PostHog.
* Case-sensitivity is the silent killer: `Signup` and `signup` become two different events,
  so force one convention (lowercase) across every event and property name.
* Prefer fewer events with more properties — collapse `upload_youtube`/`upload_vimeo`/
  `upload_mp4` into one `upload_video` with a `video_type` property, and reuse property
  names across events so the data stays readable.

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: Other ways to get events in
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
Capturing from your own code is the default and the cheapest. But it's worth knowing the
alternatives so you choose deliberately:

* **A CDP (customer data platform) pipe — Segment or RudderStack.** A CDP captures your
  events once and fans the same stream out to PostHog *and* your warehouse *and* your ad
  tools. **Worth it when** you have many destinations and want one canonical schema;
  **overkill when** PostHog is your only destination — then you're adding a hop and a
  bill for nothing.
* **Google Tag Manager.** Marketers love it because it adds tags without a deploy. The
  catch for a product team: GTM-injected tags are *more* likely to be ad-blocked (they
  load from Google's domains), and it pulls your event logic out of the codebase where it
  can be code-reviewed. **Fine for marketing-site pixels; not where your product funnel
  should live.**
* **Reverse-ETL / warehouse → PostHog.** If your source of truth is a database (orders,
  subscriptions), you can sync those rows in as events rather than instrumenting the app.
  **Good for back-office facts** (refunds, fulfilment) that never happen in a browser.
* **PostHog managed Sources.** PostHog's own CDP can pull from Stripe, Hubspot, and
  others directly — useful for joining revenue to behaviour without writing the pipe.

For most teams this course is written for, the deliberate choice comes out the same
way: **your own front-end and back-end do the capturing, the CDP waits until you have a
second destination, and GTM stays on the marketing site.**

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: Beating ad blockers
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
Ad blockers quietly delete a slice of your data: **10–30% of your client-side events can
simply vanish**, because blockers maintain blocklists of analytics domains and drop any
browser request to `*.posthog.com`. Your charts under-count, and you don't know by how
much. Worse, the loss is *biased* — technical audiences block more, so a dev tool's
charts under-count precisely the users it cares about most.

Two fixes, used together:

### Reverse proxy (recovers client-side events)

Route events through a subdomain of *your own* domain (e.g. `e.yourdomain.com`), which
blockers haven't catalogued. On PostHog Cloud this is a **free, managed** feature:

1. In **Organization settings**, create a managed reverse proxy.
2. Choose a neutral subdomain — *avoid* obvious words like "analytics" or "tracking."
3. Add the CNAME record it gives you; wait a few minutes for SSL.
4. Point your SDK's `api_host` at your subdomain.

This typically recovers 10–30% of otherwise-blocked events. (Note: the managed proxy
runs on Cloudflare and is not compliant with **HIPAA**, the US health-data privacy law —
so don't route **PHI**, protected health information like patient records, through it.
If you self-host the proxy, prefer a Cloudflare Worker over PostHog's managed CNAME,
which can be "uncloaked" by blocklists over time.)

### Server-side capture (immune by design)

A reverse proxy helps the browser. But the truly robust answer for your *important*
events is to fire them from the **backend** with `posthog-node`. Those events go
server → PostHog and never touch the user's browser, so **ad blockers cannot block
them at all.** It's the same reason money-critical events — signup, subscription,
purchase — belong on the backend in the first place.

There's a sharper way to hold this distinction. **The events that drive your lifecycle
journeys are server-side, and therefore ad-blocker-proof.** Your client-side product
*analytics* still take the ~10–30% hit, and only a proxy recovers a slice of it — but
the events that *trigger your emails and Discord messages* fire from your server
regardless of what's running in the browser. Keep the split precise: a proxy mitigates
the *analytics*, server-side capture makes the *triggers* unblockable.

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: Actions, and why clean events unlock experiments
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
## Events vs Actions

Actions are PostHog's escape hatch for a taxonomy that turns out slightly wrong, and
knowing they exist saves you re-instrumenting later. Two definitions set up the
difference:

* **Events** are the raw records you capture in code.
* **Actions** are groupings you define *inside PostHog* — combine several events that
  mean the same thing ("all the CTAs that reach /pricing"), or rename a messy
  autocaptured event into something friendly.

The useful part: **actions apply retroactively**, to events you already captured. So if
you realise three different buttons all represent "started checkout," you can unify them
in the UI without shipping code or losing history. It's the escape hatch for the
inevitable day your taxonomy turns out slightly wrong — which is why a *good* taxonomy
matters but a *perfect* one isn't worth blocking on.

## Clean events are the price of admission to experiments

There's a payoff to all this discipline that most teams only discover later:
**everything you just instrumented is also your A/B testing infrastructure.**
PostHog's [experiments](https://posthog.com/docs/experiments) measure a variant against
a goal metric — and the goal metric is one of your events. If `project_created` fires
reliably and means one thing, you can test onboarding changes against it this
afternoon. If it's three differently-named events fired from two places, every
experiment you run inherits that mess, and your "results" measure your instrumentation
as much as your product.

The dependency runs in one direction: **instrument first, test second.** Teams that
adopt an experimentation tool before their events are clean end up debugging data
instead of learning — which is exactly why the dedicated experiments chapter waits until
your event kit is trustworthy. The maturity ladder looks like: no testing → informal
before/after comparisons ("we shipped it and signups went up") → regular A/B tests →
tests with proper **holdouts** (a slice of users deliberately kept out of the test to
serve as a clean baseline) and written-up results. Before/after comparisons are the rung
most teams get stuck on — they feel like measurement but can't separate your change from
the newsletter that went out the same day.

Wherever you are on that ladder, the path up runs through the same place: this
chapter's event kit firing cleanly. Once it does, every experiment you run later becomes
cheap.

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: Keep it clean
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
Clean instrumentation is a maintenance discipline, not a one-time install. An event
taxonomy rots the way a codebase rots — quietly, one shortcut at a time — until the
funnels start to lie and nobody quite trusts the dashboard. The good news is that the
habits which keep it honest are cheap, and they cost far less than the clean-up they
head off:

* **Filter out internal/test users** — by email domain, IP, or a test flag — so your
  own activity doesn't pollute the funnels. (Founders refreshing their own app is the
  number-one source of fake activation.)
* **Don't leak PII (personally identifiable information — emails, full names, and the
  like) into event or property *names*** — values are fine; names are not.
* **Version events on breaking changes** (`registration_v2`) rather than silently
  changing a name's meaning.
* **Keep the website and app in one project** so you see the full journey from first
  anonymous visit to paying customer.
* **QA instrumentation like code.** After shipping an event, it's worth watching it
  arrive in PostHog's activity view, checking its properties, and watching one session
  replay of the flow to confirm it fires when you think it does. The trade is ten
  minutes now against a quarter's worth of subtly wrong funnels later.

Those habits are universal, but the emphasis shifts with what you're building — the same
discipline just bites in a different place first.

### For your kind of startup

* **Mobile-first** — use the native SDK; note that session replay and autocapture behave
  differently than on web, and store-purchase events come from the platform, not a
  browser.
* **B2B** — turn on groups (mind the billing), and track the *account*-level activation,
  not just the user's.
* **E-commerce** — fire `order_completed` server-side from the payment webhook (the
  browser tab often closes before a client event lands), and capture `product_*`
  properties for merchandising analysis.
* **Dev tool** — instrument the docs and the first successful API call; the docs-to-first-
  call path is your real activation funnel.

The rules don't change from startup to startup; only which one you'll wish you'd kept
does.

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
Six things worth having in place before the next chapter — roughly an afternoon of
work, and everything later builds on them:

1. `posthog-js` installed with `person_profiles: "identified_only"` — the cost default
   that's painful to change later.
2. `posthog.identify()` at login and `posthog.reset()` at logout — the pair that keeps
   identities whole and unmixed.
3. The starter events, with **signup, subscription, and payment firing from your
   backend** — webhook sources of truth, so the numbers stay honest.
4. A **tracking plan**, even if it's ten rows in a Markdown file in the repo — the doc
   that stops two engineers inventing two names for one thing.
5. The managed reverse proxy — a few minutes of DNS that recovers the blocked slice of
   your client-side events.
6. Your one **activation event** firing, QA'd against a session replay — the event the
   whole next chapter's funnel hangs off.

With data flowing, the next chapter is the daily habit that turns all this
instrumentation into decisions.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [PostHog — capturing events](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/capture-events) — the canonical reference for custom events, client and server.
* [PostHog — identify users](https://posthog.com/docs/getting-started/identify-users) — distinct ids, merging, and the identify lifecycle in full.
* [PostHog — autocapture](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/autocapture) — exactly what's collected automatically and how to tune it.
* [Segment — naming conventions for clean data](https://www.twilio.com/en-us/resource-center/naming-conventions-for-clean-data) — the object-action framework this chapter's rules come from.
* [Segment — how to create a tracking plan](https://www.twilio.com/en-us/resource-center/how-to-create-a-tracking-plan) — the plan as a cross-team contract, with templates.
* [Avo — naming conventions](https://www.avo.app/docs/data-design/best-practices/naming-conventions) — practitioner-grade event/property naming, opinionated in the right places.
* [Amplitude — great analytics needs a great taxonomy](https://amplitude.com/blog/event-taxonomy) — why taxonomy quality decides analytics quality, vendor-neutral.
* [RudderStack — behavioral data collection best practices](https://www.rudderstack.com/knowledge-base/data-collection-best-practices/) — the engineering-side rules (schemas, naming, QA) before you instrument.

growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.title: 3 — Instrument PostHog from zero
growth-with-posthog/03-instrument-postho.text:
> **In this chapter:** the snippet vs npm vs server/mobile SDKs, what autocapture gives
> you free, how to identify users and set properties, the small set of events to track
> first, the tracking plan that keeps a team honest, the other ways to pipe events in
> (Segment, GTM, reverse-ETL), events vs actions, why clean events are the price of
> admission to A/B testing, and the two techniques that beat ad blockers. Code included.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into ten short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Where a piece has a video, the idea is the same each time: read the
one-line reason it's worth your time, watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then
read the short digest so the point sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you
need:

1. [Ways to install](./01-ways-to-install.mdx) — pick the SDK by where the event actually happens.
2. [Autocapture](./02-autocapture.mdx) — what you get free the moment `init()` runs, and where it stops.
3. [Custom events](./03-custom-events.mdx) — the signal you add yourself, and why money events fire server-side.
4. [Identifying users](./04-identify-users.mdx) — connect activity to a person, and the biggest cost lever in the setup.
5. [The events to track first](./05-events-to-track-first.mdx) — a small named kit and the tracking plan that keeps a team honest.
6. [Other ways to get events in](./06-other-ways-in.mdx) — CDPs, GTM, reverse-ETL, and when each earns its keep.
7. [Beating ad blockers](./07-beat-ad-blockers.mdx) — recover the blocked slice, and keep your triggers unblockable.
8. [Actions and experiments](./08-actions-and-experiments.mdx) — the retroactive escape hatch, and clean events as test infrastructure.
9. [Keep it clean](./09-keep-it-clean.mdx) — the habits that prevent the most common messes.
10. [Before you move on](./10-before-you-move-on.mdx) — the afternoon's checklist, then a quiz to check yourself.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter:**

* **AARRR** — the five stages a growth funnel breaks into: Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue.
* **Cohort** — a group of users bucketed by a shared trait, usually signup week, and tracked over time.
* **LTV (lifetime value)** — the total revenue one customer brings over the whole relationship, not just the first payment.
* **UTM parameters** — the `utm_source`/`utm_campaign` tags appended to a link to record where a click came from.
* **CDP (customer data platform)** — a pipe like Segment or RudderStack that captures events once and fans them out to many tools.
* **Holdout** — a slice of users deliberately kept out of a test to serve as a clean, untouched baseline.
* **PII (personally identifiable information)** — data that names a real person: emails, full names, and the like.
* **PHI / HIPAA** — protected health information, governed by HIPAA, the US health-data privacy law.

growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.title: The insight types, and how to read them
growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.text:
PostHog has seven ways to look at your events, and you'll lean on four of them. All
seven are still worth knowing, because picking the wrong insight type is the most
common reason a founder ends up staring at a chart that can't answer their question.

* **Trends** — a metric over time (counts, unique users, formulas), with breakdowns by
  any property. The workhorse. *Read it for direction and breakdown:* "signups per day,
  by channel — which channel is growing?" *Its failure mode:* counting events when you
  mean people. Ten pageviews from one user and one pageview from ten users look identical
  until you switch the series to unique users.
* **Funnels** — step-by-step conversion
  ([docs](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/funnels)). Shows the drop-off at
  each step and the time to convert. *Read it for the worst step:* the biggest drop is
  your biggest leak. Click a step to see — or replay — the users who fell out. Two
  settings people miss: the **conversion window** (a user who converts on day 15 of a
  7-day window counts as dropped) and **exclusion steps** (remove users who, say, hit an
  error page between steps). *Its failure mode:* steps in an order users don't actually
  follow — check User Paths first if conversion looks impossibly low.
* **Retention** — a cohort grid ([docs](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/retention)):
  pick a start event ("signed up") and a return event ("did the core action"). Each row
  is a cohort (the users who started in one period), each column a later period. *Read it
  for the shape* (the retention piece walks through it) — this is the leaky-bucket test
  made visual.
* **Lifecycle** — splits your active users each period into **New**, **Returning**,
  **Resurrecting**, and **Dormant**. *Read it for whether growth is real:* a big Dormant
  band under modest New growth means churn-and-replace, not growth.
* **Stickiness** — *how many times* users did something in a period (depth), as opposed
  to retention's *whether they came back*. *Read it to find your power users* and what
  separates them: if users who fire your core action 5+ days a week all created three
  or more projects in week one, "three projects" is a candidate activation bar worth
  testing — the way the activation chapter defines one.
* **User Paths** — a flow diagram of how people move through the product. *Read it to
  discover* unexpected routes and dead ends you didn't think to build a funnel for —
  the classic find is a settings or pricing page appearing in the middle of what you
  assumed was a straight onboarding line.
* **SQL** — write SQL (HogQL) directly when the UI can't express your question.
  PostHog's AI assistant (Max) can write these for you, which makes SQL approachable even
  if you don't know HogQL.

The four you'll read daily are **Trends, Funnels, Retention, and Lifecycle.**

**What to take from it:**

* A funnel is just ordered steps; rename each step so the chart reads clearly, then break
  it down by a property (channel, continent) to see which segment actually converts.
* Two levers change what a funnel *means*: strict vs sequential step order (strict counts
  only direct step-to-step, and conversion usually drops), and exclusion steps that remove
  users who took a detour — say, hit the support page — between steps.
* Filtering to one traffic source shows fewer users but often a higher conversion — segment
  before you judge the number.

growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.title: The daily checklist
growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.text:
You don't need twenty charts — what works is a short, fixed list read the same way
every morning. Here's a good one, with what each chart means *and what it's telling you
to do when it looks wrong*, because a chart nobody acts on is just decoration: the
**dashboard graveyard**, a board full of true numbers that nobody ever decides anything
from.

Most founders read reactively — they open the dashboard after a bad week, once the
damage has already landed. A fixed daily read turns that around: a problem shows up on
the morning it starts, not the week it hurts. So before the list, one honest question
about where you're reading from today.

Whatever your honest answer, the aim is the same: a read so fixed and so fast that doing
it daily costs less than the anxiety of not knowing. Built as a dashboard with alerts,
that read is a two-minute glance.

### 1. Active users (Trends — unique users on your core action)

**The pulse.** If this is flat or falling week-over-week, nothing else matters yet.
The *trend* is what to watch, not the absolute number — early on, direction is the
signal. "Active" works best defined as your **core action** rather than "opened the
app": a user who logs in and does nothing isn't active in any sense that pays you.
*If it's falling:* check the lifecycle chart (#6) — is it fewer New, or more Dormant?

### 2. New signups (Trends — count of `user_signed_up`, server-side)

**Is acquisition working?** A useful number, but a vanity one on its own: a signup
spike with no matching activation means you attracted the wrong people or overpromised.
It reads best next to chart 3, with the **channel breakdown** on — a flat total can
hide one channel doubling while another collapses.
*If it spikes but activation doesn't:* you bought the wrong traffic — check the channel
breakdown.

### 3. Activation funnel (Funnel — `signup → your aha event`)

**The most actionable chart you have.** Your **aha event** is the first action where a
new user actually feels the product's value — the first message sent, the first file
synced, the first project shared — and this funnel counts how many signups reach it. The
drop-off between steps shows you *exactly* where onboarding leaks. Click the worst step
and pull the list of users who fell out — or jump straight to their session replays
(chart 7) to see why. A useful discipline: know your funnel's *baseline* by heart (say,
"62% reach step 2, 31% activate") so a change registers as a change and not a guess.
*If the drop is at one step:* that step is your week's work.

### 4. Signup conversion rate (Web Analytics goal, or a funnel)

**Is the top of the funnel smooth?** Visitors → signup. A low number here points at
friction on the landing page or in the signup flow itself.
*If it's low:* watch replays of the landing page; it's usually copy or a form, not the
product.

### 5. Weekly retention curve (Retention — start: signup; return: core action)

**The single best product-market-fit signal.** Read the *shape* (the full how-to is the
retention piece): a curve that decays to zero is a leaky bucket; one that flattens above
zero is a product with real pull.

### 6. Lifecycle (Lifecycle insight on your core action)

**Is your growth real?** A healthy chart shows New and Returning growing. A big red
**Dormant** band underneath modest New growth means you're refilling a leaking bucket —
the same users churning and being replaced. This is the leaky bucket, drawn for you.
*If Dormant dominates:* acquisition can wait — this is a retention problem, not an
acquisition one.

### 7. Session replays of drop-offs and errors

**The "why" behind the numbers.** Filter replays to users who hit a funnel drop-off, a
rage-click, or an error, and watch a few. Five minutes of watching real people struggle
will teach you more than an hour of staring at a chart. The workflow that keeps this
cheap is arriving from a chart (a funnel step, an error, a rage-click filter) rather
than browsing cold — that way every replay you watch is pre-selected to show you a
problem ([replay docs](https://posthog.com/docs/session-replay)).
*What you're looking for:* the moment of hesitation. Cursor circling, tab-switching,
a form field cleared and retyped — the qualitative signature of the leak your funnel
found quantitatively.

### 8. Errors / exceptions (Error Tracking)

**Production health.** New or spiking issues. Best piped to Slack (see the alerts piece)
so you don't have to remember to look. *A spike in errors often explains a dip in
activation — check this before blaming your onboarding copy.*

### 9. Paid conversion (free → paid %)

**The strongest value signal there is.** People paying is the hardest proof that the
problem you solve is worth solving. Track the conversion rate, not just the count.

That's the list. The first time, it'll take you twenty minutes. Once it's a dashboard,
it's a two-minute glance.

### What a real read looks like

To make the ritual concrete, here's a Monday morning read of the nine charts, as it
actually goes for a small SaaS:

*Active users: flat, fine. Signups: up 18% — nice, but the breakdown says it's all one
Reddit thread, so don't celebrate a trend. Activation funnel: baseline is 31%, today
it reads 24% — that's outside normal bounce. Worst step is still "create first
project," but the drop moved to the step before it: the workspace-naming screen.
Errors: a new exception, `WorkspaceNameValidation`, started Friday. There's the story.
Replays: three watched, all three show users typing a workspace name with a space,
getting a red field with no message, and leaving. Lifecycle and retention: unchanged,
they move on week timescales. Paid conversion: unchanged.*

One decision comes out of it — fix the validation message today, then confirm tomorrow
that the funnel step recovers to baseline. Total time: eight minutes, because the
charts were pre-built and the errors alert had already flagged Friday's exception.

Notice what the read did *not* involve: no exploring, no new charts, no debate about
what "active" means. The nine tiles answered "where's the leak?" and the replays
answered "why?" — and every future morning starts from the same fixed list, so a
deviation registers instantly. That's the entire skill. It's pattern-matching against
a baseline you know by heart, and it only works because the list doesn't change.

**What to take from it:**

* Errors are worth enriching at capture time: attaching context properties (environment,
  inputs, the model being processed) is what later lets you filter the dashboard down to
  just your team's production issues and auto-route them.
* The debugging path runs anomaly → frequent issue → stack trace → the exact file and line
  → the custom properties that reveal the root cause — the same chart-to-cause move item 7
  describes, applied to exceptions.
* The fix is usually upstream of where it throws: here a bad input (a persons-model export
  hitting an events-only path) points to adding a guard at the API and front-end layer, not
  patching the crash site.

growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.title: How to read a retention curve
growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.text:
Most founders glance at retention and move on, which is a shame — read properly, this
one chart answers your single most important strategic question: *am I in the "fix the
bucket" or "open the tap" phase?* Three things worth looking at:

* **The shape, not the height.** Three archetypes: a curve that **slides to zero** (no
  **product-market fit (PMF)** — the product never finds a group it holds, so every cohort
  eventually leaves), a curve that **declines then flattens** (the "smile" or plateau —
  some cohort sticks forever, the sign of real product-market fit), and the rare
  **smiling curve that ticks back up** (resurrection exceeds churn — usually a
  network-effect or content product). You want, at minimum, a flattening above zero.
* **Where it flattens.** A curve flattening at 40% means four in ten users become
  permanent; flattening at 5% means you have a trickle. The flattening *level* is roughly
  your ceiling without intervention — and lifecycle messaging (automated nudges that bring
  users back before they drift) is how you lift it, which is what the lifecycle chapters
  are for.
* **Cohort over cohort.** Are *newer* cohorts retaining better than older ones? That's
  the real signal your product changes are working — each week's improvements should show
  up as a higher curve for the cohorts that joined after them. PostHog's cohort grid lets
  you compare rows directly.

Three technicalities that change what the same grid means — worth knowing before you
panic or celebrate ([Amplitude's retention guide](https://amplitude.com/blog/retention-analysis)
covers all three in depth):

* **N-day vs unbounded retention.** N-day asks "did they return *on* day 7?";
  unbounded asks "did they return on day 7 *or any day after*?" N-day is stricter and
  lower; unbounded suits products used sporadically. It's worth checking which your
  grid shows before comparing anything to anything.
* **The right-hand columns are younger.** In a weekly grid, the newest cohort has only
  had one week to prove itself — the bottom-right of the grid is always sparse and
  noisy. Read the *oldest few cohorts* for shape, the newest few for early trend.
* **Small cohorts lie.** A 12-user cohort swinging from 40% to 25% is three people —
  not something to re-plan a roadmap on. The trend across several cohorts is the
  signal worth waiting for.

A practical benchmark, held loosely: for many B2B SaaS products a flattening D30 in the
*tens of percent* is healthy; consumer social needs higher; e-commerce thinks in repeat-
purchase rate instead. [Lenny Rachitsky's benchmark survey of growth
leaders](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/what-is-good-retention-issue-29) puts real
numbers on "good" and "great" by category if you want to calibrate — but your own
cohort-over-cohort trend is a better guide than anyone's benchmark, and retention is
still [the single best PMF signal you
have](https://posthog.com/founders/measure-product-market-fit).

growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.title: Build it as one dashboard
growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.text:
There's no reason to navigate to nine insights every day. Each one can be saved, added
to a single **dashboard**, and that dashboard **pinned to your project homepage** so
it's the first thing you see when you log in
([dashboards docs](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/dashboards)). A few
things that make the board work harder:

* Your **North Star metric** — the single number that best captures the value your
  product delivers (nights booked, messages sent — the one metric you'd optimise if you
  could pick only one) — belongs in the top tile, with its input metrics beneath it. The
  layout itself should say what matters most
  ([PostHog's founder guide to North Star metrics](https://posthog.com/founders/north-star-metrics)
  makes the case that the metric on top shapes what the team optimises).
* Use dashboard-level date and property filters so you can switch the whole board to
  "last 7 days" or "paid users only" at once.
* PostHog ships dashboard templates (web metrics, product health) you can start from
  instead of building blank.
* Every non-obvious tile earns a one-line description ("D7 retention, weekly cohorts,
  core action = project\_created"). The tile that needs a tribal elder to interpret is
  the tile that stops being read.

This dashboard is a growth team's shared artifact. When everyone opens the same board and
reads the same nine charts the same way, "how are we doing" stops being a meeting and
becomes a glance. That's the product-led operating model this course is built on, made
concrete — and it's exactly the board a weekly growth review opens with (the
operating-cadence chapter walks through that review in full).

growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.title: Stop logging in: alerts and subscriptions
growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.text:
The goal is not to *remember* to check these. The goal is for the important ones to
come to you, and for you to only open PostHog when something's worth investigating.

* **Alerts** fire when an insight crosses a threshold or trips anomaly detection, and
  notify you in-app, by email, on Slack, or via webhook. One on each critical chart
  covers it: "signups dropped below X today," "errors spiked." You choose how often
  each checks (hourly/daily/weekly).
* **Subscriptions** send a scheduled snapshot of an insight or dashboard to email or
  Slack on a cadence. A weekly snapshot of your North Star dashboard, delivered Monday
  morning, keeps the team aligned with zero effort.

Tuning matters more than setting. An alert that fires daily is furniture within a
week — nobody clicks it, and then nobody clicks the one that mattered. Two rules keep
alerts honest: **every alert that fires gets acted on or re-thresholded** (no third
option), and **prefer few loud alerts to many quiet ones** — three alerts someone
investigates beat fifteen that scroll by. If you're deleting an alert because it never
mattered, that's the system working.

Set the alerts once and your daily ritual shrinks to: read the Slack alerts, and open
the dashboard only when one fires. That two-minute daily glance is the first rung of a
full operating cadence — daily notice, weekly review, monthly re-examination — that the
operating-cadence chapter lays out in full.

There's a deeper seam here, and it's exactly the one Hogsend extends. A PostHog alert
tells *you* something changed; a Hogsend **journey** — an automated sequence that
messages the user when an event fires — tells *the user* something instead, without you
in the loop. Notice for you, action for them: turning that second loop on is what the
lifecycle chapters are about.

growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.title: Other ways teams stay on top of the numbers
growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.text:
The pinned dashboard plus alerts is the default, and for most small teams it's the whole
system. A few other setups earn their place once you've outgrown it, or when a particular
question doesn't fit a permanent tile:

* **The weekly metrics email/Slack post.** A human (or a subscription) posts the five
  headline numbers with one line of commentary every Monday. Lower-tech than a dashboard,
  higher-signal for a team — the commentary forces interpretation, not just observation.
* **Warehouse BI (Metabase, Looker, Lightdash).** If you've outgrown PostHog's insights
  or need to join heavy financial data, a BI tool on your warehouse is more flexible.
  It's also more setup and another tool to maintain — reach for it when PostHog genuinely
  can't express the question, not before.
* **PostHog's home + "Max" AI.** You can ask Max a plain-language question ("what's our
  D7 retention this month vs last?") instead of building an insight. Good for ad-hoc
  questions that don't deserve a permanent tile.

It's worth seeing what this looks like at the small end — one founder, real numbers, a
process that fits in a morning coffee:

**What to take from it:**

* Track as little as possible early: copying big-company dashboards at five users just buys
  information paralysis. One metric — week-one retention — comes first, and until it's
  healthy nothing else matters.
* Analytics tells you *what's* broken, not *why* — the retention jump from under 1% to 19%
  came from talking to users (they needed Outlook and Apple Calendar support), not from a
  chart.
* Once retention is decent, per-feature adoption numbers (only 3% create lists, 17% link a
  calendar) become the prioritisation tool — surprisingly low usage usually signals a
  discoverability problem, not a demand one.

The anti-pattern to avoid: **dashboard sprawl.** A team with forty dashboards has none —
nobody knows which is canonical. One pinned board everyone reads beats a wall of charts
nobody trusts.

### For your kind of startup

* **Marketplace** — your daily pulse is *liquidity*: track both sides (supply added,
  demand served) and the match/transaction rate between them. A healthy one side and a
  starved other is the failure mode.
* **Consumer app** — **DAU/MAU** (daily active users over monthly active users — the
  share of your monthly users who show up on a given day) and the retention curve are the
  whole game; paid conversion may not even apply.
* **E-commerce** — repeat-purchase rate and time-between-orders replace "retention curve";
  the activation chart becomes first → second purchase.
* **Dev tool** — the activation funnel (docs → first successful call) is your #1 chart;
  signups without a first call are noise.

growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.title: A note on reading numbers honestly
growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.text:
A dashboard can't lie to you, but you can still misread it — and the misreadings are
quiet, because every number on the screen is technically true. The habit is only as
honest as the way you read it, and three failure modes corrode that reading as it forms:

* **Watching vanity metrics.** Signups, pageviews, and registered-user counts feel
  good and move easily — [Amplitude's definition](https://amplitude.com/blog/vanity-metrics)
  is blunt: a vanity metric is one that can go up while the business goes nowhere.
  The metrics that reflect *value delivered* — activation, retention, paid
  conversion — are the anchor; the vanity numbers are context around them.
* **Reacting to noise.** Day-to-day numbers bounce. Look at trends over weeks before
  concluding something changed. An alert is for "investigate," not "panic." A single bad
  day is weather; a two-week trend is climate.
* **Reading for confirmation.** The subtle one. If you only dig into charts that look
  wrong when you expected them to look wrong, the dashboard becomes a mirror. The
  discipline is the fixed list read the same way every time (the daily checklist) — it
  forces you past the charts you'd rather skip. The same honesty is what a rigorous
  experimentation program later leans on: a team that reads charts for confirmation will
  run experiments for confirmation too.

The through-line is the same in all three: a fixed read, done the same way whether the
week was good or bad, is what keeps the dashboard a mirror you can trust rather than one
that only flatters.

growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.text:
Six things worth doing while this chapter is fresh — together they turn the events
you've instrumented into a habit that costs two minutes a day:

1. Create the nine insights from the daily checklist — the fixed list is what makes
   deviations register.
2. Put them on one dashboard, pinned to your homepage, North Star (your single most
   important metric) on top — so the right read is also the path of least resistance.
3. Add alerts on signups, activation, and errors → Slack — few and loud, so a fired
   alert always means something.
4. Schedule a weekly subscription of the dashboard to yourself/your team — alignment
   with zero effort.
5. Read your **retention curve** with the retention piece in hand, and see if you can say
   out loud which phase you're in — fix the bucket, or open the tap. That one sentence is
   the one that decides what you work on next.
6. Watch three session replays of your worst funnel step's drop-outs, arriving from
   the chart rather than browsing cold — the cheapest "why" you'll ever collect.

You can now *see* your funnel and your leaks. The rest of the course is about acting on
them — first by keeping the users you have, then by going to get more.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [PostHog — funnels](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/funnels) — conversion windows, exclusions, and drop-off analysis in full.
* [PostHog — retention](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/retention) — building and reading the cohort grid.
* [PostHog — dashboards](https://posthog.com/docs/product-analytics/dashboards) — assembling, filtering, and pinning the daily board.
* [PostHog — session replay](https://posthog.com/docs/session-replay) — setup and the filtering that makes replays diagnostic.
* [PostHog — finding your North Star metric](https://posthog.com/founders/north-star-metrics) — why the top tile of the dashboard shapes what the team optimises.
* [Amplitude — funnel analysis: a complete guide](https://amplitude.com/blog/funnel-analysis) — vendor-neutral funnel theory when you want the general case.
* [Amplitude — retention analysis guide](https://amplitude.com/blog/retention-analysis) — n-day vs unbounded, cohort mechanics, and the maths behind the retention piece.
* [Lenny Rachitsky — what is good retention?](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/what-is-good-retention-issue-29) — the benchmark survey: "good" and "great" retention by product category.
* [Lenny's Newsletter — the most important bottom-up SaaS metrics](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-most-important-bottom-up-saas-69d) — which of the many numbers deserve a tile at all.

growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.title: 4 — What to look at every day
growth-with-posthog/04-your-daily-dashbo.text:
> **In this chapter:** the PostHog insight types you'll actually use and how to read each,
> a concrete daily checklist with the meaning behind every chart (and what to do when one
> goes bad), how to read a retention curve like a growth team, the replay workflow that
> explains your drop-offs, how to automate the whole thing with alerts, and the other ways
> teams stay on top of the numbers.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into eight short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Where a piece has a video, the idea is the same each time: read the
one-line reason it's worth your time, watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then
read the short digest so the point sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you
need:

1. [The insight types, and how to read them](./01-insight-types.mdx) — the four charts you'll read daily, and the failure modes of each.
2. [The daily checklist](./02-daily-checklist.mdx) — the fixed nine-chart morning read, and a worked example.
3. [How to read a retention curve](./03-retention-curve.mdx) — shape, not height: fix the bucket or open the tap.
4. [Build it as one dashboard](./04-one-dashboard.mdx) — pin the nine charts so the right read is the easy one.
5. [Stop logging in: alerts and subscriptions](./05-alerts.mdx) — make the important charts come to you.
6. [Other ways teams stay on top](./06-staying-on-top.mdx) — the weekly post, warehouse BI, Max AI, and your kind of startup.
7. [A note on reading numbers honestly](./07-reading-honestly.mdx) — vanity metrics, noise, and reading for confirmation.
8. [Before you move on](./08-before-you-move-on.mdx) — the six setup steps that make it a habit, then a quiz.

## Words you'll meet in this chapter

* **North Star metric** — the single number that best captures the value your product delivers to users (nights booked, messages sent) — the one you'd optimise if you could pick only one.
* **Aha event** — the first action where a new user actually feels the product's value; the moment onboarding exists to reach.
* **Product-market fit (PMF)** — the point where a product has real, durable pull: some group of users keeps coming back instead of drifting away.
* **DAU/MAU** — daily active users divided by monthly active users, the share of a month's users who show up on a given day; a standard stickiness ratio.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Lifecycle messaging plugs the bucket
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
Fixing retention is not something you do in product code alone. A large part of it is
*reaching the user at the right moment* and pulling them back toward value — and that is
lifecycle messaging: the right message, triggered by the right behaviour, at the right
time. It's the operational layer that plugs a leaky bucket, because each hole in the
bucket has a message shaped to fit it.

* A user signs up but never reaches value → an **onboarding** sequence pulls them to
  *activation* (the first time the product's core value clicks — its **aha moment**).
* An active user goes quiet → a **win-back** message brings them back before they're
  gone for good.
* A card fails → a **dunning** sequence (the retries and reminder emails that recover a
  failed subscription payment) wins back revenue you already earned.

Lifecycle messaging isn't "marketing email." It's the operational layer that moves
people between the stages of the **AARRR funnel** — Acquisition, Activation, Retention,
Referral, Revenue, the five stages a user passes through — and it's the cheapest
retention lever you have, because the users are already yours. Measurement tells a team
where the bucket is leaking; lifecycle messaging is how the team acts on that leak
automatically, every time, without anyone having to remember. The measurement work
earlier in the course is the *seeing*; this chapter is the *acting*.

The framing that changes how you design every message is this: lifecycle messaging is a
*service* to the user, not a broadcast at them. Every good lifecycle message answers a
question the user has at that moment — "what do I do first?", "why did my payment fail?",
"what changed since I left?" A message with no answer behind it is noise, and users bill
you for noise in unsubscribes and spam complaints (the deliverability piece later shows
how expensive that bill gets). The whole discipline of this chapter — triggers, cadence,
exit conditions, holdouts — exists to keep the program on the right side of one line:
every message either answers a question or becomes one.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: The stages, mapped to AARRR
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
Every lifecycle program is built from the same short list of stages, each one tied to a
point in the **AARRR funnel** — Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue,
the five stages a user moves through. Here's each stage: when it fires, and which part
of the funnel it serves.

| Program | AARRR stage | Fires when | Typical trigger event |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Welcome** | Acquisition → Activation | Right after signup | `user_signed_up` |
| **Onboarding / activation** | Activation | New user hasn't hit the aha yet | signup + "not yet activated" |
| **Engagement / feature adoption** | Retention | A valuable feature goes unused | `feature_x_never_used` after N days |
| **Retention / habit** | Retention | On the product's natural usage rhythm | usage cadence, streaks, digests |
| **Resurrection / win-back** | Retention | A user goes dormant | inactivity 30/60/90 days |
| **Referral** | Referral | At peak satisfaction | activation milestone hit |
| **Expansion / upsell** | Revenue | Approaching a plan limit / power usage | `approaching_limit` |
| **Dunning / failed payment** | Revenue | A charge fails | `payment_failed` |

A useful mental model (from Reforge): **retention is an output, not a stage you push
on directly.** Its inputs are *activation*, *engagement*, and *resurrection* — and
lifecycle messaging is how you move each of those inputs.
[Customer.io's lifecycle guide](https://customer.io/learn/lifecycle-marketing/automation-and-personalization-guide)
frames the same program as a five-step arc — capture, activate, retain, win back,
expand — which is a decent sanity check that your table above covers the whole
relationship, not just the first week of it.

The best programs reinforce the product's *existing* habit loop rather than inventing
new reasons to log in. A fitness app's habit is the workout; the message that says
"your Tuesday session is waiting" works because it strengthens a loop that already
exists. A message that invents a reason ("check out our blog!") fights the user's
attention instead of riding it. Before writing any sequence, it's worth naming the
habit loop you're reinforcing — teams that can't name one usually discover, a few
months in, that they've built a newsletter and called it lifecycle.

**What to take from it:**

* Practitioners split lifecycle into three pillars — activation, monetisation, then
  retention and advocacy — and choose what to work on by asking where the gap in the
  customer journey is, which is the same question that sets the build order.
* Their favourite post-welcome move is the behaviour-triggered milestone email ("you've
  sent your 10th invoice"), which reinforces progress and quietly collects the
  first-party data that sharpens every later message.
* Assume nobody reads the whole welcome series, so deliberately reinforcing the same key
  points across later messages is expected, not redundant — and plain, clearly-written
  email routinely beats the heavily-designed kind.

***

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: The order to build them — and why
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
You can't build all eight programs at once — and you don't need to. Here's the order
most teams converge on, and the reasoning behind it, so you can adapt it rather than
follow it blindly.

### 1. Welcome + onboarding — first, and most important

It's hard to justify starting anywhere else, for three reasons:

* **Peak intent.** A new user is more engaged the moment after signup than they will
  ever be again. Welcome and onboarding emails consistently post the highest open and
  click rates of any automated message — welcome-email open rates commonly land around
  35–50% *(well-attested across benchmark reports; the high end is SaaS-blog
  territory — [Litmus's State of Email reports](https://www.litmus.com/state-of-email-reports)
  are the least salesy place to calibrate)*.
* **It plugs the biggest hole.** Most early churn happens in the gap between signup and
  activation. That's the widest leak in the bucket, and onboarding is the patch.
* **It's the highest-leverage growth lever you have.** Activated users — those who've
  reached the product's core value — retain roughly ~2× better than non-activated ones
  ([Lenny Rachitsky's activation benchmarks](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/what-is-a-good-activation-rate)).
  Doubling activation can beat doubling signups — at zero acquisition cost. Onboarding
  email is the cheapest way to move activation.

> ⚠️ You'll see "poor onboarding causes 40–60% of early churn" repeated everywhere.
> It's directional folklore with no single solid study behind it — believe the
> direction (onboarding matters enormously), not the decimal.

What a good welcome sequence actually contains, since "send a welcome series" hides all
the craft: message one delivers the single next action (not five features — the
first action from your activation funnel, the one you instrument earlier in the course);
message two, a day or two later, either celebrates progress or unblocks the stall,
branching on behaviour (the next chapter shows the code); message three introduces
the second habit only once the first stuck. Every message earns the next; none of them
fires if the user has already moved past it. That last property — *not sending* — is
what separates lifecycle from a drip.

### 2. Dunning / failed-payment recovery — early, if you charge

**Dunning** — the retries and reminder emails that chase a failed payment — gets buried
in most lifecycle guides, because they're written for e-commerce. For a subscription
product it belongs near the top, because it's often the highest dollar-ROI journey
you'll ever build: it recovers revenue you already earned.

* **Involuntary churn** (failed cards, expirations) is 20–40% of total SaaS churn
  *(well-attested — consistent across many independent sources, including
  [Baremetrics' dunning writeup](https://baremetrics.com/blog/dunning-management))*.
* Well-run dunning recovers a large share of it.
  [Stripe's Smart Retries documentation](https://docs.stripe.com/billing/revenue-recovery/smart-retries)
  is the primary source worth reading: machine-timed retries plus reminder emails are
  the whole mechanism, and Stripe publishes how its retry timing is chosen. Vendor
  case studies claim recovery rates well above half of failed payments — treat the
  precise percentages as marketing, the mechanism as real.

**What to take from it:**

* Most failed payments are *involuntary* — an expired card, a bank flag — and have
  nothing to do with intent, yet the standard "your payment failed" email is missed by
  most people, because average email open rates sit around 20%.
* Recovery is a friction problem, not a persuasion one: a one-tap card-update link, an
  in-app "payment recovery wall" for users still in the product, and an SMS fallback
  (opens near 98% when email goes unseen) each remove a reason the fix didn't happen.
* The headline "89%" is a vendor best case — take the mechanism (retry + multi-channel
  reminder + a frictionless update), not the number.

A handful of retry-and-remind emails recovers a meaningful slice of your **MRR**
(monthly recurring revenue — the predictable subscription income a churned card quietly
subtracts). The sequence itself is short: an immediate "your payment didn't go through"
with a one-click card-update link, a reminder a few days later if the retry hasn't
cleared, and a final "your account is about to pause" — all of it exiting the instant a
payment succeeds (the next chapter builds exactly this sequence in code). These users
*want* to keep paying; their card just expired. You're removing friction, not
persuading, which is why this one rewards being built early.

### 3. Win-back / resurrection — next

Reactivating a lapsed user is cheaper than acquiring a new one, and they already
understand the value. Reactivation rates of 10–30% of lapsed users are the commonly
cited band *(directional)*. The structure that works —
[Klaviyo's win-back examples](https://www.klaviyo.com/blog/winback-email-campaign-examples)
are e-commerce but the shape transfers — is a gentle "here's what's new" first touch,
a concrete-value second touch, and only then an incentive if you use one at all.
Leading with what changed since they left works because "we miss you" answers no
question the user has. Dormancy is also worth segmenting honestly: 30 days idle in a
daily-use product is churn; in a tax product it's Tuesday. Your dormancy threshold
comes from *your* usage interval — the shape of your **retention curve** (how many users
are still active N days after signup, read off the retention chapter) — not from a
template.

### 4. Engagement + retention/habit — once the above are stable

Feature-adoption nudges and habit reinforcement, tuned to your product's natural usage
rhythm. These are the journeys that most reward behavioural branching (the
personalise-on-behaviour piece has the mechanics) — "you've never tried X" is only a
good email if it's true.

### 5. Referral — after you have a reliable activation milestone

You need a real "aha" to ask from — the ask only lands once the user has felt the
value, which is why this waits for a reliable activation milestone (the own-your-audience
chapter goes deeper).

### 6. Expansion / upsell — as monetisation matures

Upgrade and expansion nudges — fired as a user approaches a plan limit or shows
power-usage patterns — earn their place once monetisation is mature enough that there's
a paid tier worth expanding into. Built any earlier, they're a solution without a
problem.

> One thing to *drop*, not build: rigid, time-based lead-nurture drips that ignore
> what the user is actually doing. They underperform behavioural triggers
> ([MarTech's lifecycle-programs piece](https://martech.org/5-email-lifecycle-programs-you-need-and-one-to-drop/)
> makes the same call), and a plain newsletter does the relationship-building job
> better.

Build in this order and each journey compounds off the one before it. The single thing
never worth building is the message that fires on a clock instead of a behaviour.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Triggered beats batch-and-blast
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
A message triggered by behaviour reaches a person at a moment of intent; a batch send
hits everyone at once, regardless of where they are in their journey. That difference —
relevance pinned to a moment versus relevance averaged across a list — is the core
argument for the whole approach, and the performance gap it produces is large and
consistent:

* Automated/triggered flows are a tiny fraction of send volume but drive a hugely
  disproportionate share of revenue — a common benchmark is **flows ≈ 5% of sends but
  \~40% of email revenue** *(vendor benchmark, directional — the direction is rock
  solid even if the exact split varies)*.
* Triggered emails show materially higher open and click rates than batch campaigns —
  often roughly **70–120% higher opens and several times the click rate**. Treat these
  as a *range*, not a precise multiple; the numbers vary wildly by source.

> Avoid the fake-precise stats floating around win-back blogs ("2,361% higher
> conversion"). They're uncited SEO bait. The honest version: multi-touch behavioural
> sequences materially outperform single batch sends. You don't need invented decimals
> to make the case.

Why the gap exists is worth internalising, because it predicts where triggered wins
biggest. A batch send's relevance is the *average* relevance across your whole list —
necessarily mediocre. A triggered send's relevance is pinned to a moment: the user
*just* signed up, *just* hit a milestone, *just* had a card fail. The bigger the spread
in your users' states, the bigger triggered's advantage — which is why it dominates in
SaaS onboarding (users are wildly spread) and matters less for a weekly content digest
(everyone's in the same state: it's Thursday).

### How a product event becomes a message

The mechanism, which the next chapter implements:

1. You instrument a behaviour as an **event** with properties (the instrumentation
   chapter shows how) — `trial_started`, `project_created`, `payment_failed { amount }`.
2. A journey is **subscribed** to that event. When the event fires, the journey starts
   for that user.
3. The journey runs its steps — wait, check a condition, send, branch — and an **exit
   condition** stops it the instant the goal is met (e.g. stop the "upgrade" sequence
   the moment the user upgrades).

A few reliable trigger → message pairs to model yours on, each pairing a behaviour with
one clear **call to action** (CTA):

| Behaviour | Message |
|---|---|
| Signed up | Welcome #1 — one clear CTA to the first action |
| Trial started | Onboarding sequence begins |
| Key action not done in 24–72h | Activation nudge |
| Milestone hit | Celebration + (timed) referral ask |
| Inactive 30 days | Re-engagement #1 |
| Inactive 60–90 days | Win-back with an incentive |
| Payment failed | Dunning: retry + update-card CTA |
| Subscription cancelled | Win-back / save offer |

Note what's *absent* from the table: "it's been 7 days since email #2." Time appears
only as a window on behaviour ("not done in 24–72h"), never as the reason to send. If the
trigger column starts filling with durations instead of behaviours, that's usually the
drip you were escaping, quietly creeping back in.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Why touch-points matter (and how many)
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
A single email is a single chance to catch someone at the right moment — and most of
the time you'll miss it. Sequences work because they account for missed opens and rising
intent over days. Multi-touch beats single-touch. The defensible framing: **a 3–5
message sequence materially outperforms a one-shot send.**

But messaging is a dial you can turn too far in either direction:

* **Under-messaging** is the silent failure. No onboarding push means the bucket just
  leaks. There's also a deliverability cost: senders who email irregularly see roughly
  *double* the unsubscribe rate of those who send consistently — inbox reputation is
  built by showing up predictably.
* **Over-messaging** burns the relationship: fatigue, unsubscribes, spam complaints,
  and damaged sender reputation. *(One survey claims ~56% of people unsubscribe after
  4 marketing emails in 30 days — directional, but the effect is real.)* The spam
  complaint is the expensive one — it doesn't just lose that user, it degrades
  delivery to everyone else (the deliverability piece explains why one complaint
  travels so far).

A workable default cadence:

* **Welcome series:** every 2–3 days for the first few touches, while intent is high.
* **Active users:** 1–3 per week, depending on your product's rhythm.
* **Dormant users:** slow and value-led — a user who's already drifting is the last
  person a barrage will win back.

The signal that tells you the dial has gone too far: **engagement per send drops with
each added message.** Opens, clicks, or revenue-per-email falling as you add sends is
the program telling you it's reached the useful maximum.

Two practices become load-bearing the moment you run more than one journey:

* **Frequency capping** — a ceiling on total messages per user across all journeys and
  channels, so two journeys don't both fire on the same bad day. The failure mode it
  prevents is real and common: a user's card fails the same week they go dormant and
