  hit a plan limit, and three well-intentioned journeys each send twice. Every message
  was individually justified; the pile-up is what gets you marked as spam.
* **Suppression** — stop a sequence the instant its goal is met, and respect an opt-out
  on *every* channel, not just the one they unsubscribed from.

Get the cap and the suppressions right and the dial mostly takes care of itself: each
journey argues for its own messages, and the system keeps the total humane.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Deliverability: none of this works if you don't land
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
A message only earns its result if it reaches the inbox — and reaching the inbox is
earned, not given. Since early 2024 it's also *specified*. Gmail and Yahoo's bulk-sender
rules ([Google's sender guidelines](https://support.google.com/mail/answer/81126) are
the primary source;
[Resend's summary](https://resend.com/blog/gmail-and-yahoo-bulk-sending-requirements-for-2024)
is the readable version) turned the previously-soft best practices into hard
requirements:

* **Authenticate everything: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC.** These are DNS records proving
  your mail actually comes from your domain. No auth, no inbox — it's that binary now
  for senders above trivial volume.
* **One-click unsubscribe** (the `List-Unsubscribe` header), honoured within two days.
  Making people hunt for the link doesn't retain them; it converts unsubscribes into
  spam complaints.
* **Spam complaint rate below 0.3%** — and realistically well below it. This is the
  metric the cadence discipline from the touch-points piece exists to protect.

**What to take from it:**

* Authenticate and isolate: SPF, DKIM and DMARC prove your mail is really yours (DMARC
  also blocks spoofing), and sending marketing from a *subdomain* keeps a reputation
  problem there from dragging down your transactional mail.
* List hygiene *is* deliverability: only mail people who opted in, prune non-engagers and
  complainers, and never hard-bounce fake addresses when testing — the bounces are real
  even when the addresses aren't, and they damage your reputation.
* Small practical wins compound: keep emails under Gmail's ~102KB clip limit with a
  plain-text version, avoid no-reply senders (they signal one-way, low-trust mail), and
  warm up volume gradually instead of blasting a cold domain.

Beyond the hard rules, three habits keep a young domain healthy: **send from a
subdomain** (e.g. `mail.yourapp.com`) so lifecycle reputation is insulated from your
transactional mail; **warm up volume gradually** rather than blasting a cold list on
day one; and **prune non-engagers** — continuing to mail people who never open is how
good lists rot into spam-trap territory. None of this is busywork. A lifecycle program
with a damaged sender domain is a program that silently stopped existing; you'll keep
"sending" for months before anyone notices nothing lands.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Personalise on behaviour, not just name
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
`Hi {{first_name}}` is not personalisation. Real lifecycle personalisation branches on
**what the user did or didn't do in the product** — their plan, which features they've
touched, how many projects they've created, whether they hit the *aha moment* (the point
the product's value first clicks). Behavioural triggers reportedly drive several times
the engagement of time-based sends
*(directional —
[Userlist's trigger catalogue](https://userlist.com/blog/behavior-email-campaign-triggers/)
is a good idea-bank)*, and it's the thing a code-first engine does well: your journey
can read event history and person properties and take a different path for each user.
The next chapter shows exactly that.

The test for whether personalisation is real: **could this exact message have been sent
to a user in a different state?** "You created 3 projects but haven't invited a
teammate" passes. "Here are five tips for getting started," sent to someone who
finished setup a week ago, fails — and quietly teaches the user your emails aren't
worth opening. The damage of fake personalisation is cumulative and invisible in any
single send's metrics.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Multi-channel: email is the workhorse, not the only horse
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
Email carries most of a lifecycle program, but it isn't the only channel — and the
strongest programs pick the channel by the job the moment needs done, because each one
does something the others can't:

* **Email** — highest reach, best for storytelling and education. The workhorse for
  welcome, onboarding, and win-back.
* **In-app** — highest *relevance*, because the user is already in the product. Best
  for activation nudges and, crucially, **referral asks at the aha moment** (the moment
  the product's value has just clicked) — in-product asks convert better than email asks
  because you're catching them while they're holding the value.
* **SMS / push / Discord / Telegram** — urgency and community. Linking Discord and
  Telegram accounts (the own-your-audience chapter) lets community engagement feed the
  *same* journey graph as your emails.

The principle: **one trigger can branch across channels**, on unified data, evaluated in
real time — with frequency capping and cross-channel suppression applied across all of
them. The mistake to avoid is running channels as separate programs with separate
tools and separate opt-out lists: that's how a user who muted your emails gets the
same campaign by push, and how nobody on the team can answer "how many times did we
contact this person this week?"

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: The tools you could use (and when no-code is right)
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
The messaging-tool landscape is crowded, and a growth team should choose deliberately
rather than default into whatever the last company used. This course builds journeys in
Hogsend, but the honest map is worth knowing:

* **Customer.io** — the closest "serious" behavioural-messaging tool: event-triggered,
  multi-channel, strong segmentation. A genuinely good choice, especially for a
  marketing-owned program. Trade-off: your lifecycle logic lives in their UI, not your
  repo.
* **Loops.so** — clean, developer-friendly transactional + lifecycle email; a lovely
  developer experience (DX — how pleasant a tool is to build on) for simple flows.
  Trade-off: less powerful when your branching gets complex.
* **Klaviyo** — the e-commerce default; superb for cart/browse/post-purchase flows on
  Shopify. If you're commerce, start here. Weaker fit for product-event-driven SaaS.
* **Braze / Iterable** — enterprise-grade multi-channel orchestration. Powerful,
  expensive, and overkill until you're at real scale with a marketing team.
* **Intercom** — strong when messaging and support live together (in-app + email +
  inbox). You pay for the suite.
* **Resend Broadcasts / Mailchimp / ConvertKit** — broadcast-first. Fine for a
  newsletter; not built for behavioural, per-user journeys.
* **PostHog Workflows** — no-code, lives next to your data, good for batch + simple
  drips (introduced back in the PostHog chapter).

**When a no-code tool is the right call:** if a non-engineer owns messaging, if your
flows are simple drips, or if you need to ship something this week without touching the
codebase — Customer.io or Loops will serve you well, and there's no virtue in code for
its own sake.

**Why this course is code-first (Hogsend):** because for a product-led growth team — one
that treats growth as an engineering discipline rather than a budget — the messaging
layer *is* part of the product, and it benefits from the same things your product code
does: version control, code review, real branching logic, testing, and exactly-once
guarantees. When your journey needs to *read* arbitrary event history and *branch* on it,
a flowchart UI becomes the bottleneck; code doesn't. And the identity graph that makes
owning your audience work is much easier when the messaging engine is yours. The
trade-off is real and you should make it with eyes open: code-first asks an engineer to
own lifecycle. For the audience of this course, that's a feature, not a tax.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Prove it works: holdouts
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
Opens and clicks tell you a message was received, not whether it changed anything —
maybe those users would have converted regardless. The discipline that separates a real
lifecycle program from cargo-culting is measuring what a message *caused*, and you do
that by measuring **incrementality** with a **holdout**:

* Randomly withhold the message(s) from a **control group**, and compare the outcome —
  activation, retention, revenue — between the users who got messaged and those who
  didn't.
* The difference is the *incremental lift* you can actually attribute to the messaging.

Two kinds:

* **Global holdout** — a small % of users excluded from *all* lifecycle messaging.
  Measures the whole program's lift. This is the number that justifies the program's
  existence in the monthly growth review.
* **Campaign holdout** — users excluded from one specific journey. Measures that
  journey's lift, and powers the kill-or-keep decision per journey.

A worked example: messaged group upgrades at 18%, held-out group at 11% → **+7
points** of lift you can credit to the journey. A standing holdout on every important
flow, with lift reported monthly, is the habit that keeps the whole program honest. It's
the same correlation-versus-causation problem the activation metric solves: a number
that only *correlates* with success can't tell you what to build, and only an experiment
proves cause.
[Eppo's incrementality primer](https://www.geteppo.com/blog/marketing-incrementality-101)
covers the standard designs (user-level holdout, geo holdout, and PSA tests — where the
control group sees a neutral public-service ad instead of your real one) if you want
the fuller statistical picture, and
[Reforge's lock-down incrementality method](https://www.reforge.com/guides/method-run-a-lock-down-incrementality-test-on-meta-ads)
shows the same logic applied to a paid channel — the same holdout you're building here,
pointed at ad spend.

**What to take from it:**

* Incrementality is *causal* where attribution is only *correlational*: rather than
  overlaying a model on historical data — where seasonality, competitors, and product
  mix all confound the read — you run a point-in-time experiment that isolates the one
  thing you're measuring.
* The mechanism is a holdout: show the treatment to one randomly-chosen group, withhold
  it from a control, and attribute only the difference. It's geo holdouts in the ad
  world here, but it's exactly your lifecycle holdout with "region" swapped for "user."
* What the platform reports isn't the true lift — in the example, reported ROAS (return
  on ad spend) of 2.31 diverged from the incremental read of 2.78. That gap is the same
  reason opens and clicks can't tell you whether a message *caused* the conversion.

Common holdout mistakes, so you don't relearn them expensively: **too-small holdouts**
on low-volume journeys (a 5% holdout of 200 users tells you nothing — use 20–50% until
volume grows); **peeking early** and stopping the moment the numbers look good
(decide the read date in advance); and **quietly excluding the holdout from only some
messages** (the comparison is only clean if the held-out group's *entire* experience
differs by exactly the thing you're measuring).

A code-first engine makes holdouts natural: randomly skip enrollment for a held-out
cohort and compare their downstream events. The next chapter shows where that hook lives
([Blueshift's holdout writeup](https://blueshift.com/blog/product-spotlight-understand-the-true-impact-of-your-campaigns-with-holdout-testing/)
shows the same idea in a no-code tool, if that's your stack). This is also where
lifecycle messaging stops being "marketing" and becomes the growth-team experiment
loop — every important journey a standing A/B test with a measured result, not an act of
faith.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Which journeys first, for your kind of startup
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
The default build order — welcome and onboarding first, dunning early if you charge,
then win-back — is a self-serve SaaS default, not a universal law. Your business model
votes on the order too: the widest hole in a commerce funnel sits in a different place
from the widest hole in a marketplace, and the journey that earns its build first is
whichever one plugs *your* biggest leak. Here's how the order reweights by type:

* **Self-serve SaaS** — welcome/onboarding → dunning → win-back, exactly as the default
  has it.
* **E-commerce** — abandoned-cart and browse-abandonment first (the highest-ROI flows in
  commerce), then post-purchase and win-back. Klaviyo territory.
* **Consumer app** — habit/streak and resurrection dominate; onboarding is about
  forming the habit, not "setup."
* **Marketplace** — onboarding has *two* versions (supply and demand), and the key
  retention message is "your side of the market is active" (a new listing, a new buyer).
* **Community / web3** — the link-and-verify and gamification journeys (the
  own-your-audience chapter) *are* the lifecycle program; email supports them.
* **B2B with sales** — onboarding + expansion/upsell matter most; dunning if self-serve
  billing exists; here lifecycle messaging assists the human, it doesn't replace them.

The stages are the same everywhere; only their order changes. Find the widest hole in
your particular bucket, and start there.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
Six decisions are worth settling while the chapter is fresh — each one makes the build
in the next chapter noticeably smoother:

1. **Pick the first journey** — welcome + onboarding, for the peak-intent reasons the
   build-order piece lays out (with dunning next if you charge; it's the fastest revenue
   win in the list).
2. **Map each journey to its events** — the one that triggers it and the one that
   exits it. If the exit event is hard to name, that's usually a sign the journey
   doesn't have a clear goal yet.
3. **Sketch your cadence** per stage and a global frequency cap, so the same-bad-week
   pile-up from the touch-points piece can't happen by accident.
4. **Check the deliverability basics** — SPF/DKIM/DMARC on a sending subdomain and
   one-click unsubscribe. Cheap to set up now; expensive to retrofit once a domain's
   reputation is damaged.
5. **Choose your tool deliberately** — and if it's code-first, the next chapter is the
   build.
6. **Plan a holdout** for that first journey, so its lift is measured from day one
   rather than argued about later.

You now know *what* to build and *why*. Next, you build it — in code.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Customer.io — lifecycle automation & personalization guide](https://customer.io/learn/lifecycle-marketing/automation-and-personalization-guide) — the whole program as a five-step arc, from a tool built for exactly this.
* [Reforge — build your lifecycle marketing strategy](https://www.reforge.com/guides/build-your-lifecycle-marketing-strategy) — the retention-as-output framing the stages piece leans on.
* [Lenny Rachitsky — what is a good activation rate](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/what-is-a-good-activation-rate) — the activation benchmarks behind the "build onboarding first" argument.
* [Stripe — Smart Retries and revenue recovery](https://docs.stripe.com/billing/revenue-recovery/smart-retries) — the primary source on dunning mechanics.
* [Klaviyo — win-back email examples](https://www.klaviyo.com/blog/winback-email-campaign-examples) — flow structure and timing for resurrection, transferable beyond e-commerce.
* [Google — email sender guidelines](https://support.google.com/mail/answer/81126) — the actual inbox rules; everything else is commentary.
* [Resend — Gmail & Yahoo bulk sending requirements](https://resend.com/blog/gmail-and-yahoo-bulk-sending-requirements-for-2024) — the readable version of those rules.
* [Litmus — State of Email reports](https://www.litmus.com/state-of-email-reports) — benchmark data for calibrating open/click expectations without vendor spin.
* [Eppo — marketing incrementality 101](https://www.geteppo.com/blog/marketing-incrementality-101) — holdout test designs, one level deeper than the holdouts piece.
* [Reforge — run a lock-down incrementality test](https://www.reforge.com/guides/method-run-a-lock-down-incrementality-test-on-meta-ads) — the same holdout logic applied to paid channels, previewing the paid-acquisition chapter.
* [Userlist — behavioural email triggers](https://userlist.com/blog/behavior-email-campaign-triggers/) — an idea-bank of trigger → message pairs.

growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.title: 5 — Lifecycle messaging: which emails, in what order
growth-with-posthog/05-lifecycle-messagi.text:
> **In this chapter:** the lifecycle stages mapped to the AARRR funnel (Acquisition,
> Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue), the order to build them in (and why), why
> triggered messages beat batch-and-blast, how many touch-points is right, how to stay
> out of the spam folder, the landscape of tools (and when no-code is the right call),
> and how to measure impact honestly with holdouts. This is the strategy; the next
> chapter is the code.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into twelve short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Four of them carry a short video, with a transcript and a distilled
digest underneath so the point sticks even if you don't watch. Work through them in
order, or jump to what you need:

1. [Lifecycle messaging plugs the bucket](./01-plugs-the-bucket.mdx) — the right message, triggered by the right behaviour, at the right time.
2. [The stages, mapped to AARRR](./02-stages-aarrr.mdx) — the eight programs and which stage each one serves.
3. [The order to build them — and why](./03-build-order.mdx) — welcome first, dunning early if you charge, then win-back.
4. [Triggered beats batch-and-blast](./04-triggered-beats-batch.mdx) — how a product event becomes a message, and why the gap is huge.
5. [Why touch-points matter](./05-touch-points.mdx) — multi-touch beats single-touch, and the dial you can turn too far.
6. [Deliverability](./06-deliverability.mdx) — none of this works if you don't land in the inbox.
7. [Personalise on behaviour](./07-personalise-on-behaviour.mdx) — `Hi {{first_name}}` is not personalisation.
8. [Multi-channel](./08-multi-channel.mdx) — email is the workhorse, not the only horse.
9. [The tools you could use](./09-the-tools.mdx) — the honest map, and when no-code is the right call.
10. [Prove it works: holdouts](./10-holdouts.mdx) — opens and clicks don't tell you what a message *caused*.
11. [Which journeys first](./11-which-journeys-first.mdx) — how the default order shifts by your kind of startup.
12. [Before you move on](./12-before-you-move-on.mdx) — what to settle now, then the quiz.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter.**

* **AARRR** — Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue: the five stages a user moves through, from first touch to paying and referring others.
* **Activation** — the moment a new user first reaches the product's core value; the target of onboarding.
* **Aha moment** — the first time the product's value clicks for a user.
* **Dunning** — the sequence of retries and reminder emails that recovers a failed subscription payment.
* **Holdout** — a randomly withheld control group, used to measure what a message actually *caused* rather than what merely correlated.
* **Incrementality** — the lift you can credit to a message, proven by comparing the messaged group against a holdout.
* **MRR** — monthly recurring revenue, the predictable subscription income a business earns each month.
* **ROAS** — return on ad spend, revenue divided by what you paid to acquire it.
* **CTA** — call to action: the single thing a message asks the reader to do.
* **DX** — developer experience: how pleasant a tool is to build on.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: The lifecycle loop
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Every lifecycle message runs through the same small loop, whatever its job — the welcome,
the **dunning** notice that recovers a failed payment, the **win-back** that re-engages a
user who's gone quiet, all eight of them. Strip away the copy and each one is the same
three beats:

1. **Something happens.** A real thing a user did: they signed up, hit an activation
   milestone, let their card expire, went quiet for two weeks. This is an *event* — the
   same server-side events you're already sending to PostHog when you instrument the product.
2. **The right message goes out at the right time.** Not to everyone — to *that one user*,
   keyed to what they just did, after whatever delay makes it land well.
3. **You measure whether it worked.** The message reports back — it emits its own events —
   so you can see in PostHog whether it lifted the number you built it to lift.

That's the whole model. A message is worth building only when you can name all three:
the event that starts it, the thing you want to happen, and the way you'll know if it did.

### Why per-user beats a batch blast

This loop is the difference between lifecycle messaging and a newsletter, and it's worth
being precise about why it wins — the case for lifecycle as a discipline, brought all the
way down to the level of a single send.

A **batch blast** is calendar-driven: you pick a Tuesday, write one message, and send it
to everyone on the list at once. It ignores what any individual user is doing. The person
who signed up an hour ago and the person who's been paying for two years get the same
email at the same moment — one of whom it's wrong for.

A **triggered, per-user loop** is behaviour-driven: each user gets the message the moment
their own behaviour earns it. The welcome email fires when *they* sign up, not on the
sender's schedule. The upgrade nudge fires when *they* hit the usage ceiling. Relevance
stops being a thing you guess at and becomes a property of *when* the message is allowed
to fire.

The most underrated consequence of this is what the loop *doesn't* do. A batch blast has
no way to know a user already did the thing — so it emails them anyway. A triggered loop
only reaches the people the event actually describes, and stops the instant they move
past it. The power of a lifecycle program is as much in who it *doesn't* email as who it
does, and the loop is what makes that restraint automatic rather than a thing you police
by hand.

### One event, two jobs

The events that drive this loop are the same server-side events you're already emitting
for analytics. You don't build a separate tracking layer for lifecycle — the moment your
backend records "this user signed up" for PostHog, that same signal can start a **journey**
(the automated, multi-step sequence that carries one user through the emails that follow).
One emission, two consumers: analytics *measures* it, lifecycle *acts on* it.

Sending these events from your server rather than the browser matters for a second
reason: it's ad-blocker-proof. Roughly a third of users block
client-side scripts, so a lifecycle program that depended on a browser tag would silently
skip a third of the people it was built to reach. Server-side events don't have that
hole — every user who does the thing gets counted, and gets the message.

### The messages have to wait — reliably

There's one property of lifecycle work that shapes every tool built for it: a lifecycle
message is mostly *waiting*. Send, wait two days, check, send, wait five days, check. A
single journey can hold a user in mid-flight for weeks.

That waiting has to survive the real world. Your servers get redeployed, restarted, and
occasionally fall over — and none of that is allowed to strand a user halfway through a
sequence or fire a two-days-later email six days late. Getting this right by hand (a
spreadsheet of "who's due what, when," poked by a nightly cron) is exactly the fragile
plumbing that makes teams give up on lifecycle. It's infrastructure you want handled for
you, so you can spend your attention on the *decisions* — which is what the rest of this
chapter is about. (For how Hogsend makes the waiting durable, see
[the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs).)

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Anatomy of a lifecycle message
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Every lifecycle email you'll ever build comes down to six decisions. Get them right and
the implementation is almost mechanical; get them wrong and no amount of clever copy
saves the message. These are the decisions a growth person owns — the tool just carries
them out. Here they are in the order you actually make them.

### 1. The trigger — which event starts it

The trigger is the single behaviour that means *now is the moment*. Choosing it is
choosing when the message fires, so it's the most consequential decision on the list. "A
user signed up" is a trigger. So is "a user hit their fifth project," "a payment failed,"
or "no activity for fourteen days."

Precision here is everything. "Signed up" and "signed up *and is on the free plan*" are
different triggers that produce different programs — the second one never wastes an
upgrade sequence on someone who paid at the door. The tighter you can describe the
moment, the less filtering you have to do later. The events you already defined when you
instrumented your product are exactly the raw material; the craft is picking the one
that most precisely marks the moment this message earns its place in the inbox.

### 2. The audience — who's eligible, and who to suppress

A trigger fires for a lot of people. The audience decision narrows it to the people this
message is actually *for*, and — just as importantly — excludes the people it would harm.

Two separate questions live here:

* **Eligibility.** Who qualifies? Free-plan users only. Trial users in their first week.
  Accounts over a certain size. This is a filter on top of the trigger.
* **Suppression and re-enrollment.** Who should *not* get this even though they qualify?
  Someone who's already mid-upgrade shouldn't get the upgrade nudge. Someone who finished
  this sequence last week shouldn't be dropped straight back into it. You decide whether a
  user can enter this journey *once ever*, *once per period*, or *every time the trigger
  fires* — and that re-enrollment policy is one of the most common places lifecycle
  programs go wrong. Set it too loose and you spam; too tight and you miss a legitimate
  second occasion.

This is a correctness decision, not a copy decision. The best-written email sent to the
wrong person is a complaint waiting to happen — and a high complaint rate is the fastest
way to wreck your sender reputation and drop the rest of your mail into spam.

### 3. The timing — the delay, and the time of day

Two sub-decisions, both easy to under-think.

The **delay** is a tactic. Fire the welcome email instantly — intent is never higher than
the second after signup. But the "you've been quiet" nudge needs to wait long enough that
being quiet actually means something, and the "how's it going?" check-in lands better on
day three than day zero. Too soon feels robotic; too late misses the moment.

The **time of day** is the one teams forget. A message that resolves to "send two days
later" will, naively, fire at whatever 3am-in-the-user's-timezone your server clock
happens to hit. The fix is to send in the *user's* local time and inside a sane send
window — a well-timed 9am email beats a perfectly-written 3am one, because nobody reads
the 3am one. Deciding this is a strategy call ("breakfast, their time"); making it happen
reliably is what the engine handles for you — see [the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs)
for the timezone and send-window mechanics.

### 4. The content — one job, one CTA

A lifecycle email is closer to a *receipt* than a *brochure*: one job, one call to action,
plain and fast. The content decision is really a decision about *restraint* — what to
leave out. Every lifecycle message should be able to answer "what is the one thing I want
this person to do?" in a single sentence. If it can't, it's two emails wearing a trench
coat. The craft of the email itself — how to make it land — is a subject of its own; for
now, the anatomical point is that "one job" is a design constraint you set here, before a
word is written.

### 5. The exit — the event that stops it

The exit is the mirror image of the trigger, and it's every bit as important. It's the
event that ends the journey early, the instant it happens.

The canonical case: an upgrade sequence should stop the *moment* the user upgrades. If it
doesn't, they get "still thinking about upgrading?" a day after they already paid — which
reads as "we don't even know you're a customer," the most trust-eroding message you can
send. This is the suppression principle made concrete: the exit is how a journey knows
when it has been overtaken by events and should get out of the way.

A journey that's parked mid-wait — say, five days into a check-in delay — has to be able
to abort cleanly when its exit event arrives, so no later step fires after the reason for
it has gone. Deciding *what* stops the message is the growth call; wiring the exit so it
fires even mid-wait is engine territory
([the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs) cover it).

### 6. The measurement — did it lift anything

The last decision closes the lifecycle loop every message runs: how will you know this
message worked? A lifecycle email exists to move a number — activation, recovered revenue,
reactivation. If you can't say which number and can't see it move, you've built a message
you can neither improve nor safely retire.

So every message should report back — emit events on send, open, click, and (ideally) the
downstream action it was trying to cause — so the effect shows up in PostHog next to the
funnel it's meant to help. This is what lets you answer the only question that matters
after launch: *is this email earning its place, or is it quietly costing me sender
reputation for no lift?* A message you can't measure is a message you'll never turn off,
even when it's hurting.

***

Those six — trigger, audience, timing, content, exit, measurement — are the whole anatomy.
Notice that five of the six are pure growth judgment; none of them is a coding problem.
The value you add is in the decisions. The engine's job is to execute them faithfully, in
the right order, for the right user, at the right time — and to keep doing it through
every deploy and restart. For how each decision maps to an actual journey, see
[the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs).

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: What makes a good lifecycle email
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
A lifecycle email lives under constraints a marketing page never faces. It has to render
in email clients whose CSS support froze around 2009, clear the spam filters that decide
whether it reaches an inbox at all, and do exactly one job the moment someone glances at
it. The journey settles *when* a message fires and *who* gets it; the email itself decides
what actually lands. Good lifecycle email is a craft, and it's worth learning what
separates a message that works from one that gets ignored — or worse, marked as spam.

### Deliverability is the first feature

None of the craft matters if the message lands in spam, so deliverability is the first
thing to get right on every individual send: authenticate your sending domain, keep a
plain-text version so the message clears the filters and stays under Gmail's ~102KB clip
limit, and watch the phrases that drag a spam score up. The best-designed email in the
world scores zero if half of it never reaches an inbox — so treat "does this land?" as the
first question about a template, not the last.

### The rendering reality

Email is not the web. The clients your users read in — old *and* new — still don't support
most of the CSS you'd reach for on a landing page, so an email has to be built as
table-based HTML with inline styles to survive the trip. Modern component-based tooling
(the [React Email](https://react.email/docs/introduction) model) exists precisely to hide
this: you write ordinary components and it emits the cross-client HTML underneath. The
video below is Resend's founder building and sending a template end to end — the clearest
tour of what "author once, render everywhere" actually looks like.

**What to take from it:**

* The Vercel × Resend template wires an isolated API key into your project *mid-deploy*,
  so a fresh Next.js app ships with email hooked up from the first commit — no separate
  setup step.
* The email itself is a React Email component with typed props and Tailwind; Zeno's whole
  point is that you write ordinary React and get the Outlook/Gmail cross-client
  compatibility handled for you, instead of hand-writing inline styles and table soup.
* React Email's local dev server previews every template in mobile and desktop, shows the
  HTML *and* plain-text versions, and lets you fire a real test send — the exact
  preview-before-you-send loop this piece recommends.

The next video is a conference take on *why* the component model fits email's constraints
so well — good background on the rendering problem before you design your own layout.

**What to take from it:**

* The problem React Email exists to solve: email clients — old *and* new (Gmail, Yahoo,
  Outlook, Superhuman, Notion Mail) — still don't support SVG, `border-radius`, and most
  modern CSS, so you need a component layer that emits cross-client, table-based HTML.
* The workflow is three steps — *code* the template (unstyled components + Tailwind +
  typed props that fail the build when missing), *test* it (preview across clients, a
  linter that flags broken and 404 links), then *send* it (a `render` util turns the
  component into plain HTML any provider accepts).
* The existing open-source options were stale (one's last commit was seven years ago;
  another's docs were badly outdated), which is exactly why a fresh component-model tool
  was worth building.

### Plain text still matters

A serious lifecycle email ships with a plain-text version, not as an afterthought but as a
real signal. Filters read it as a sign of a well-formed message; screen readers rely on
it; and plenty of people read in a preview pane where the plain text is what they see
first. Skipping it costs you deliverability and accessibility at once — two things a
lifecycle program can't afford to leak.

### Receipt, not brochure

Here is the single most useful mental model for lifecycle email: **a lifecycle email is
closer to a receipt than a brochure.** One job, one call to action, plain and fast. The
best lifecycle emails look almost boring — a sentence of context, the one action you want,
a button, done. That's not a lack of polish; it's the polish. A user who opened your "your
payment failed" email wants a card-update link, not a hero image and three testimonials.
The transactional-email conventions worth internalising —
[Postmark's guide](https://postmarkapp.com/guides/transactional-email-best-practices) is
the standard reference — all point the same way: respect the reader's time, say the one
thing, make the action unmissable.

### Test it before thousands of people see it

A template is about to sit in front of every user a trigger matches, so it's worth
actually looking at it first. Modern tooling makes this cheap: preview every template with
sample data across clients, send a test to yourself against
[test addresses](https://resend.com/docs/dashboard/emails/send-test-emails) (or a capture
sandbox — [Mailtrap's testing guide](https://mailtrap.io/blog/how-to-test-email/) covers
the methodology) before pointing at real users, and run the copy through a spam checker in
preview rather than discovering the problem after a send. The last video covers exactly
this linting-and-compatibility layer.

**What to take from it:**

* React Email 4 adds a spam checker (it runs SpamAssassin, the standard open-source tool)
  that scores your email the way inboxes will and flags the phrases dragging the score
  down — catch deliverability problems in preview, not after a send.
* The linter surfaces accessibility and performance issues — a missing `alt` tag, an
  oversized image that slows the client — and links each finding straight to the offending
  line.
* The compatibility checker (backed by "Can I email") flags CSS that breaks in specific
  clients — `object-fit`, or `height` vs `min-height` diverging between Outlook and Yahoo —
  so the "Outlook breaks this" bug is caught in the preview instead of a customer's inbox.

### Own your emails

There's a strategic decision hiding inside all of this: *where do your emails live?* The
common default is a rented drag-and-drop tool, where each template lives in someone else's
dashboard. It's fast to start, and it costs you three things that compound over time.

* **You can't version or review them.** An email that lives in a dashboard has no history
  you can diff, no pull request where a teammate catches the broken link before it ships,
  and no way to tell which version went out with last quarter's campaign. When you own the
  email, it sits in your codebase next to everything else — reviewable, revertable, and
  diffable like any other change.
* **Personalisation is capped at the tool's merge tags.** A rented editor lets you drop in
  a first name and whatever fields it chose to expose. When the email is yours, it can be
  personalised on *your* data — the same activation events, plan details, and behaviour
  you already track — so the message can say something the merge-tag menu never could.
* **Content and plumbing get tangled.** In the owned model the split stays clean: the
  journey decides *who* gets mail and *when*; the template decides only *what it says*. The
  same separation your product already keeps between logic and view — logic never
  concatenates HTML, and a view never decides who it's for.

None of this argues against a hosted *sending provider* — you still want someone reliable
putting bytes on the wire, and Resend, Postmark, and the like do that job well. The
argument is about where the *email itself* is authored and reviewed: in your repo, under
version control, personalised on your own data — versus locked in a canvas you rent. For
how Hogsend renders these owned templates and hands them to whichever provider you've
configured, see [the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs).

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Welcome + onboarding (Activation)
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
The welcome-and-onboarding pair is the first journey to build, and the highest-leverage
one you'll ever ship, because it patches the **widest leak in the bucket** — the gap
between someone signing up and someone reaching first value, where most products lose the
users they just paid to acquire. A new user is more engaged the moment after
signup than they will ever be again. Welcome emails post the highest open and click
rates of any automated message — commonly **35–50%** open rates
([Litmus's State of Email reports](https://www.litmus.com/state-of-email-reports) are
the least salesy place to calibrate). Spend that attention on the one action that
predicts they'll stay.

Almost all of the work is done by two messages: a **welcome** the instant they sign up,
and an **activation nudge** a day or two later — sent *only if they haven't reached the
aha moment yet.* Together they plug the biggest hole in your funnel; separately, each
has a job precise enough to write on an index card.

### Message one — the welcome

The welcome is the receipt on their decision to sign up and the single push toward first
value, and it exists because peak intent is perishable: the further a user gets from the
signup click without doing the one thing that makes the product *click for them*, the less
likely they ever will. Its trigger is the signup event itself — the account-created /
`user.created` event your backend emits, not a nightly batch or a **cohort** sync (a cohort
being a group of users defined by a shared trait) — and its timing is *immediate*, within
seconds. This is the one lifecycle email where waiting costs you: the user is still in the
tab, so the welcome is a continuation of the session rather than an interruption of their
day, and the timezone and send-window discipline that governs everything else simply
doesn't apply. Send now.

Everyone who signs up should get it, exactly once. Guard against the double-fire — a user
who submits twice, a retried webhook — with a short suppression window so no one receives
two welcomes, and exit the journey the instant the account is deleted, because a churned or
bounced signup should never get the day-two follow-up. The content is a receipt, not a
brochure: one job, one CTA (call to action), and that CTA is *the single first action from
your activation funnel* — "create your first project," "connect your data," "invite a
teammate" — never a five-feature tour. Add one line setting expectations (what this list
is, how often you'll write) so the next email is welcome rather than a surprise; everything
else is noise competing with the first-value action. Whether it actually worked is a
separate question: opens and clicks only tell you the subject line and CTA are landing, and
the number that matters is downstream — the activation rate of welcomed users against a
small random holdout you deliberately don't email. That holdout is the only honest way to
separate "the email caused activation" from "these users were going to activate anyway,"
because correlation is not causation and a held-out group is how you tell the two apart on
your own program.

### Message two — the activation nudge

The activation nudge is a single, well-aimed follow-up for the users who signed up and then
*stalled* — the ones who never reached the aha moment — and it's where the signup-to-
activation leak actually gets plugged. The welcome catches the users who were always going
to activate; the nudge recovers the ones who wouldn't have. Its trigger isn't a timer
firing blindly but a *check*: a day or two after the welcome, the journey looks at whether
the user has hit the activation event yet and branches on the answer. Reached it, and they
don't need this email (send "here's what to try next," or nothing); didn't, and the nudge
goes out. That's behavioural personalisation — the send is conditioned on what the user
actually did, not on a guess.

Roughly **48 hours** is the common window: long enough that the keen users have already
activated and filtered themselves out, short enough that the stalled ones haven't gone cold.
But *when* on that day matters too — a fixed 48-hour delay after a 2am signup lands the
nudge at 2am, whereas sending at 9am in the user's own timezone, inside a sane send window,
is a small change that makes the whole program feel human rather than machine-timed. The
audience takes care of itself, because the branch reads behaviour before choosing what to
send: only the not-yet-activated get the nudge, everyone who reached the aha moment in the
gap is suppressed automatically, and a deleted account exits before the check ever runs. The
content should remove the *specific* blocker, not a generic "you haven't finished setup" —
if the activation action is "connect your data source," the nudge is a short, concrete
unstick for that exact step, the one link standing between them and value, one job and one
CTA, same as the welcome. To measure its lift, compare the activation rate of the nudged
cohort against a holdout that gets the welcome but not the nudge, which isolates the nudge's
own contribution — and watch the *reason* users stall, because if most are stuck on the same
step, that's a product signal worth more than any email.

### Why this pair is the whole ballgame

Activated users retain roughly **~2× better** than non-activated ones
([Lenny Rachitsky's activation benchmarks](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/what-is-a-good-activation-rate)),
and most early churn happens in exactly the window these two emails cover. Doubling
activation can beat doubling signups — at zero acquisition cost. That's why the welcome
pair is built first: it's the cheapest lever you have on the traffic you already paid
for, and it moves the metric (activation) that everything downstream compounds on.

The behavioural branch is what separates this from a drip. A drip would send both
emails to everyone on a timer; this program *reads what happened* and only sends the
nudge to the users who need it — respect and relevance in the same move. When the
mechanics matter (how the trigger, the wait, the behavioural check, and the timezone
scheduling actually get wired), [the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs) cover the
implementation; the strategy above is what makes the implementation worth building.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: A behavioural nudge series (Activation)
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Most onboarding grows past a single welcome-and-nudge pair into a **series** — three to
five touches over the first week or two — and the good ones all share one shape:

> **Wait a beat. Check whether the action you wanted actually happened. Send the next
> nudge only if it didn't.**

That single loop — sleep, check, send-only-if-needed — covers most onboarding programs
you'll ever write. It's one principle — branch on behaviour, not on a guess — turned into
a repeatable rhythm.

### Why "send only if needed" beats a fixed drip

A fixed drip fires every message to every signup on a timer, regardless of what they've
done. The behavioural series only reaches the users who actually stalled — and that
difference is both strategic and moral:

* **Relevance.** "You haven't tried the key feature yet" is only a good email *if it's
  true.* A drip sends it to the user who tried the feature an hour ago, and instantly
  reads as "this company doesn't know what I'm doing." The behavioural check makes every
  send true by construction.
* **Respect.** Attention is the scarcest thing a new user gives you. Spending it only
  when you have something the user genuinely needs is how you keep the *next* email
  worth opening. Every irrelevant send is a small withdrawal from that account.
* **Signal quality.** Because each touch is gated on a real event, the series doubles as
  a funnel diagnostic: the step where most users still need nudging is your product's
  worst stall, visible for free.

### How the nudges escalate

Escalation doesn't mean louder — it means *later.* Each touch targets the next blocker
in the activation path, not a more urgent version of the last message:

1. **First nudge** (~day 2): the first-value action from your activation funnel — the
   thing the welcome already asked for, now unblocked more concretely.
2. **Second nudge** (~day 3–4): the *next* step, the one that only makes sense once the
   first is done — deeper setup, the second habit, the integration that makes the
   product sticky.
3. **Later touches**, if any: help, social proof, or a human offer ("reply if you're
   stuck") — used sparingly.

Each message assumes the user completed the previous step, because the behavioural
gate guarantees the ones still receiving mail haven't. The series walks the stalls in
order rather than dumping the whole product on day one.

### When to stop

This is the discipline that separates a series from nagging:

* **Stop the instant they activate.** The moment the user reaches the aha moment, the
  series has done its job — every further nudge now contradicts their behaviour and
  erodes the trust the welcome earned. The whole program should end there, mid-sequence,
  automatically.
* **Cap the number of touches.** Three to five, then stop, even if they never activated.
  A series with no end trains people to tune you out or unsubscribe — and an
  unsubscribe costs you every *future* journey, not just this one.
* **Never nudge a churned or unsubscribed user.** Deleted accounts exit; unsubscribes
  are honoured at send time. Re-checking who still wants to hear from you before a late
  send keeps the program's logic honest.

### How to prove it worked

Hold back a random slice of stalled users from the nudges and compare their activation
rate against the nudged cohort. The gap is the lift the series actually *caused* — not
the correlation you'd get by comparing "users who opened the emails" (who were always
your keenest). When the timing, the behavioural checks, and the exit-on-activation wiring
matter, [the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs) cover the implementation; the
strategy here is what decides whether it's worth wiring at all.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Win-back (Reactivation)
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Win-back re-engages a user who's gone dormant — slipped from active to silent without
formally cancelling. It plugs the *resurrection* leak — the reactivation stage of the
**AARRR** funnel (Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue) — the pool of
users who already understood the value, already got through onboarding, and then drifted. That's
why it's worth building — reactivating a lapsed user is far cheaper than acquiring a new
one, and you're not persuading a stranger, you're reminding someone who already believed.
Commonly cited reactivation rates land in the **10–30% of lapsed users** band
([Klaviyo's win-back examples](https://www.klaviyo.com/blog/winback-email-campaign-examples)
are e-commerce but the shape transfers) — *directional*, but enough to matter.

### First, define dormancy for *your* product

Everything about win-back depends on one number, and it's not a number you can borrow.
Dormancy is the inactivity window that means "this user is slipping away," and it comes
straight from your product's natural usage rhythm:

* **30 days idle in a daily-use product is churn.** In a tax-filing product, it's a
  Tuesday. Same 30 days, opposite meaning.
* Your threshold comes from **your retention curve** — how many users are still active as
  the days since signup add up — read to the interval after which a silent user almost
  never comes back on their own. Find the point where the
  curve flattens into "gone" and set the dormancy window just before it, while a nudge
  can still catch them.
* Set it **too short** and you'll pester active users between normal sessions — the
  fastest way to *cause* the churn you were trying to prevent. Set it **too long** and
  you're emailing people who have already emotionally cancelled. The window is a tuning
  dial, not a default.

That threshold becomes the **trigger**: a dormancy event — "no core action in N days" —
that you emit from a scheduled check or a PostHog cohort that fires when a user crosses
the line. The journey subscribes to that event; getting the definition right is 80% of
the work before a single word of copy is written.

### Give them a real reason to return — not "we miss you"

The instinct is to lead with sentiment. Resist it. *"We miss you"* answers no question
the user actually has, and it makes the email about you. Lead with what changed since
they left — the concrete value waiting for them now:

* **What's new / what they're missing.** The feature that shipped, the data that
  accumulated, the thing that's better than when they left. Give them a reason grounded
  in *their* interest, not your loneliness.
* **One job, one CTA.** A win-back is a receipt, not a brochure — the single most
  compelling reason to come back, and one link to act on it. Bury it in a feature roundup
  and you've wasted the one open you were going to get.
* **Personalise on their history where you can.** "Here's what happened in the project
  you started" beats a generic "here's what's new" — the behavioural detail is what
  makes it feel addressed to a person rather than a segment.

### The one-window-then-stop discipline

This is what keeps win-back from tipping into desperation. The whole program is: **send
once, give them a bounded window to come back on their own, send exactly one final nudge
if they don't — then stop.**

* **Bound the comeback window as a tactic.** After the first email, don't fire the
  second on a blind timer. *Wait to see if they return* — give them, say, five days — and
  only send the final nudge if the window elapses with no sign of them. That bounded
  wait is what makes win-back feel human: the program is listening for the user's own
  behaviour, not counting down regardless.
* **Exit the instant they return.** If the user becomes active at any point — even the
  day after the first email — the journey stops immediately. Nothing is more corrosive
  to trust than a "we miss you" email landing the morning after someone logged back in.
  Returning is the goal; the moment it happens, the program has no further business.
* **One window, then leave them alone.** Don't restart the sequence next week. A user who
  ignored a genuine reason to return and a final nudge has told you something; hammering
  them past that just earns unsubscribes and spam complaints, which cost you every future
  journey. Cap re-entry to once per dormancy period.
* **Re-check consent after the wait.** A long wait can straddle an unsubscribe. An exit
  event ends the journey; an unsubscribe is a preference honoured at send time — but
  confirming who still wants to hear from you before the final nudge keeps the program's
  logic honest.

### How to measure lift

Compare the return rate of the win-back cohort against a holdout of dormant users you
deliberately don't email. Some lapsed users drift back on their own; the holdout is the
only way to tell how many you actually *reactivated* versus how many would have returned
anyway. Watch the downstream number too — a user who opens the email but churns again in
a week wasn't really won back. When the dormancy trigger, the bounded wait-for-return,
and the exit-on-activity wiring matter,
[the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs) cover the implementation; the strategy above
is what makes it worth building.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Dunning (Revenue / Churn prevention)
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Of every message in your lifecycle program, this is the one that pays for the rest.
Dunning is the sequence that recovers a **failed payment** — and it earns more per email
sent than anything else you'll build, because it's aimed squarely at revenue you've
already won and are about to lose for no good reason.

The reason it out-earns every other message is the churn it targets. Somewhere between
20% and 40% of churn is *involuntary* — not a customer who decided to leave, but a card
that expired, a bank that declined a charge, a limit that was briefly hit. These people
still want your product; they chose to pay and simply don't know they've stopped. A short,
well-timed nudge recovers a large fraction of them, which makes dunning the closest thing
to free money in the whole funnel. Every point of involuntary churn you claw back drops
straight to retained **MRR** (monthly recurring revenue) — the predictable subscription
income you'd otherwise just lose.

The trigger is a failed-charge webhook. The sequence starts when your payment provider
tells you a charge failed — a Stripe `invoice.payment_failed`, or the equivalent from
whoever bills your customers. That webhook becomes an event in your pipeline, and that
event enrolls the customer in the dunning journey. The billing system is the source of
truth here, not a scheduled guess — you react the moment money actually fails to move.
(Wiring an external webhook into a trigger is covered in
[the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs).)

The shape is retry plus reminder. Your payment processor is already retrying the card on
its own schedule (Stripe's Smart Retries, for example). Your job is the *human* half — the
message that says "your payment didn't go through, here's the one thing to fix it." So the
sequence is short and specific:

* **On failure:** a first message, immediately. Plain subject, plain body — the charge
  didn't go through, here's a one-click link to update the card.
* **A few days later, only if it's still unpaid:** one reminder. Same ask, a touch more
  urgent, and a note about what happens if it isn't fixed (the subscription pauses).
* Spaced across the window your processor gives you before it auto-cancels the
  subscription — typically about two weeks. Two messages, at most three. Dunning is not a
  drip campaign; it's a fix-this-one-thing prompt.

The single most important rule is to **exit the instant payment clears.** The moment the
retry succeeds — or the customer updates their card — the journey must stop, cancelling any
pending reminder. Nothing corrodes trust faster than a "your payment failed" email landing
*after* the payment went through: it makes your billing look broken and your company look
asleep. So the journey watches for the payment-succeeded event and exits on it. This is
the same "exit the instant the reason is gone" discipline as win-back stopping when a user
returns — here it's just worth real dollars.

The content is a receipt, not a brochure. Dunning email is transactional in tone. State the
amount, name the card if you can ("your Visa ending 4242"), give one action (update
payment method) behind one link, and say what happens next. No product marketing, no
upsell, no "we miss you." The customer isn't lapsed — they're one click from staying. Get
out of their way.

You measure it on two numbers: recovery rate — of the charges that failed, what fraction
were paid within the window — and dollars recovered per month. Watch recovery rate over
time; if it sags, your copy or your timing has drifted. And because this journey
deliberately sends the same "update your card" message more than once, it leans harder
than any other on your tooling being *reliable* about repeat sends.

The number matters because involuntary churn is a slice of your MRR bleeding out every
single month, and a retry-plus-reminder sequence recovers most of it — from customers who
already decided to pay you. Put your own numbers in below. For most paid products this is
the single calculation that justifies the entire afternoon it takes to build the journey —
and often the whole course.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Referral (Referral)
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Your happiest customers are your cheapest, highest-converting acquisition channel — a warm
introduction from someone who already loves the product beats any ad you could buy. The
referral email is how you ask for that introduction. Almost everything about this journey
is timing and taste, because a referral ask at the wrong moment doesn't just fail — it
spends goodwill you worked to earn.

The timing rule is precise: ask at peak satisfaction, right after the customer *feels*
the value — the aha moment, a shipped result, a milestone hit, a renewal, a five-star
support interaction. That's when they'd actually recommend you to a friend,
because the good feeling is fresh. Ask on a random Tuesday and you're interrupting; ask a
beat after a great outcome and you're riding a wave they're already on.

* **The trigger is a real win, not the calendar.** Fire the journey off a *milestone* or
  success event — the same behavioural events you're already tracking — not a fixed
  "day 14" timer. The event *is* the moment of satisfaction.
* **Wait a beat, don't pounce.** Let a little time pass after the win so the ask feels like
  a natural afterglow rather than a popup bolted onto the achievement. A short delay reads
  as thoughtful; an instant ask reads as a script.

The second discipline is to ask only users who are still active. Before the send, check
that the person has actually used the product recently — a session, a login, real activity in the last few
weeks. Never ask a disengaged or churning customer to vouch for you: at best it's ignored,
at worst it reminds someone on their way out that they're on their way out. The guard is
simple — no recent activity, no ask — and it's the whole difference between a referral
program and a spray of hopeful emails.

Then make the sharing itself effortless. The email should carry a personal referral link, pre-written
share copy the customer can send as-is, and a single obvious action. Every extra step —
"log in, find the referrals tab, copy your code" — roughly halves the number of people who
follow through. The easier you make the share, the more shares you get; this is mechanical,
not motivational.

It pays to reward real referrals. The ask converts far better when there's something in it — for
the referrer, the friend, or both (double-sided rewards are the classic). But the reward
should fire on a *real* referral: a friend who actually signed up or converted, tracked on
an **identity graph** you own — the map that ties one person's many devices, emails, and
sessions to a single identity — not on a click or a good intention.

You measure it on three numbers: referral emails sent, invites generated, and — the number
that matters — invites that convert to real signups. Watch the conversion, not the send volume;
a small, well-timed program that converts beats a blast that gets ignored.

That last point — turning the ask into a *tracked, rewarded conversion on an identity graph
you own* — is the referral **loop**, and it's a whole chapter of its own later in the
course. This journey is just the ask: the well-timed tap on the shoulder that starts the
loop, and getting the timing and the audience right here is what makes the loop worth
building. (For wiring the ask itself, see [the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs).)

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Reliability: the system has to be trustworthy
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Everything in this chapter has quietly assumed one thing: that when your program decides to
send a message, it sends it **once** — not zero times, not twice. That assumption is doing
enormous work, and it's the difference between a lifecycle program people trust and one they
quietly route around.

**The failure mode that kills trust isn't a dropped email — it's a double-send.** A missed
message is invisible; nobody complains about an email they never got. A *duplicate* is
loud. Two identical welcomes make you look broken. Two "your payment failed" notices —
especially one that lands after the card already cleared — make you look alarming. A
referral ask that arrives three times makes you look desperate. Users don't file a bug;
they mark you as spam, and internally your own team stops trusting the program enough to
add to it. Reliability isn't a polish item you get to later. It's the foundation the whole
program stands on.

This is genuinely hard to get right, and worth caring that it's handled. Durable
lifecycle systems survive crashes and deploys by *replaying* a journey from the top and
fast-forwarding through the steps that already happened. That's exactly what lets a
three-day wait survive a Tuesday redeploy. But it also means that if a worker dies at the
wrong instant — *after* the email provider accepted your send but *before* the system
recorded it as done — a naïve replay re-runs the send and the user gets two. At any real
scale, every deploy becomes a small chance of embarrassing some slice of your users. The
mechanism that makes long waits reliable is the same mechanism that, done carelessly,
double-sends.

So here's what to demand from your tooling, as the growth owner:

* **Exactly-once delivery by default.** A crash, a redeploy, or a retry mid-journey must
  never turn one intended message into two. This should be the engine's job — automatic,
  with nothing extra for you to remember on the common path — not a discipline you
  hand-maintain send by send.
* **Deliberate repeat sends stay possible.** Some journeys send the *same* message twice on
  purpose — the dunning reminder is the same "update your card" template as the first
  notice. Your tooling has to tell the difference between "the same send, replayed by a
  crash" (collapse it) and "a second send I meant to happen" (deliver it). If getting that
  distinction right needs care, the tool should catch a mistake *loudly in development* —
  not silently drop a message in production.
* **A clear re-enrollment policy.** Separate from crashes, you need a deliberate answer to
  "can this customer enter this journey again?" A welcome should fire **once** — a person
  signs up once. Win-back and dunning should be **once per period** — a customer can
  genuinely lapse again, but shouldn't be re-nudged daily. A few counting journeys (a
  Discord loop that tallies every reaction, say) are **unlimited** by design. This is a
  policy decision you make per journey; the tool just has to enforce it.
* **Determinism where a choice drives the message.** If something non-deterministic picks
  *what* to send — an LLM choosing a variant, a random A/B split — a replay must not
  re-roll that choice into a different-but-duplicate message. The decision has to be
  recorded once and reused. Same for timestamps: anchor to the event's own time, not "now,"
  so a replay reads the same clock.

You get this kind of correctness *because* the journey runs inside a system purpose-built
for it. Exactly-once delivery across redeploys is brutally hard to get right by hand — it's
one of the subtlest reasons a dedicated engine beats a roll-your-own stack, and a real
weight on the build-versus-buy scale. For how the engine actually guarantees it —
the idempotency keys, the replay-safe primitives, the loud dev-time errors — see
[the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs). The point for you as the growth owner is
simpler: reliability is a requirement you get to *assume*, and a program you can't trust to
send exactly once is a program no one will let you grow.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Build vs buy
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
You have a real choice about *where* your lifecycle program lives, and a growth team should
make it deliberately rather than default into it. On one side: **rent** a hosted tool —
Customer.io, Braze, Iterable, Loops, Klaviyo — and run your journeys in someone else's UI.
On the other: **own** it — run the program on your own data and identity graph, versioned
alongside your product code. Both are legitimate. The mistake is picking without seeing
the trade.

**What renting buys you.** A hosted lifecycle tool is, for a lot of teams, the right first
move — and there's no virtue in building for its own sake:

* **A non-engineer can own it.** If a marketer or lifecycle manager runs your messaging, a
  visual tool lets them ship without a code review or a deploy. That alone is often
  decisive.
* **Time-to-first-journey is hours, not a sprint.** Templates, a drag-and-drop builder,
  deliverability, and analytics come in the box. You can have a welcome flow live this
  afternoon.
* **Someone else runs the infrastructure.** No workers, no queues, no on-call for the
  sending pipeline.
* **It's mature.** Segmentation, A/B tools, multi-channel (email/SMS/push), and reporting
  are years deep. Klaviyo on Shopify, or Customer.io for a marketing-led SaaS, will serve
  you well for a long time.

If your flows are simple drips, you need to move this week, and no engineer wants to own
lifecycle — rent. Start there with a clear conscience.

**What renting costs you — and where owning wins.** The bill for convenience comes due
along a few predictable lines, and they're exactly the ones that matter as your program
gets ambitious:

* **Your logic lives in their UI, not your repo.** The program that decides who gets which
  message becomes a set of flowcharts only reachable by logging into a vendor. No diff, no
  code review, no branch, no test, no "git blame this rule." When journeys get genuinely
  conditional — *read this user's whole event history and branch on it* — a canvas becomes
  the bottleneck that code never is.
* **Your identity graph fragments.** A hosted tool keeps its *own* picture of your users.
  Your **identity graph** — the map that ties one person's many devices, emails, and
  sessions to a single identity, the thing a real referral loop is built on — is what lets
  messaging share the same resolution as your product. A separate system holding a separate
  copy of "who is this person" is quietly working against that. Own the messaging engine and
  it reads the same identity you already trust.
* **You rent your own data back.** Behavioural data flows out to the vendor to make the
  tool work, and the interesting questions ("what's the real incremental lift of this
  journey?") get answered in their terms, on their export schedule.
* **Per-contact pricing scales against you.** Hosted tools bill by contacts or sends —
  fine early, a tax later. The pricing wall arrives right when the program is working and
  your list is growing, which is the worst possible time to be penalised for success.

**The honest summary.** Lifecycle messaging is a *durable, stateful, per-user* problem, and
both hosted tools and a purpose-built engine solve the hard parts of it — long waits, exit
conditions, exactly-once delivery — so you're not choosing on raw capability. You're
choosing on **ownership**: whose UI holds your logic, whose system holds your identity
graph, and whose pricing meters your growth. For a product-led team that already codes,
owning the layer — versioned, testable, on your own identity, with no per-contact wall — is
usually worth asking an engineer to own it. For a marketing-led team
shipping simple flows, renting is the honest right answer. Make the call with eyes open;
you can always start rented and bring it home once the program earns the investment. (For
what owning it looks like in practice, see [the Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs).)

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Feeding your journeys and proving lift
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
A journey is only as good as the events feeding it, and only as trustworthy as your ability
to prove it works. Those are the two sides of the system around every journey you ship:
what *fires* it, and how you *measure* it.

### Where the events come from

Three sources feed your triggers, and most programs use all three:

* **Your product backend.** Your own application emits the events that describe what
  customers do — signed up, activated, hit a milestone, paid, cancelled. This is the
  primary source, and because it's sent server-side from your backend it's immune to
  ad-blockers in a way client-side browser tracking never is. These are events you
  already own; the lifecycle program just listens to them.
* **External webhooks.** Systems you don't control — Stripe, a form provider, a CRM, your
  billing platform — already emit webhooks when something happens. You point those at your
  lifecycle system, map each provider's payload onto your own event names once, and from
  then on a Stripe invoice failure is just another event in the same pipeline as a product
  signup. This is how a dunning trigger arrives: the *billing system* is the source of
  truth, not a scheduled guess. ([The Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs) cover
  wiring up a webhook source.)
* **PostHog cohorts.** Some audiences aren't a single event — they're a *pattern* best
  computed in your analytics tool: "signed up but didn't activate within 7 days," "active
  last month, silent this month." PostHog already maintains these as cohorts. You let
  PostHog decide *who* qualifies and have it hand that verdict to your lifecycle system,
  which then decides *what* to do. Nothing has to poll your database looking for dormancy —
  the analytics layer that's already computing it tells you.

That last source is the clean expression of the whole course's spine — **"PostHog detects,
the lifecycle acts."** The thing that's genuinely hard to compute in real time
(30-day dormancy across every user) is a cohort PostHog maintains for free; your program
just receives the verdict and runs. Detection and action stay in the tools that are best at
each.

### Proving lift with a holdout

Shipping a journey is not the same as knowing it worked. Opens and clicks tell you a message
was *seen*; they tell you nothing about whether it *caused* anything — those users might
have converted anyway. The only honest way to know is a **holdout**:

* **Quietly withhold the messages from a small random slice of otherwise-eligible users.**
  They qualify for the journey in every way; they just don't get the emails. Everyone else
  does.
* **Keep the slice stable.** A given user should always land on the same side of the line —
  held out or messaged — not get re-rolled each time. Otherwise you can't cleanly compare
  the groups, and you'll message someone one week and hold them out the next.
* **Make the held-out group visible.** The control group has to be *observable* in your
  analytics — tagged as held-out — or you'll have nothing to compare against. A control you
  can't see isn't a control.
* **Compare outcomes, not opens.** Look at activation, retention, or revenue for the
  messaged group versus the held-out group. The *difference* is the incremental lift you
  can actually credit to the program — the number that survives scrutiny.

A worked read looks like this: messaged users convert at 18%, held-out users at 11%,
so the journey is worth **+7 points** of conversion you can attribute to it — not
correlation, cause. Use a *larger* holdout (20–50%) on low-volume journeys so the
comparison means something, decide your read date in advance so you don't stop the moment
it looks good, and hold out the *whole* experience rather than just some of the messages.

This is the growth-team experiment loop — ship, hold out a control, read the lift — running
continuously, in production, on the journey you just shipped. And it's exactly where the
answer comes from when your monthly growth review asks the only question that ultimately
matters: **what is the lifecycle program actually worth?**

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Build these in the order they pay you back, biggest leak first. There's no prize for a
clever referral loop while the bucket you're filling still pours users out the bottom — you
plug the widest holes before you open the tap. If you want a plan for the week, here it is,
in order of impact:

1. **Welcome + activation first.** The gap between signup and activation is nearly always
   the biggest leak in the funnel, so the welcome and its activation nudge are the highest-return
   emails you can ship. Build these before anything else.
2. **Dunning next, if you charge.** Failed payments are pure, recoverable churn — usually the
   fastest revenue in the whole course for the least work.
3. **Then win-back**, for users who've gone quiet — reactivating someone who already knew the
   value is far cheaper than acquiring a stranger.
4. **Put a holdout on that first journey** and read the lift — so when someone asks whether the
   program works, there's a number instead of a feeling.

And hold the *system* in mind from the second journey on: a frequency cap, a priority order, and
one global holdout are what keep a growing pile of emails helpful instead of overwhelming.

You've now plugged the bucket. The rest of the course opens the tap — and makes sure every drop
lands in an audience you own.

## Go deeper

* [Postmark — transactional email best practices](https://postmarkapp.com/guides/transactional-email-best-practices) — the one-job-one-CTA discipline every lifecycle email should copy.
* [Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs) — how to actually build the journeys and templates from this chapter, when you're ready to implement.
* [Hogsend — email templates by lifecycle stage](https://hogsend.com/emails) — the same AARRR catalog this chapter teaches, as ready-made components.
* [Reforge — retention & engagement](https://www.reforge.com/blog) — the strategy behind the retention and reactivation emails, in depth.
* [Lenny's Newsletter](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/) — operator essays on onboarding, conversion, and lifecycle across every stage.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Free to paid (Conversion)
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Conversion emails move an activated free user to a paying one. The word that matters there
is *activated*. The most common mistake in the whole funnel is asking someone to pay before
they've felt the value — a trial user who never did the core thing, hit with a "your card
on file?" email, just unsubscribes. Conversion is **earned by activation**; an upgrade
email sent to someone who hasn't reached the aha moment is pushing on a door that was
never opened.

Once they *have* felt the value, three emails do most of the work:

* **The value recap.** Not a feature list — a mirror. Show them what they've already gotten
  out of the product (the reports they ran, the teammates they invited, the hours they saved)
  and, right beside it, what's behind the wall. The recap works because it's anchored in
  *their* usage, so it reads as "here's what you'd lose," which lands harder than "here's what
  you'd gain" — [loss aversion](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/loss-aversion/) is one of the
  more well-attested findings in behavioural work.
* **The trial-ending sequence.** A short run — a few days before, the day it ends, and a
  short grace window after — tied to *real* urgency, not a fake countdown. Manufactured
  scarcity ("only 2 hours left!" that resets on refresh) converts a few extra people once and
  costs you trust with everyone who notices. Honest urgency ("your trial ends Friday and here's
  exactly what changes") converts about as well and doesn't spend credibility.
* **The upgrade nudge, triggered by a wall.** The highest-intent conversion email is the one
  the user summons themselves by bumping into a limit — inviting a fourth teammate on a
  three-seat plan, hitting the export cap, reaching for a paid-only feature. That moment is
  peak willingness to pay, because the need is concrete and immediate. A journey that waits for
  that signal and sends one clean "unlock it" email beats any calendar-based drip.

The tactics under all three are the same as every lifecycle email: fire on the *right signal*
(a limit hit, a power action, a real trial deadline — not a fixed day), personalise on their
actual usage, keep it to one job and one CTA (*upgrade*), and prove it with a holdout so you
know the emails caused the conversions rather than riding on people who'd have paid anyway.

Freemium free-to-paid conversion commonly lands in the low single digits — often quoted around
2–5% *(directional; it varies enormously by product and how "free" is drawn)* — so small,
honest improvements here compound against a large base.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: Asking for feedback (Feedback)
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
