A feedback email asks the user what they think — and a good one is timed, short, and *wired
to do something with the answer*. Most teams get the first two and drop the third, which is
where the value was.

**Time it to a real success, not the calendar.** Ask right after the user felt something go
well — they hit the activation moment, completed a purchase, got a support issue resolved.
Asking a frustrated or idle user "how are we doing?" measures your worst moments and annoys
people; asking just after a win gets a higher response rate and a truer read. The trigger is
an event, like every other lifecycle email.

**Keep it to one question.** The classic is the [Net Promoter Score](https://hbr.org/2003/12/the-one-number-you-need-to-grow),
or NPS ("how likely are you to recommend us, 0–10?"), but a single open question — "what nearly
stopped you signing up?" — often teaches you more. Either way, one question in the email
itself, answerable in a tap, beats a link to a ten-field form nobody finishes. Treat the NPS
*number* as directional, not gospel — it's a widely used but [heavily critiqued](https://www.medallia.com/blog/net-promoter-score-nps-criticism/)
metric; the free-text *reasons* are where the signal is.

**Close the loop — and route the answer.** This is the part that turns a survey into a
lifecycle system:

* A **detractor** (a user who scored you low) should trigger a save flow — a real human
  reply, or a "what went wrong?" journey — not vanish into a dashboard.
* A **promoter** (a user who scored you high) is a warm referral ask or a review request,
  timed to the moment they just told you they love it.
* Every answer should feed *back into the product* — the reasons people give are your roadmap,
  and the patterns are the next thing to fix in onboarding or pricing.

So the feedback email isn't the end of a journey; it's a fork that starts the next one.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: How they come together
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
Every email so far has been good in isolation. But you won't run one — you'll run a dozen, all
firing off different events, and the same user is eligible for several at once. A new signup
who fails a payment is, on paper, in the welcome journey, the activation nudge, *and* dunning.
Without coordination, they get three emails in an afternoon and unsubscribe from all of it. The
difference between a lifecycle *system* and a pile of journeys is four rules.

The first and most important guardrail is a global frequency cap — a hard limit on how many
lifecycle emails one person can receive in a day or a week, enforced *across every journey* so
no combination can pile on. When journeys overlap, the cap decides who waits. It's the thing
that protects the user from your enthusiasm, and it protects your deliverability from the spam
complaints that overwhelmed users generate.

Second comes a priority order. When two emails could fire at the same moment, one has to win,
and the rule of thumb is simple: **money and trust beat promotion.** A failed-payment (dunning) email or a security
notice outranks a feature nudge; a transactional receipt always sends. Decide the order once,
up front, so the cap has something to sort by instead of dropping messages at random.

Third, keep a stage map. Lay every email you send against the funnel — Activation, Conversion,
Retention, Reactivation, Revenue, Referral, Feedback — and the gaps jump out. Most teams
discover they have three activation emails and nothing for reactivation, or a slick referral
ask and no win-back. The map is a five-minute coverage audit that tells you the next journey
worth building.

Finally, one global holdout. Individual holdouts prove one email's lift; a *program* holdout proves
the whole system's worth. Quietly hold a small random slice of users out of **all** lifecycle
email, and compare their retention and revenue to everyone else. That gap is what the lifecycle
program is actually worth — the number your monthly growth review asks for, and the one that
justifies the whole effort to a skeptical cofounder when you have to defend the program out
loud. Keep it running permanently; it's cheap insurance against a program that's quietly
stopped earning its keep.

Build the emails one at a time, in order of impact — but hold the *system* in mind from the
first one, because the second journey is where the coordination starts to matter.

growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.title: 6 — Your lifecycle playbook
growth-with-posthog/06-building-lifecycl.text:
> **In this chapter:** the lifecycle program made concrete. The lifecycle-strategy
> chapter argued *which* journeys to build and why; this chapter is the playbook for each
> one — what the email
> is, which leak in the funnel it plugs, the trigger, the timing, the content, and how you
> measure it — walked stage by stage across the funnel (Activation → Conversion →
> Reactivation → Revenue → Referral → Feedback), then pulled together into a coordinated
> system. This is strategy and tactics, not code — when you're ready to build it, the
> [Hogsend docs](https://hogsend.com/docs) cover the how.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into fifteen short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. One piece (What makes a good lifecycle email) carries three short videos
on the craft of building email that renders and delivers. Work through them in order, or
jump to the stage you're on:

1. [The lifecycle loop](./01-the-loop.mdx) — an event happens, the right message goes out, you measure whether it worked.
2. [Anatomy of a lifecycle message](./02-anatomy-of-a-journey.mdx) — the six decisions behind every email: trigger, audience, timing, content, exit, measurement.
3. [What makes a good lifecycle email](./03-templates-email-as-code.mdx) — deliverability, receipt-not-brochure, and the case for owning your emails.
4. [Welcome + onboarding (Activation)](./04-welcome-onboarding.mdx) — the highest-leverage pair: welcome now, nudge if they haven't reached value.
5. [A behavioural nudge series (Activation)](./05-behavioural-nudge-series.mdx) — wait, check, and send *only if the action didn't happen*.
6. [Free to paid (Conversion)](./13-conversion-emails.mdx) — the upgrade emails, earned by activation and triggered by hitting a wall.
7. [Win-back (Reactivation)](./06-win-back.mdx) — re-engage the dormant with a real reason, one window, then stop.
8. [Dunning (Revenue)](./07-dunning.mdx) — recover a failed payment, the highest-ROI email you'll send.
9. [Referral (Referral)](./08-referral.mdx) — ask at peak satisfaction, and only ask still-active users.
10. [Asking for feedback (Feedback)](./14-feedback-and-nps.mdx) — the survey that triggers the next journey instead of vanishing into a dashboard.
11. [Reliability](./09-exactly-once.mdx) — why a lifecycle system that double-sends or drops messages destroys trust fast.
12. [Build vs buy](./10-other-ways.mdx) — own your lifecycle system or rent one, and when each is right.
13. [Feeding your journeys and proving lift](./11-triggers-and-holdout.mdx) — where the events come from, and the holdout that measures your impact.
14. [How they come together](./15-how-they-come-together.mdx) — frequency caps, a priority order, and the program holdout that make it a system.
15. [Before you move on](./12-before-you-move-on.mdx) — the build order for the week, then a quiz to check yourself.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter.**

* **Journey** — an automated, multi-step message sequence that carries one user through a stretch of their lifecycle: send, wait, check, maybe send again — one person at a time, not a list-wide blast.
* **Trigger** — the single event that starts a journey: a signup, a failed payment, two weeks of silence.
* **Dunning** — the sequence that recovers a *failed payment* (an expired card, a declined charge) before the subscription lapses.
* **Win-back** — a journey that re-engages a user who's gone *dormant*: slipped from active to silent without ever formally cancelling.
* **Holdout** — a small random slice of otherwise-eligible users you deliberately *don't* email, kept as a control so you can measure what the messages actually caused.
* **Cohort** — a group of users defined by a shared trait or moment: everyone who signed up last week, everyone active last month but silent this one.
* **Identity graph** — the map that ties one person's many devices, emails, and sessions to a single identity, so the same human is recognised everywhere.
* **AARRR** — the funnel framework Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue, sometimes called *pirate metrics*.
* **MRR** — monthly recurring revenue: the predictable subscription income each customer adds every month, and loses you when they churn.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: Where are you starting from?
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
Where your users come from today decides which half of this chapter is urgent. A team
growing almost entirely through word of mouth and launches is really asking one question:
is paid worth *starting* at all? A team already spending heavily on ads is asking a
different one: where is that spend leaking, and how much of the audience am I renting
instead of owning? Same tactics, opposite priorities.

The one thing true for both is that paid acquisition only compounds once the product
already keeps the users it wins. Pour traffic into something that can't retain and you're
just *buying churn* — the acquired users leak straight back out, and the ad spend hides
the real problem instead of solving it. So before any of the tactics, it's worth being
honest about your current mix, because that's what tells you whether this chapter is about
starting paid carefully or measuring the paid you already run.

Two facts frame everything that follows:

* **Channels decay.** Andrew Chen's
  [law of shitty clickthroughs](https://andrewchen.com/the-law-of-shitty-clickthroughs/)
  is the empirical observation that every marketing channel's performance degrades as
  it saturates — banner ads went from a 78% click-through rate (CTR — the share of people
  who saw one and clicked) in 1994 to ~0.05%, and every channel since has followed the
  same curve. Whatever works for you now will work less well per dollar next year. The
  counter isn't channel-hopping; it's capturing what a channel brings you into something
  you own, while the channel still works.
* **Early traction rarely comes from paid at all.** Paul Graham's
  [Do Things that Don't Scale](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html) is the canonical
  argument: almost all startups get their first users manually — recruiting one at a
  time, launching in communities, doing unscalable favours — and the founders who wait
  for ads to do it stall. If you're pre-revenue, most of this chapter's job is to stop
  you spending money you shouldn't spend yet.

Both facts point the same way. A channel is a lease, not a deed — so the real game isn't
finding the perfect one, it's converting whatever any channel rents you into an audience
you own outright. The next piece is the menu of channels themselves, and why, for a lot
of readers here, paid social isn't the first one.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The channel menu — and how to choose
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
The right channel is the one that fits how your product is actually discovered, which is
rarely the channel with the loudest marketing around it. Meta (Facebook and Instagram) is
this chapter's worked example — chosen because it's the most common paid channel and its
tracking story teaches the privacy lessons every channel now shares — but for many readers
here it's the wrong place to start. The paid options each answer a different kind of
demand:

* **Google Ads (Search).** Intent-based — you reach people *already searching* for the
  solution. For products with clear search demand (someone Googling "invoice software"),
  this often converts far better than interrupting a feed. It has its own server-side
  conversion path (Enhanced Conversions, the Google Ads API).
* **TikTok / Reddit / X ads.** TikTok for consumer and younger demographics; Reddit for
  niche and technical communities (it can work surprisingly well for dev tools in the
  right subreddits); X for tech and founder audiences. Each has its own pixel plus a
  server-side events API on the same deduplication logic the Pixel-and-CAPI pieces cover
  later.
* **LinkedIn Ads.** Expensive per click, but the only platform with reliable job-title
  and company targeting — the B2B choice when your buyer is defined by role.

For this course's audience — developer tools, B2B, community and web3 products — the
channels that most often beat paid are the ones you own outright:

* **Content / SEO** — compounding, owned, and the canonical growth engine for dev
  tools and B2B; it's *why* Supabase, PostHog itself, and others grew. Slow to start,
  nearly free to sustain. Rob Walling's rule of thumb for bootstrapped SaaS is that
  content is a 6–12-month investment before it pays — budget the patience, not just the
  writing.
* **Launches** — [Show HN](https://news.ycombinator.com/showhn.html) (whose actual rules
  are worth reading first: no marketing-speak, the thing must be tryable), Product Hunt,
  and the subreddit/newsletter/Discord ecosystem of your niche. Launches are spikes, not
  engines — their real value is seeding the loop, because every launch visitor you capture
  into a channel you own keeps paying after the spike dies.
* **Community** — Discord, Reddit and Slack communities, and the subject of the
  owned-audience chapter that follows. For community-led and web3 products this *is* the
  acquisition channel.
* **Partnerships / integrations / marketplaces** — listing in the ecosystem your users
  already live in (a Shopify app, a Vercel integration, a Raycast extension). You inherit
  the platform's traffic and its trust.

**What to take from it:**

* Content marketing fails without a plan for *what to write first* — Walling's own early
  spend flopped because every piece was generic top-of-funnel with no sequencing behind it.
* Start at the bottom of the funnel: comparison and alternative-to pages, case studies,
  and feature docs close sales *and* work their way up to the broader terms over time.
* Match each piece to search intent, because commercial and transactional keywords convert
  while purely informational ones mostly build awareness.

### A note for dev-tool, community, and web3 founders

For developer tools and B2B, paid social is weak — content, community, and product-led
growth usually out-perform it. Developers install ad blockers at the highest rate of any
audience, distrust interruption marketing, and discover tools through
[GitHub, Hacker News, docs, and each other](https://www.heavybit.com/library/article/developer-marketing-guide/)
rather than feeds. Heavybit's
[developer-first go-to-market material](https://www.heavybit.com/devguild/developer-first-go-to-market)
and First Round's
[Retool case study](https://review.firstround.com/retools-path-to-product-market-fit-lessons-for-getting-to-100-happy-customers-faster/)
both land on the same shape: bottom-up, user-to-user distribution, seeded by content and
community, with paid as (at most) an amplifier.

**What to take from it:**

* Developers judge a tool by its docs and free tier, not its marketing pages — for a
  developer tool, the product itself *is* the marketing.
* Trust compounds slowly: devs are late-adopter sceptics who often need around ten touches
  before they try something, so repeated useful exposure beats one big push.
* The loop that works is to talk to developers, fix what frustrates them fast, and — the
  step most teams skip — *tell them* you fixed it.

Web3 and crypto ads carry the same weakness for a different reason: both Meta and Google
restrict them by policy. For dev tools, communities and crypto alike, the honest
conclusion is the same — treat paid as *fuel for the loop, not the loop itself*. The
accuracy work in the rest of this chapter still matters, but it applies to whatever small
paid spend you run while your real leverage compounds in the audience you own. So if
you're a dev tool, read what follows as *how paid works and how to measure it honestly* —
not as marching orders to go buy ads.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The maths that decides: CAC, LTV, payback
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
Whatever the channel, the same three numbers decide whether it's working. The
definitions matter — a16z's
[16 Startup Metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/) exists because founders
routinely get them wrong in ways that flatter the business:

* **CAC (customer acquisition cost)** — *fully loaded* spend to acquire one paying
  customer: ad spend plus the content, tools, and people behind it, divided by
  customers acquired. The classic self-deception is reporting CAC on ad spend alone,
  or on signups instead of customers.
* **LTV (lifetime value)** — the **gross-margin** contribution of a customer over their
  lifetime, where lifetime comes from your churn rate (roughly, monthly margin ÷
  monthly churn). Computing LTV on revenue instead of margin is the second classic
  self-deception — a16z calls both out by name.
* **Payback period** — months of gross margin to recover CAC. For a cash-constrained
  startup this is often the number that matters most: an LTV:CAC of 5 is cold comfort
  if payback takes three years and the bank account empties in one.

The folk benchmark you'll hear is **LTV:CAC ≥ 3 and payback under ~12 months** for
SaaS — better treated as a sanity range than a law; what actually protects you is
knowing your numbers and their trend. Two habits follow from that:

* **CAC is most useful per channel, not blended.** A blended CAC averages your free
  organic users into the paid number, which can hide a channel that's quietly
  unprofitable. Blended CAC is a board metric; per-channel CAC is the operating one.
  Watching the blended figure fall over time is a useful signal in its own right — it
  usually means owned and referral growth is pulling your average cost down.
* **LTV is worth segmenting by cohort and source.** A **cohort** here is a group of
  customers bucketed by when they signed up or where they came from, and users from
  different sources retain differently — paid-social users commonly churn faster than
  organic, having arrived with less intent. If paid cohorts' LTV is half your organic
  LTV, your real paid LTV:CAC may be under water while the blended number looks fine.

**What to take from it:**

* LTV falls out of ARPA and churn: average revenue per account ÷ churn rate, taken down
  to margin — a $100 ARPA at 10% monthly churn is a $1,000 lifetime value, the ceiling on
  what you can spend to acquire that customer.
* Track **gross** revenue retention (churn and contraction only), not just the flashier
  net revenue retention, which can exceed 100% on expansion and quietly hide the bad news
  gross retention would show you.
* MRR (monthly recurring revenue) is not cash flow — a $1,200 annual deal is $100 of MRR —
  and segmenting churn and CAC by customer size or source tells you far more than any
  blended number.

Where do the numbers come from? The machine you already built: revenue events captured
server-side in PostHog (the instrumentation chapter sets that up), and source plus UTM
tags — the `utm_source`/`utm_medium` parameters on a campaign link — landed as person
properties (the PostHog-feeding piece later shows how). So "LTV by acquisition source" is
a PostHog insight, not a spreadsheet safari.

Benchmarks are worth less than your own numbers, so put them in. Watch the payback figure
most when cash is tight — a healthy LTV:CAC with a payback longer than your runway still
sinks you.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The shape of a Meta campaign
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
A Meta campaign has a fixed three-tier shape — campaign, ad set, ad — and it's worth
getting comfortable with it first, because every other decision hangs off which tier it
lives on:

* **Campaign** — sets the **objective** and budget strategy. One objective per campaign.
* **Ad Set** — the levers: **audience, placements, the optimisation event, budget,
  schedule**.
* **Ad** — the creative the user sees (image/video, copy, CTA, destination).

The single most consequential choice is the **campaign objective**, because it tells
Meta's delivery algorithm *which kind of person to go find*. Meta consolidated its old
objectives into six, under a scheme it calls ODAX (Outcome-Driven Ad Experiences):

1. **Awareness** — reach/recall, top of funnel.
2. **Traffic** — cheap clicks to a page. *Not* optimised for conversions — a common
   beginner trap is to use Traffic and wonder why clicks don't convert. Traffic buys you
   visitors who clicked; Sales buys you visitors who *act*. They are different people.
3. **Engagement** — interactions, video views, messages.
4. **Leads** — form fills, newsletter signups.
5. **App Promotion** — installs and in-app events.
6. **Sales** — purchases / highest-intent conversions.

**The rule of thumb worth carrying:** optimise for the event closest to revenue that
you can feed Meta *enough of*. The algorithm needs roughly **50 conversions per ad set
per week** to exit its "learning phase" — the early period where it's still working out
who to deliver to — and deliver efficiently. If you can't generate 50 purchases a week
yet, a higher-funnel event (lead or signup) as the optimisation target, with the deeper
conversion passed back server-side (the Conversions API piece covers this), keeps the
algorithm fed. The most common early pattern goes the other way: optimising for
`purchase` at five a week starves the algorithm of signal, and it never learns who to
find.

Two practical corollaries of the learning phase:

* **Edits reset learning.** Every significant change (budget, audience, creative) can
  send the ad set back to school. The pattern that hurts — check daily, worry, edit —
  keeps it permanently in learning and permanently expensive. Weekly changes give the
  system time to settle; the operating-cadence chapter later turns that into a habit.
* **Fewer, bigger ad sets learn faster.** Five ad sets splitting 10 conversions a week
  will all starve; one ad set receiving all 50 can learn. Consolidation is the modern
  default.

Get the shape and the objective right and the algorithm does the heavy lifting; get
them wrong and no amount of creative saves you. The next piece is who the ads go to —
the three audiences, and the order to earn them.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The three audiences
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
Every ad set targets one of three kinds of audience:

* **Broad / interest ("cold").** Demographics and interests, or — increasingly — fully
  broad "Advantage+" audiences where you let Meta's machine learning find people. The
  2024–2026 trend is clear: **broad targeting + strong creative + a good conversion signal
  now beats hand-built interest stacks**, because the algorithm does the targeting.
  Interest targeting is more of a hint than a filter now — which shifts the work from
  audience-picking to *creative* and *signal quality* (the tracking pieces later in this
  chapter are all about that signal).
* **Custom Audiences ("warm", first-party).** Built from *your* data: a customer-list
  upload, site/app visitors, or events sent via the Conversions API. These are the
  highest-signal inputs you can give Meta — and they only exist because you captured
  first-party identity, which is what the owned-audience chapter that follows is built on.
* **Lookalike Audiences ("prospecting").** Meta finds new people resembling a **seed**
  custom audience. **Seed quality dominates the result** — your *best* customers
  (recent purchasers, high-LTV) make a far better seed than "everyone who ever signed
  up." A 1% lookalike is the tightest match; widening to 3–10% trades match for scale.

### The full funnel

A standard structure layers these:

* **Top (cold):** Awareness/Traffic/Video to broad audiences. Cheap short video both
  earns reach *and* manufactures the video-viewer/engager audiences you'll retarget.
* **Middle (warm):** retarget site visitors, video viewers, engagers with
  trust/education content.
* **Bottom (hot):** retarget pricing-page visitors, trial-starters, cart abandoners
  with a direct offer.

> A starting split you'll see quoted is ~50–60% cold / 20–30% warm / 15–20% hot
> *(directional heuristic, not gospel)*. The pattern that catches small founders is
> over-investing in retargeting too early — a tiny warm pool can't scale spend. The
> warm pool grows only as you capture more first-party identity of your own.

And a word on the part this course can't do for you: **creative is now the targeting.**
With broad audiences, the ad itself selects the audience — a video about "deploy
previews for your Next.js app" finds developers because only developers watch it. The
practical implication: you'll likely need more creative variants than feels natural
(winners fatigue in weeks), and the first three seconds are where the qualification
happens.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: Your conversion tracking is lying to you
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
The **Meta Pixel** is browser-side JavaScript — a snippet that fires from the user's
browser and depends on cookies and the request actually completing — and that single fact
is why the conversion numbers it reports are always an under-count. A stack of things now
breaks it:

* **iOS App Tracking Transparency (ATT)** — Apple's prompt that lets users refuse
  cross-app tracking, and most iOS users decline it *(opt-in is commonly cited around
  20–25%, though it varies a lot by source and region — directional)*.
* **Safari / Firefox / Brave** cap or expire the cookies the Pixel relies on.
* **Ad blockers** drop the Pixel outright for ~10–30% of users — the same blocking that
  already eats into your client-side analytics.
* **Third-party cookie deprecation** — the date keeps slipping, but the direction is
  certain.

The result: the Pixel under-counts conversions, Meta's algorithm optimises on partial
data, and your reported ROAS (return on ad spend — revenue divided by what you spent to
get it) is wrong. You can't fix bid strategy on numbers that are quietly missing a third
of reality. It's the same lesson as client-side analytics loss, now with money attached:
browser-only measurement under-counts, and the fix is to move the important events
server-side.

The knock-on effect is worse than bad reporting: **the algorithm optimises on what it
sees.** If the Pixel disproportionately loses conversions from privacy-conscious,
ad-blocking users (it does), Meta learns to find *fewer people like them* — a silent
bias in delivery, not just a counting error. Signal quality is targeting quality.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: The fix: Pixel and Conversions API
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
The **Conversions API (CAPI)** sends events **server-side** — from your server (or a tool
such as a CDP, a customer data platform that collects and routes your events) directly to
Meta's endpoint, skipping the browser entirely. Because it never touches the user's
browser, it survives ad blockers, cookie expiry, and failed Pixel loads.

Meta's own guidance is not "CAPI instead of Pixel" — it's **both, deduplicated.** You
send the same event from the browser (Pixel) and the server (CAPI), and Meta merges them.
Redundancy plus better matching.

Server-side genuinely fixes three things. It **recovers web conversions** lost to ad
blockers, to Safari's Intelligent Tracking Prevention (ITP — its system for capping and
expiring tracking cookies), and to failed Pixel loads. It raises **match quality**,
because your server holds the user's hashed email or phone that the browser often doesn't.
And it lets you send **back-office conversions the browser never sees** — trial→paid,
qualified-lead, refunds, LTV, offline events. That last one is the real prize: these are
usually your *most valuable* conversions, and no pixel can see a single one of them.

It's worth being precise about what server-side does *not* fix, because this is where a
lot of advice overpromises. **It is not a consent loophole** — you still need a lawful
basis to collect, hash and share user data (the consent piece is next), and a
SHA-256-hashed email is **pseudonymous, not anonymous**: same input, same hash,
re-identifiable. **It does not restore iOS *app-install* attribution** — that's governed
by Apple's SKAdNetwork plus Meta's Aggregated Event Measurement, which is aggregated,
delayed and modelled, *not* user-level, and web CAPI doesn't bring per-user iOS
attribution back. **It doesn't fix bad inputs** — CAPI with no email, phone or
external\_id matches poorly and barely helps; garbage in, garbage matched. And it doesn't
dedupe by magic, which is the next thing to get right.

> ⚠️ **A dated claim you'll still run into: the AEM "8 prioritised events" limit.**
> Meta removed the 8-event cap and manual configuration around **June 2025** and now
> [auto-aggregates eligible events](https://www.conversios.io/blog/meta-aggregated-event-measurement/).
> Older blogs and courses still teach the cap — if a guide mentions it, that's a good
> sign the rest is stale too.

**Deduplication is the thing people get wrong.** When an event goes out from both Pixel
and CAPI, both copies need the **same `event_id`** (and a matching `event_name`) — Meta
dedupes on `event_id` and counts it once. Without it, a hybrid setup **double-counts**
conversions, corrupting the very ROAS it was built to measure. It's the single most
common CAPI mistake, and it stays silent until you notice the numbers look too good.

The measure of how well all this lands is **Event Match Quality (EMQ)** — Meta's 0–10
score for how well each event source's data matches real Meta users. Aim for **6 or
higher** (8+ is better). The score climbs with more and better identifiers, roughly
weighted:

* **Strongest:** email, the click ID (`fbc`, from the `fbclid` on the landing URL),
  Facebook Login ID.
* **Medium:** phone, the browser ID (`fbp`), and your stable **`external_id`** (your
  user/contact id).
* **Weaker:** name, city, IP, user agent.

All PII (personally identifiable information — email, phone, name) must be normalised
(trim, lowercase) and **SHA-256 hashed** before sending. The way to raise EMQ is to
**feed CAPI from where you actually have identity** — your backend or CRM, which holds
the email and phone the browser never sees. Which is exactly where PostHog and Hogsend
come in.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: Feeding it from PostHog (and Hogsend)
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
You don't have to hand-build a CAPI (Conversions API) integration. PostHog's **Data
Pipelines** include a **Meta Ads conversions destination**:

* You give it your **Pixel/Dataset ID and access token**. PostHog **SHA-256-hashes**
  the email/name, and sends `event_name`, a unique `event_id` (enabling Pixel dedup),
  `event_time`, plus `fbc`/`fbclid` and user agent.
* Use the **same Pixel ID** for the browser Pixel and the PostHog destination so
  deduplication works.
* Filter the destination to **conversion events only** — you don't want every pageview
  going to Meta.

PostHog also auto-captures the ad-click IDs — `fbclid` (Meta's) and `gclid` (Google's
equivalent) — and UTM tags — the `utm_source`/`utm_medium`
parameters on a campaign link — as person properties, and stitches anonymous → identified
when the user gives an email, so a later server-side conversion carries the identifiers
Meta needs for a high EMQ (Event Match Quality — Meta's 0–10 match score).

**Custom audiences without CSVs:** build a PostHog **cohort** (a saved group of people who
share a behaviour, e.g. "closed-won in the last 180 days") and sync it as a Meta custom
audience — for retargeting, or as a lookalike seed. No manual list upload, and it stays
fresh.

**Where Hogsend fits:** Hogsend's events are server-side and keyed to its own identity
graph (email ↔ Discord ↔ Telegram ↔ anonymous). That means Hogsend can fire **enriched,
deep-funnel conversions** — trial→paid, qualified-lead — **server-side, with strong
identity**, which is exactly the high-EMQ, browser-invisible conversion that makes CAPI
worth it. The honest caveat: this makes your *conversions* accurate and your *journey
triggers* ad-blocker-proof, but it does **not** fix the user's client-side PostHog
analytics loss, and it does not override consent or Apple's App Tracking Transparency
(ATT).

### Other ways to send server-side conversions

PostHog's destination is the path this course recommends because you're already in
PostHog, but it's not the only one — the field is worth knowing:

* **Stape / server-side Google Tag Manager** — a popular dedicated server container that
  forwards to Meta, Google, TikTok, etc. More control, more setup.
* **Segment / RudderStack** — if you already run a CDP (customer data platform), it has
  Meta/Google destinations.
* **Direct CAPI integration** — call Meta's endpoint from your backend yourself. Most
  control, most maintenance; only worth it if the managed options can't express what you
  need.

The principle is the same regardless of tool: **send from where you have identity,
dedupe on `event_id`, send only consented conversion events.**

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.title: Consent — the part that isn't optional
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/0.text:
Server-side tracking is not a way around consent, and over 2025 the law moved against the
loophole rather than towards it. Since **January 2025**, Meta itself requires **explicit
user consent** before you upload contact information for a custom audience or send PII
(personally identifiable information — an email, a phone number) through CAPI. The server
is a different pipe, not a different rulebook.

The obligations sit with you, not Meta. When you upload a list you are the **data
controller** — [Meta's own custom-audience terms](https://www.facebook.com/legal/terms/customaudience)
say so explicitly — which means you need a lawful basis, usually consent. A German
regulator has already ruled that list uploads without consent are unlawful, and hashing
doesn't rescue you: a SHA-256-hashed email is pseudonymous, not anonymous, so in law it
is still personal data.

Meta gives you the controls to do this properly — consent signals and data-processing
options exist for exactly this purpose — and in consent regimes the safe default is
simply not to send events for users who haven't opted in. Less is safer as a rule: send
only what you need (a hashed email, phone or external\_id) and you stay clear of the
sharpest edges. As of **September 2025**, Meta blocks custom conversions that carry
sensitive health or financial data outright.

The strategic point underneath all of it: an **owned, consented identity graph** — emails
captured *with opt-in* at signup and verification — is what makes the whole custom-
audience, CAPI and lookalike machine both *effective* and *compliant*. You can only feed
Meta good, legal data if you collected it well in the first place. That is what the next
chapter is about.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/1.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic/1.text:
A few things worth doing while this chapter is fresh:

1. **Settle whether paid social is even your channel.** For a dev tool or community
   product the honest answer is usually *mostly no* — in which case the next chapter, on
   owned audiences, is where your energy goes, with paid kept to small tests.
2. **Write down your per-channel CAC and payback** for whatever you're doing today, even
   roughly. A new channel can only be judged against a baseline, and most teams discover
   they don't have one.
3. **If Meta is your channel, get the dedup working.** The **Meta Pixel** on your site is
   step one; the **PostHog → Meta Ads** destination with the **same Pixel ID**, filtered
   to conversion events, is step two — together they're the deduplication working for you.
4. **Check your conversions carry email, `fbc`, and `external_id`.** That's what lifts EMQ
   (Meta's 0–10 match-quality score) to 6 or higher, and match quality is targeting
   quality.
5. **Start with one campaign, not five:** a Sales or Leads objective, a broad audience,
   strong creative, optimising on the deepest event you can feed ~50 of a week.
6. **Take opt-in consent at the moment you collect the email.** That's what makes the
   whole audience machine this chapter described legal to run.

With traffic flowing and honestly measured, the last piece is the one that compounds:
turning that traffic into an audience you never have to pay to reach again.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [a16z — 16 Startup Metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/) — the canonical CAC/LTV definitions, including the exact ways founders fool themselves.
* [Andrew Chen — The Law of Shitty Clickthroughs](https://andrewchen.com/the-law-of-shitty-clickthroughs/) — why every channel decays, and what that implies for owning your audience.
* [Paul Graham — Do Things that Don't Scale](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html) — the case that early traction is manual, not paid.
* [Heavybit — Developer Marketing Guide](https://www.heavybit.com/library/article/developer-marketing-guide/) — where developers actually discover tools, from the fund that specialises in them.
* [Heavybit — Developer-First Go-to-Market](https://www.heavybit.com/devguild/developer-first-go-to-market) — bottom-up distribution mechanics for dev tools.
* [First Round — Retool's Path to Product-Market Fit](https://review.firstround.com/retools-path-to-product-market-fit-lessons-for-getting-to-100-happy-customers-faster/) — a real dev-tool's first-100-customers playbook, no paid ads in sight.
* [First Round Review — Go-to-Market hub](https://review.firstround.com/articles/go-to-market/) — a library of channel and positioning essays worth raiding.
* [Show HN — the official rules](https://news.ycombinator.com/showhn.html) — read before launching; the norms are the strategy.
* [Meta — Conversions API docs](https://developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/conversions-api/) — the primary source for the Pixel-and-CAPI mechanics.
* [PostHog — Meta Ads destination](https://posthog.com/docs/cdp/destinations/meta-ads) — the managed CAPI path this course recommends.

growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic.title: 7 — Driving traffic
growth-with-posthog/07-driving-traffic.text:
> **In this chapter:** how to choose an acquisition channel deliberately (and why paid
> social is the wrong first channel for many readers of this course), the CAC/LTV maths
> that decides whether a channel is working, the structure of a Meta campaign, the three
> audience types, why Pixel-and-Conversions-API (not Pixel alone), what server-side
> tracking does and doesn't fix, and how PostHog/Hogsend feed conversions and audiences
> back to the ad platform. Accuracy matters here — the privacy landscape is full of
> half-truths.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into ten short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Where a piece has a video, the idea is the same each time: read the
one-line reason it's worth your time, watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then
read the short digest so the point sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you
need:

1. [Where are you starting from?](./01-where-you-start.mdx) — your acquisition mix changes how the rest reads; and channels decay.
2. [The channel menu](./02-channel-menu.mdx) — pick the channel that fits your product, and why dev tools skip paid social.
3. [The maths: CAC, LTV, payback](./03-cac-ltv-payback.mdx) — the three numbers that decide whether a channel works.
4. [The shape of a Meta campaign](./04-meta-campaign-shape.mdx) — campaign/ad-set/ad, and the objective that matters most.
5. [The three audiences](./05-three-audiences.mdx) — cold, warm, and lookalike, and why seed quality wins.
6. [Your conversion tracking is lying](./06-tracking-is-lying.mdx) — why the browser Pixel under-counts.
7. [The fix: Pixel *and* Conversions API](./07-pixel-and-capi.mdx) — server-side events, deduplicated, at high match quality.
8. [Feeding it from PostHog](./08-feeding-from-posthog.mdx) — the managed CAPI path, and where Hogsend fits.
9. [Consent](./09-consent.mdx) — the part that isn't optional.
10. [Before you move on](./10-before-you-move-on.mdx) — a short checklist while it's fresh, then a quiz to check yourself.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter.** This is an acronym-dense chapter — every term is
also defined the moment it first appears, but here's the landing list:

* **CAC** (customer-acquisition cost) — the fully-loaded spend to win one paying customer.
* **LTV** (lifetime value) — the gross-margin a customer contributes over their whole lifetime.
* **Payback period** — how many months of margin it takes to earn back a customer's CAC.
* **MRR** (monthly recurring revenue) — subscription revenue normalised to a monthly figure.
* **ARPA** (average revenue per account) — revenue divided by the number of accounts.
* **CTR** (click-through rate) — the share of people who see something and click it.
* **ROAS** (return on ad spend) — revenue earned for every pound spent on ads.
* **UTM** — the `utm_source`/`utm_medium` tags on a campaign link that record where a visitor came from.
* **Cohort** — a saved group of people who share a behaviour ("signed up last week", "closed-won in 180 days").
* **Lookalike** — an audience Meta builds by finding new people who resemble a seed list of yours.
* **Pixel** — Meta's browser-side tracking snippet, fired from a visitor's browser.
* **CAPI** (Conversions API) — Meta's server-side counterpart to the Pixel, sent straight from your server.
* **EMQ** (Event Match Quality) — Meta's 0–10 score for how well an event's data matches a real Meta user.
* **PII** (personally identifiable information) — data that identifies a person, like an email or phone number.
* **ATT** (App Tracking Transparency) — Apple's iOS prompt that lets users decline cross-app tracking.
* **ITP** (Intelligent Tracking Prevention) — Safari's system for capping and expiring tracking cookies.
* **CDP** (customer data platform) — a tool that collects and routes your customer events (Segment, RudderStack).

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Owned beats rented
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
Every ad audience is **rented.** You pay to reach those people the first time, and you
pay again every time you want to reach them after that. The moment you stop paying, the
channel goes dark. Social followings are rented too, just with a subtler landlord: the
platform's algorithm decides what fraction of "your" followers see any given post, and
that fraction has fallen for a decade across every major feed — reach you built in 2020
is not reach you have now. Andrew Chen's
[law of shitty clickthroughs](https://andrewchen.com/the-law-of-shitty-clickthroughs/)
is the organic version of the same decay: every channel's response rate erodes as it
saturates.

An **owned** channel — a verified email address, a linked Discord or Telegram account —
is the opposite. Once someone is in it:

* **There's no algorithm tax.** A Discord post or Telegram message reaches your members
  directly; an email lands in the inbox. No boost, no bid, no feed gatekeeper deciding
  who sees it.
* **Reactivation is nearly free.** Bringing a lapsed user back through an owned channel
  costs a fraction of re-buying the ad impression.
* **It's portable.** An email list exports. A Discord guild's members are linked to
  *your* identity graph — the web of links between all the ways of reaching one person —
  not just to Discord. Platform policy changes hurt; they don't zero you.

The exact multiples you'll see quoted — "acquiring costs 5–25× more than retaining,"
"owned reactivation is 5–10× cheaper than paid" — are **directional folklore** with
loose sourcing, better treated as direction than decimals. But the **mechanism is
undeniable** and it's the load-bearing argument of this chapter: *a one-time ad spend
that captures someone into an owned channel converts a rented impression into a
reusable asset.* You pay once to acquire the contact, then reach them as often as you
like for free.

**What to take from it:**

* The reason smart teams keep pouring money into rented channels is measurability — a
  cost-per-click (CPC) figure is easy to report today, while the owned-audience payoff is
  slow and asks for patience and conviction. That reporting bias is what keeps the rented
  playbook running.
* A community only sustains itself when it would exist *without you* — built around a
  mission or topic your audience already cares about, not around your product. The moment
  the company makes itself the main character, it goes quiet.
* Distribution starts from a seed (you, your network, your existing customers); the real
  constraint isn't finding the first few people, it's making something compelling enough
  that each one wants to pass it on.

This reframes what an ad is *for*. A click that bounces is money spent and gone. A click
that ends in a verified email or a linked Discord is money spent on an asset you keep —
the same dollar, but one version evaporates and the other compounds. This is why a
lifecycle tool is the natural place for acquisition to *land*: ads bring people,
lifecycle keeps them, and the bridge between the two is the moment you capture their
identity into a channel you own. An owned audience becomes an *input* to the next turn
of growth, not just the output of this one.

The scale you need is smaller than you think. Kevin Kelly's
[1,000 True Fans](https://kk.org/thetechnium/1000-true-fans/) is the foundational
version of the argument: a creator (or a niche product) doesn't need millions of
renters — it needs a thousand people it can *reach directly* who care enough to buy.
An owned audience of a few thousand engaged, reachable people is a real business
foundation; a hundred thousand rented impressions is a receipt.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: The capture mechanic: a perk for linking
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
People don't hand over their email or link their Discord out of goodwill. They do it for
a **perk.** The pattern is the same across every community that does this well:

> **"Link your account / verify your email → get \[a role, a perk, access, credits, a
> whitelist spot]."**

The perk is the conversion event, and the conversion *is* the identity capture. Three
examples worth modelling, from the communities that lean on this hardest:

* **Web3 / token-gating:** Collab.Land and Guild.xyz let a member connect a wallet, the
  bot verifies an on-chain holding, and a role unlocks gated channels or whitelist
  spots. Guild's primitive is literally **Requirements → Roles → Rewards**.
* **Quest platforms** (Zealy, Galxe, Layer3): daily actions earn XP, roles, and airdrop
  eligibility — a structured engagement loop.
* **Email-verify-for-role** (the non-web3 analog, and Hogsend's pattern): the bot links
  to a verification page; the user confirms their **email**; they get a "Verified" role
  and its perks. This is the move that produces an **email ↔ Discord** link you own —
  and several tools market it openly as list-building.

The reward works best when it maps to your product's value and lives *in the owned
channel*, so claiming it deepens the relationship rather than ending it. A perk the
user collects and walks away from buys you one email; a perk they collect *inside*
your community pulls them one step further in.

Two design considerations keep the mechanic honest:

* **The perk must be worth the link, and no more.** Over-reward and you harvest
  perk-hunters who churn the moment the reward lands (the referral-loop piece returns to
  this failure mode under referral fraud). The best perks are things a genuine user
  wanted anyway: access to the community channels, credits in the product, early
  features.
* **Ask at the moment of motivation.** The link prompt converts best right after the
  user got value — the same peak-engagement timing the lifecycle chapter uses for the
  referral ask. A cold DM asking to "verify your email for a role" is spam; the same ask
  as the doorway into a community they just decided to join is onboarding.

Get both right and the perk stops reading as a toll and starts reading as the front
door: a doorway, not a bribe.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Linking Discord, with email verification
A community worth joining
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
**The linking mechanic captures identity; it does not create a community.** A server
with a verification gate, twenty channels, and no conversation is *a list with extra
steps* — and worse than a list, because everyone who joins can *see* it's dead, which a
mailing list at least hides. The tooling delivers members; it cannot deliver a reason for
them to talk to each other. That reason is the whole job of this piece.

The practitioner who wrote the standard books on this, Jono Bacon, is the clearest voice
on what keeps a server alive, and the consensus he anchors comes down to four moves. It
starts with a **mission, not a server** — people join a movement or a topic, not a
product's support channel, so you frame the community around what your users are trying
to *become* (better at growth, better at shipping) with your product as the venue. It
runs on **fewer channels than feels right** — three active channels beat twenty empty
ones, because conversation density is the product, and you split a channel only when
volume forces it. It's held together by **rituals** — a weekly show-and-tell, a changelog
thread, an office hour: recurring hosted moments that give members a reason to come back
on a schedule (the operating-cadence chapter makes the same case for a company's own
week). And it's seeded by hand — **the first hundred members are hand-recruited**, the
community version of doing the things that don't scale: personally invited, personally
welcomed, personally introduced to each other until the conversation sustains itself
without you.

**What to take from it:**

* **A community is a belief system, not a platform** — "platforms are where people talk;
  movements are why they care." Define your creed and your rituals before you open a
  single channel.
* **Start with an event, not a server** — gather even ten people into one live session to
  share and learn, then give them the lightest possible home on a tool they already use.
* **Engagement is orchestrated, not automatic** — seed a couple of conversation-starters
  a week and broker the introductions yourself, watching daily-over-monthly active users
  (DAU/MAU) — the share of members who show up on a given day — as the heartbeat.

The movement comes first, but a movement still needs a room to happen in. Bacon's second
talk is about that room — the concrete server mechanics for once you're past the mission
stage:

**What to take from it:**

* **Foundation before tactics** — a clear purpose stated in the welcome channel, a real
  start-here onboarding path, and few enough channels that conversation stays dense.
* **Rituals carry the engagement** — scheduled events with reminders, polls for feedback,
  celebrated member milestones, and channels for member-made content.
* **Let bots do the heavy lifting** — welcome flows, reaction-roles for self-service
  interests, and gamified XP, with the stats shared back to the room to build momentum.

The measurement discipline from the rest of this course applies here too: instrument the
community like a product. `member.joined`, first message, reactions given and received —
Hogsend's connector events — give you an *activation* funnel for the community itself (the
share of joiners who reach first value, here a first post within a week) and feed the same
engagement-role journeys the Discord flow grants roles with.
[Discord's own community resources](https://discord.com/community) and the
[CMX community-building guide](https://www.cmxhub.com/reports/the-cmx-guide-to-building-a-community)
are the fuller playbooks. The tooling builds the room in an afternoon; filling it is the
year of work, and the part no bot does for you.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Linking Telegram
The identity graph
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
Every link event writes an **edge** between two ways of reaching the same person:

```
email ↔ Discord ID ↔ Telegram ID ↔ wallet ↔ anonymous web id ↔ PostHog person
```

Collect enough edges and you have an **identity graph**: one unified profile that spans
devices, sessions, and channels. The payoffs stack:

* **Retroactive stitching.** When an anonymous visitor finally gives an email or links
  Discord, all their prior touchpoints attach to the now-known profile — so attribution
  works on data *you own*, independent of cookies.
* **Channel choice on reactivation.** A lapsed user isn't reachable only by an ad you
  must re-buy — you can email them, ping them in Discord, or DM them on Telegram. The
  denser the graph, the more ways back in, and the lower your cost to re-reach anyone.
* **Better ads, legally.** Those verified, consented emails are the cleanest inputs an
  ad platform can take — a **custom audience** (your own contacts uploaded so you target
  exactly those people) and a **lookalike seed** (that same list handed to the platform
  as an example, so it finds new strangers who resemble your customers). Because each
  address is confirmed and consented, it scores high on **event match quality** (EMQ —
  the platform's 1–10 rating of how well a reported conversion matches a real person),
  which makes the whole ad machine cheaper to run. The graph you build here is the fuel
  the driving-traffic chapter's ads run on.

There's a strategic layer on top of the operational one. Chris Dixon's
[come for the tool, stay for the network](https://cdixon.org/2015/01/31/come-for-the-tool-stay-for-the-network/)
describes the classic ladder: a single-player tool earns the user, and the network they
join is what keeps them (and keeps competitors out). A linked community sits exactly on
that ladder — the product is the tool; the community and its graph are the network. And
a16z's [Hidden Networks](https://a16z.com/hidden-networks-network-effects-that-dont-look-like-network-effects/)
makes the subtler point that defensible networks often don't look like social networks —
an identity graph connecting your users' channels, reachable by you and no competitor,
is precisely such a hidden network. Features can be copied in a quarter; a dense,
consented identity graph has to be earned edge by edge.

Hogsend builds this graph on its **own** identity model (it stitches email ↔ channel ↔
anonymous), and its connectors emit the engagement events — `discord.linked`,
`reaction_added`, `member.joined`, `link.clicked` — that both trigger journeys *and* add
edges. The graph is the hub of the flywheel; every channel you link spins it faster. And
because it's *yours* — not a rented list inside someone else's platform — it's the
compounding asset a growth team is actually building over time. Features come and go;
the identity graph only gets denser.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: The referral loop
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
Referrals are where the owned graph pays off most, because **a referral program only
works if you can attribute it** — and attribution is exactly what the graph gives you.
The prize is real:
[Lenny Rachitsky's survey of referral programs](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/this-week-16-building-a-referrals)
found they commonly drive **15–50% of new-user growth** at companies that get them
right — and, in the same breath, that most programs see **under 10% of users ever
refer anyone**. Both numbers matter: the upside is large, and the median program
underdelivers because it was bolted on rather than designed.

### Incentive structures that work

* **Double-sided (give-get).** Reward *both* the referrer and the new user. It removes
  the "I'm asking a favour" friction. **Dropbox** is the canonical case: 500 MB of
  storage to *each side* per referral, capped at 16 GB. The reward was the product's own
  core value, the ask was the last step of onboarding, and it drove **~3,900% growth in
  15 months.** (The backstory: paid ads were costing Dropbox far more than a customer was
  worth, so a product-value referral was dramatically cheaper.)
* **Milestone / tiered.** An escalating ladder of rewards — **Morning Brew**'s newsletter
  referral program (stickers → shirt → bigger swag → trip to HQ) is the model, with a
  leaderboard for status. Near-zero marginal cost per reward; great for community.
* **Status / access.** For pre-launch and web3/games, the reward is *earlier access*, a
  *whitelist spot*, or an *exclusive role* — scarcity instead of cash. **Robinhood**'s
  skip-the-line waitlist is the classic.

The design principles that run across all of them: sharing that costs nothing
(prewritten copy, a one-click link), rewards on both sides, a reward aligned to product
value, visible progress (a dashboard or leaderboard), and **an ask timed to peak
engagement** — right after the *aha moment*, the first point a new user gets real value,
which is exactly where the lifecycle chapter fires its referral ask.

The two levers a loop actually turns on are *invites sent per user* and *the share of
invites that convert* — their product is the k-factor. Below 1 a loop doesn't sustain
itself, but it still quietly multiplies every other channel; at or above 1 you have true
organic virality. See what your assumptions imply before you build:

### The honest failure mode: referred users can be your worst users

Before settling on a reward, it's worth sitting with Andrew Chen's
[counterpoint](https://andrewchen.com/worst-users-referral-trials-coupons-gamification/):
users acquired through heavy incentives — referral bounties, coupons, gamified
rewards — are often the *lowest-quality* cohort you acquire — a *cohort* being any group
of users bucketed by something they share, here how they arrived — because the incentive
(not the product) did the selecting. The symptoms: referred users who never *activate*
(never reach that first-value moment), reward-farmers inviting throwaway accounts, and a
referral "channel" whose retention embarrasses every other source.

The defences are all things this course already gave you:

* **Cohorting the referred users** — grouping them by how they arrived, the same cohorts
  the daily-dashboard chapter sets up — and comparing their retention against other
  channels; a referral program is judged on retained users, not invites sent.
* **Rewarding verified behaviour, not signups.** When the referee has to verify email,
  link an account, or hit a real activation milestone before the reward fires, farmers
  lose interest. This is exactly the anti-fraud power the identity graph gives you
  (below).
* **A reward aligned to product value** (Dropbox's storage, not cash), so the people
  the incentive attracts are people who wanted the product anyway.

### Why the owned graph makes attribution work

Cookie-based referral tracking breaks on the exact journey referrals take: a friend
shares a link, the recipient clicks on their phone, then signs up on a laptop weeks
later. *Last-click* attribution — crediting the signup only to the final link clicked —
misses it entirely; the referrer goes uncredited; the program looks like it doesn't work
and gets cut.

An owned identity graph fixes this:

* First-party referral codes/links are tracked **independent of third-party cookies**,
  so you capture the conversion even as browsers tighten.
* The graph **resolves the new user across devices** and **back-credits the referrer**
  when the anonymous visitor finally identifies (gives email / links Discord).
* You can enforce **anti-fraud rules** — the referee must verify their email or link an
  account or hit a real milestone before the reward fires — because the *whole graph is
  yours*. A Discord-linked referral resolves to a real verified person, not a cookie.

And the natural community fit closes the loop: **"X verified referrals → unlock a Discord
role"** puts the reward *in the owned channel*, which densifies the graph further. The
reward for growing your audience is itself a deeper hook into your audience.

Hogsend ships a referral v1 on exactly this shape: `visited → converted → credited`, two
journeys plus a Discord role granted at a milestone — all on its own identity graph, no
cookies required.

### Build vs buy: the referral tooling landscape

You don't have to build referral attribution from scratch, and a growth team should know
the off-the-shelf options:

* **Rewardful / GrowSurf / Referral Rock / Viral Loops** — hosted referral SaaS, fast to
  stand up, with dashboards and reward logic out of the box. **Buy when** you want a
  program live this week and a simple link-based attribution is enough.
* **PartnerStack / FirstPromoter** — affiliate/partner-program flavoured, for paid
  referrers and influencers rather than user-to-user.
* **Build it on your own graph (Hogsend's approach)** — **worth it when** cross-device
  and cross-channel attribution matters, when the reward lives in your product or
  community (a role, credits, access), and when you want anti-fraud tied to real verified
  identity. The hosted tools mostly rely on cookies/links and can't resolve the
  phone-click → laptop-signup gap; your graph can.

The honest split: a hosted tool is the right first move for a simple cash/credit referral
on a single channel. The owned-graph build wins precisely when referrals are
cross-device, community-rewarded, or fraud-prone — which is most of the interesting cases
for the products this course is written for.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Other ways to own an audience
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
"Owned audience" is a spectrum, not a binary. This course emphasises Discord and Telegram
on your own infrastructure because that builds the graph you own outright — but several
other rungs are genuinely owned in the way that matters, and they're worth naming:

* **Newsletter platforms (beehiiv, ConvertKit, Substack).** A hosted email list is a
  genuinely owned audience in the sense that matters most — you can export it and reach it
  without an algorithm. beehiiv even has a built-in referral program. **Use one when**
  email is your whole channel and you don't need the behavioural-journey or identity-graph
  depth. The trade-off: your list and its data live in their platform, and you don't get
  the cross-channel graph.
* **Community platforms (Circle, Slack, Discord, Geneva).** Circle and Slack are
  higher-polish, more B2B-feeling; Discord is the default for consumer/gaming/web3 and the
  one with the richest bot ecosystem for the link-for-perk mechanic. Choose by where your
  audience already is.
* **The fully-hosted CRM/audience suites (HubSpot, Customer.io audiences).** Convenient,
  and they own the data model. Fine if you've decided to rent the infrastructure.

The principle that ranks them: **the more of the identity graph you own outright, the more
the audience compounds and the less you're exposed to a platform's pricing or policy
changes.** A hosted newsletter is a fine, real owned audience; an identity graph on your
own infrastructure is the maximal version. The rung worth picking is the one that
matches how central owned growth is to your business.

### For your kind of startup

* **Consumer / gaming / web3** — Discord is the owned channel; the link-for-a-role
  mechanic is both activation and acquisition. This is your main chapter.
* **Dev tool** — a mix: a high-quality newsletter (changelog/deep-dives) plus a Discord
  or Slack for support and community. The docs-to-Discord path is your loop.
* **B2B SaaS** — email list + maybe a Slack community for customers; referrals are often
  partner/affiliate-shaped (PartnerStack territory) rather than viral.
* **E-commerce** — email + SMS list is the owned audience; post-purchase referral
  (give-get discount) is the canonical loop, and a hosted tool is usually enough.

Wherever you land on the ladder, the test is the same: how much of the audience could you
walk out the door with tomorrow? That number is what compounds.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: The full funnel, assembled
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
Run the two halves of this course's growth engine end to end — the ads and landing pages
that bring cold traffic in, and the linking and referral mechanics that keep it — and the
same thing happens at every stage: an identity edge gets captured. The path runs from a
cold ad click (tagged with an `fbclid`, the click id an ad platform appends, and UTM
parameters, the campaign tags you set, so you can trace where the visitor came from)
through email capture and account linking to a referral loop. At each step, something is
captured:

| Stage | Goal | What's captured |
|---|---|---|
| **Ad / content** | Cold reach | `fbclid` / UTM on the landing page |
| **Landing** | Intent | anon id stitched to the click |
| **Email capture + verify** | Own the contact | **email** (consented) |
| **Discord/Telegram link (perk)** | Densify the graph | **email ↔ Discord/Telegram** |
| **Lifecycle journeys** | Activate & retain | engagement events on your own spine |
| **Referral loop** | Compound | referrer ↔ referee edge, often a Discord role |

Read top to bottom it's a funnel; the point is what each row leaves behind — an edge you
keep.

What turns that funnel into a flywheel is that the outputs feed back to the top. The
[growth-loops model](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-loops) introduced in the
leaky-bucket chapter finally has all its pieces. The verified, consented emails you
captured become **custom audiences** (your own contacts uploaded so you can target
exactly those people) and **lookalike seeds** (that same list handed to the ad platform
to find new strangers who resemble them), so the next round of ads is cheaper and better
matched. The deeper conversions you report back straight from your servers — an
activation or a purchase, not just the click, and beyond the reach of ad-blockers — make
the platform's optimisation smarter. The owned channels you built make reactivation
near-free instead of another ad buy. And the referral loop lowers your **blended CAC**
(customer-acquisition cost averaged across every channel) a little more with every turn.

The identity graph sits at the hub. Every turn of the loop adds edges, and every edge
makes the next turn cheaper. That's the difference between a funnel you keep refilling and
a loop that compounds.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
A few things worth doing while this chapter is fresh:

1. **Choose the perk** that's worth linking for (a role, access, credits, a whitelist).
   The sweet spot: valuable enough to convert, not so valuable it attracts farmers — the
   perk-for-linking piece's two design considerations are the test.
2. **Stand up the Discord and/or Telegram connector** with the `/link` email-verify
   flow — this is the moment your list starts becoming a graph.
3. **The `discord-linked` / `telegram-linked` journey** grants the perk and welcomes
   people across both channels; it's the `/link` journey from the Discord piece, nearly
   verbatim.
4. **Give the community a reason to exist beyond the perk**: a mission statement, three
   channels, one weekly ritual — the community piece's whole playbook. The first members
   are worth recruiting by hand — conversation density is what everyone who joins next
   will judge you on.
5. **Place the referral ask at your *aha* milestone** — the first point a user gets real
   value, where the lifecycle chapter fires it — with verified referrals rewarded by a
   role, and it's worth putting "referred-cohort retention" on the dashboard before
   celebrating the invite count.
6. **Sync your verified, consented emails** as a Meta custom audience and lookalike
   seed — this is where this chapter's machine and the driving-traffic chapter's connect.

That's the whole machine. The operating-cadence chapter turns running it into a weekly
rhythm, and the planning chapter sequences everything you've built into a
30/60/90/180-day plan.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Kevin Kelly — 1,000 True Fans](https://kk.org/thetechnium/1000-true-fans/) — the foundational owned-audience thesis: direct reach to a small true-fan base beats large rented reach.
* [Reforge — Growth Loops are the new funnels](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-loops) — the loop model this chapter's flywheel implements.
* [Lenny Rachitsky — Building a referrals program](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/this-week-16-building-a-referrals) — mechanics and benchmarks: 15–50% of growth when done right, under 10% of users typically refer.
* [Andrew Chen — Worst users come from referral programs](https://andrewchen.com/worst-users-referral-trials-coupons-gamification/) — the essential counterpoint; read before setting the reward.
* [Chris Dixon — Come for the tool, stay for the network](https://cdixon.org/2015/01/31/come-for-the-tool-stay-for-the-network/) — the strategic ladder from single-player product to network moat.
* [a16z — Hidden Networks](https://a16z.com/hidden-networks-network-effects-that-dont-look-like-network-effects/) — why an identity graph is a network effect that doesn't look like one.
* [Discord — Community resources](https://discord.com/community) — the primary-source playbook for server structure, onboarding, and moderation.
* [CMX — Guide to Building a Community](https://www.cmxhub.com/reports/the-cmx-guide-to-building-a-community) — the fuller community-led-growth framework behind the community piece.
* [Guild.xyz](https://help.guild.xyz/en/articles/6934383-introduction-to-guild) and [Collab.Land](https://docs.collab.land/help-docs/key-features/token-gate-communities/) — the token-gating implementations of the perk-for-linking mechanic.
* [Telegram — deep-link docs](https://core.telegram.org/api/links) — the `?start=` primitive Telegram referrals ride on.

growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.title: 8 — The owned-audience flywheel
growth-with-posthog/08-owned-audience-id.text:
> **In this chapter:** why an owned channel beats a rented ad audience, the
> "link-your-account-for-a-perk" mechanic that captures identity, how email
> verification and Discord/Telegram linking build an identity graph, what actually
> keeps a community alive once you've built one, how the graph makes referral loops
> work (and their honest failure modes), and the off-the-shelf tools you could use
> instead. Code mirrors Hogsend's real connectors.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into ten short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Where a piece has a video, the pattern is the same each time: read the
one-line reason it's worth your time, watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then
read the short digest so the point sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you
need:

1. [Owned beats rented](./01-owned-beats-rented.mdx) — a rented impression evaporates; a captured contact compounds.
2. [The capture mechanic: a perk for linking](./02-perk-for-linking.mdx) — the conversion event *is* the identity capture.
3. [Linking Discord, with email verification](./03-linking-discord.mdx) — the typed address is never trusted until the email is clicked.
4. [A community worth joining](./04-community-worth-joining.mdx) — the linking mechanic captures identity; it doesn't create a community.
5. [Linking Telegram](./05-linking-telegram.mdx) — no native email, so the same proof-by-clicking flow binds the edge.
6. [The identity graph](./06-identity-graph.mdx) — every link event writes an edge, and the edges compound.
7. [The referral loop](./07-referral-loop.mdx) — where the owned graph pays off most, plus its honest failure mode.
8. [Other ways to own an audience](./08-other-ways-to-own.mdx) — the spectrum from a hosted newsletter to your own graph.
9. [The full funnel, assembled](./09-full-funnel.mdx) — the driving-traffic chapter and this one, joined into one identity-capturing path.
10. [Before you move on](./10-before-you-move-on.mdx) — six things to do while it's fresh, then a quiz to check yourself.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter**

* **Owned vs rented audience** — a channel you can reach for free forever (a verified email, a linked Discord) versus one you pay to reach every time (an ad audience, a social following).
* **Identity graph** — the web of links between all the ways of reaching one person (email ↔ Discord ↔ Telegram ↔ anonymous web visitor), stitched into a single profile.
* **Custom audience** — a list of your own contacts (usually hashed emails) uploaded to an ad platform so you can target exactly those people.
* **Lookalike seed** — that same contact list handed to an ad platform as an example, from which it finds new strangers who resemble your existing customers.
* **EMQ (event match quality)** — a platform's 1–10 score for how well a conversion you report back matches a real person; verified emails score high, which makes ad optimisation cheaper.
* **CAC (customer-acquisition cost)** — what you pay, on average, to acquire one customer; "blended" CAC averages it across every channel.
* **k-factor** — invites sent per user × the share that convert; above 1 a referral loop is self-sustaining, below 1 it still quietly amplifies every other channel.
* **Cohort** — a group of users bucketed by something they share (signup week, acquisition channel) so you can compare how each group retains.
* **Activation / aha moment** — the first point a new user gets real value (the "aha"), turned into a measurable event like `project_created` within seven days of signup.
* **Last-click attribution** — crediting a signup only to the last link clicked, which loses referrals that span a phone click and a later laptop signup.
* **UTM / fbclid** — tags on a URL (UTM parameters you set, `fbclid` an ad platform adds) that let you trace which campaign a visitor arrived from.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The whole thing, in one loop
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Everything in this course is a single loop, and the **identity graph** sits at its hub —
the linked record of who each person is across email, Discord, Telegram and the anonymous
session they first arrived in. Every arm of the loop either writes to that graph or reads
back from it:

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │                                                         │
   drive traffic                                                  │
   ads · content · community                                      │
        │                                                         │
        ▼                                                         │
   capture identity                 measure everything            │
   email verify · link Discord  ───► PostHog: funnels, retention  │
   /Telegram · perks for linking     the daily dashboard          │
        │                                  │                      │
        ▼                                  ▼                      │
   ┌─────────── IDENTITY GRAPH ───────────┐                       │
   │ email ↔ Discord ↔ Telegram ↔ anon    │                       │
   └──────────────────┬───────────────────┘                      │
                      │                                           │
                      ▼                                           │
   keep them                                                      │
   lifecycle journeys in Hogsend ──► fire conversions + audiences ┘
   welcome · nudge · win-back ·       back to the ads
   dunning · referral                 → referrals lower CAC
```

Read it as one sentence: **you measure so you can see the leaks; you keep users with
lifecycle journeys — welcome, nudge, win-back, dunning (the automated recovery of failed
payments), referral — so the bucket holds; then you drive traffic and fold every visitor
into an audience you own; that audience feeds better ads and a referral loop, which lowers
your customer-acquisition cost (CAC — what you pay to win one user); and the whole thing
stays measurable because every step fires an event.**

The order runs *measure → keep → grow*, and never the reverse, because of the **leaky
bucket** — a product that loses users about as fast as it wins them. Acquisition is the
last lever precisely because it wastes the most money while the bucket leaks and pays back
the most once it doesn't. Agreeing that order as a team, before anyone spends a pound on an
ad, is the single most valuable decision in the course.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: Who does what
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Every part of this system has exactly one job, and the whole thing stays legible only
because those jobs never blur into each other. You decide what matters: which stages of
the funnel to watch — **AARRR**, the five stages of acquisition, activation, retention,
referral and revenue — and which single **North Star** metric best captures the value your
product delivers. PostHog measures it. Hogsend acts on it. The channels carry the message;
none of the tools ever decides anything.

| Job | Tool |
|---|---|
| Decide what to measure (AARRR, North Star) and own the order | You |
| Collect events, identify people | PostHog |
| See the funnel, retention, leaks | PostHog |
| Run lifecycle journeys (email, Discord, Telegram) | Hogsend |
| Drive paid traffic | Meta ads (or the right channel) |
| Track conversions accurately | Pixel + CAPI (Meta's server-side Conversions API), via PostHog |
| Capture & link identity, referrals | Hogsend connectors |

**PostHog detects. Hogsend acts. You decide.** Hold onto that split and the system stays
simple no matter how much you bolt on — a new channel, a new journey, a new metric each
slot into one of the three roles rather than inventing a fourth. It's a division of labour,
not a pile of tools.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The build order at a glance
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
The course's spine compresses to four gates, and each is a bar you clear before the next
one makes any sense:

1. **Day 0 — commit.** Name the growth owner, write the **activation sentence** — one line
   fixing what "activated" means as a specific event in a specific window, like *"activated
   \= a project created within seven days of signup"* — and agree, as a team, that keeping
   users comes before buying them.
2. **Days 1–30 — Measure.** PostHog live, a tracking plan, the **activation event**
   instrumented so that first taste of value actually fires, the daily dashboard built.
   The gate: you can say where the funnel leaks, with numbers, not with a hunch.
3. **Days 31–60 — Keep.** Welcome, win-back, and **dunning** journeys — dunning being the
   automatic chasing of failed subscription payments, the fastest revenue you'll ever
