   recover — each run against a **holdout**, a slice of users deliberately left unmessaged
   so you can measure the journey's real lift instead of guessing at it. The gate: lift
   you've measured, not sensed.
4. **Days 61–180 — Grow, then compound.** Open the acquisition tap, capture every new
   visitor's identity into channels you own, and let the whole thing settle into a standing
   experimentation program that keeps improving it.

That's the shape at a glance. The dated version — each phase with its interactive
checklist, exit criteria, realistic targets, and the anti-patterns that blow it up — is a
chapter of its own. This piece is about what keeps the machine alive *after* it's built:
the cadence, and the culture of experiments.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The operating cadence
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Once it's built, it runs on a rhythm, not on heroics. This is the ritual that turns a
one-off growth project into a growth *team* — a team that keeps looking, not one that
looked once. The principle — [Reforge calls it your operating
cadence](https://www.reforge.com/blog/operating-cadence) — is that the *rhythm* of looking
is itself a designed system: decide in advance what gets read daily, weekly, and monthly,
and by whom, so attention stops depending on anyone's memory.

**Daily (2 minutes).** The daily job is *noticing*, not analysing: a glance at the Slack
alerts, the activation funnel, and the active-users trend — and the dashboard itself only
if an alert fired. If the daily glance regularly takes more than five minutes, that's
usually a sign the alerts need tuning, not that you need more discipline.

**Weekly (30 minutes, the growth review).** The one meeting this course asks you to hold,
and it has a fixed agenda — a compressed version of the weekly growth meeting [Brian
Balfour has documented](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/growth-meeting) from running growth
at HubSpot and Reforge:

1. **Metrics read (10 min).** The retention curve and the **lifecycle chart** — PostHog's
   weekly breakdown of how many users are new, returning, dormant or resurrecting — answer
   one question: is growth real, or just churn-and-replace? Then your **North Star**, the
   single metric that best tracks the value you deliver, read against last week. Anomalies
   get noted and assigned an owner rather than debugged live; group debugging is how a
   30-minute meeting becomes a 2-hour one.
2. **Journeys and spend (10 min).** Each live journey's send volume and its **holdout
   lift** — the extra activation or retention it causes, measured against a held-back
   control group. Then ad performance: cost per conversion, **EMQ** (Event Match Quality —
   Meta's score for how well your conversion data resolves to a real person), and which
   audiences are working. This is where kill-or-scale decisions get made, out loud.
3. **Experiments (10 min).** What finished (result and learning, a sentence each), what
   launches next from the top of the scored backlog, and what's blocked.

The discipline that makes the meeting work: it reviews **learnings and decisions**, not
status. "The welcome-nudge holdout shows +4 points activation, we're keeping it and
removing the day-5 email" is a growth review; "we sent 3,400 emails this week" is theatre.

Two habits tend to close the weekly loop well. Ending the week with **3–5 session
replays** — recordings of real user sessions — of the biggest drop-off gives you the
qualitative *why* behind the quantitative *where*. And **writing the week's one learning
down** somewhere permanent is cheaper than it looks — a learning that lives in one person's
head is one the team usually pays to re-discover six months later.

**Monthly (an hour).** Zoom out to the questions that are too slow to see weekly:

* The **activation metric** — is it still the best predictor of retention? The way to check
  is the method that first proved it: correlate the candidate behaviour against retention,
  then run an experiment to confirm it *causes* the retention rather than merely
  accompanying it.
* **Incremental lift** from the lifecycle program, measured against a **global holdout** (a
  slice of users held out of every journey, so the baseline stays honest) — the number that
  justifies the whole Keep phase to anyone who asks.
* **Blended CAC** — your customer-acquisition cost averaged across every channel — and the
  referral loop's contribution to it. Is growth getting cheaper as the owned audience
  grows? That compounding, where a denser owned audience makes each new user cheaper to
  reach, is the flywheel the whole Grow phase exists to start — and this is where it shows
  up, in the finances.
* **Pruning.** Journeys with no lift, events no one queries, ad sets that don't convert — a
  growth system accretes cruft exactly like a codebase, and a monthly prune keeps it
  legible.

The cadence is the point. A growth system that's checked on a rhythm survives a busy
quarter; one that depends on someone remembering to look does not. Andrew Capland — who
ran growth at Wistia — has watched teams build exactly this rhythm and lose it, and his
talk is about the drift: how a weekly ritual decays back into shipping without learning,
and what keeps it alive:

**What to take from it:**

* Culture change is an artifact, not a pep talk — write the operating manual and a short
  list of team values, then *launch* it internally like a feature so everyone knows the
  rhythm is real.
* The cadence lives or dies in the small rituals: keep "where did you get to question how
  we've always done things this week?" a standing line in planning and 1:1s, not a one-time
  speech.
* Lead by dogfooding failure — a leader who shares an experiment where their own hypothesis
  was flat wrong gives the team permission to run tests that don't pan out, which is the
  whole point of tempo.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The experimentation program
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
The cadence keeps the machine observed; experiments are how it improves. The difference
between a team that occasionally A/B tests and a team with an experimentation *program*
is three artifacts:

**A scored backlog.** Every growth idea — a new journey, a pricing page change, an
onboarding step removed — goes into one list as a **hypothesis** rather than a task:
*"if we \[change], \[metric] will move \[direction] because \[reasoning]."* Scoring each on
**[ICE](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model)** — impact, confidence,
ease, each 1–10 — lets the scores order the list.
([PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/) — potential, importance, ease —
is the same idea with different letters; either works, and the only real mistake is
debating frameworks instead of running tests.) The score's job isn't precision — it's
letting the *"this would be huge"* claim meet *"how confident are we, actually?"*, and
giving the weekly meeting a top of the list to pull from instead of the loudest idea
in the room.

**A steady tempo.** Sean Ellis — the man who coined both "growth hacking" and the
**North Star** framing (organising a whole team around the one metric that best captures
the value you deliver) this course uses — argues the compounding asset is your
**velocity of tests**: more shots on goal means faster learning, and the learning is
what compounds even when individual tests fail.

**What to take from it:**

* The best growth "hacks" aren't tricks — they're finding where people get confused in the
  product today and making it simpler, which starts with understanding why your must-have
  users actually love it.
* Volume of experiments is the lever, echoing Bezos's line that Amazon's success is a
  function of how many experiments it runs per day, month and year — nobody knows in
  advance which idea will win, so you find the big one by testing many.
* You miss every shot you don't take: running three-to-five tests a week beats polishing
  one "perfect" test, because any single one might be the outsized winner and you can't
  tell which ahead of time.

Reforge's [experiment management
system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system) puts a
number on the same idea — a standard template plus a visible backlog roughly tripled
one team's testing velocity — and [First Round's growth-sprint
process](https://review.firstround.com/the-secret-to-running-effective-growth-sprints-follow-this-process-to-learn-faster/)
wraps it in a sprint so every experiment ends in a written learning. For a startup,
"high tempo" might mean one experiment a week. That's fine. One a week, every week,
with results written down, beats a burst of ten followed by a quarter of nothing.

**Honest rules.** Tempo without discipline manufactures false wins, so the program
needs three:

* **Not everything deserves an experiment.** Obvious fixes, reversible changes, and
  changes too small to measure can just ship —
  [Lenny's guide to when *not* to test](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment)
  is a good checklist. Experiments earn their overhead on decisions that are expensive
  to get wrong.
* **Sample size decides what you can learn.** At startup traffic, most tests can't
  detect small effects — which is why big swings (whole journeys, whole onboarding
  flows) tend to beat button colours, and why "no detectable difference" is an honest,
  common result rather than a failure.
  [Kohavi and Lenny's A/B testing guide](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing)
  covers the traps — peeking, underpowered wins, p-hacking by another name.
* **The losers are worth writing down too.** A result only really counts once it's
  recorded — hypothesis, what happened, what you'd try differently. The write-up
  takes thirty seconds; re-running a forgotten failed experiment takes a month.

Where does the *content* of the backlog come from? The machine you built: the funnel's
biggest drop-off, the journey with no measured lift, the channel with decent volume but
poor activation, the session replay where users rage-click.
[YC's growth curriculum](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6k-growth-for-startups)
makes the same loop explicit at whole-company scale: pick the one metric, find the
constraint, test against it, repeat. If the backlog feels empty, the dashboard is being
read too politely.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: The patterns that undo it
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Every team that builds this system brushes against the same handful of failure modes —
not exotic ones, but the predictable ways a good machine quietly stops working. Naming
them is half the defence, because the escape route for each is something you already have
in hand:

* **Scaling acquisition into a leaky bucket.** The most expensive mistake of the set:
  pouring paid traffic into a product that loses users about as fast as it wins them. The
  escape is order — fix retention first, so the users you buy actually stay long enough to
  pay you back.
* **Chasing someone else's magic number.** "Add seven friends in ten days" was *Facebook's*
  activation threshold, not a law of nature. Find your own by correlating candidate
  behaviours against retention and confirming the winner with an experiment; a borrowed
  number is a rally cry, not physics.
* **Building event names dynamically.** Generating event names on the fly —
  `clicked_button_blue`, `clicked_button_red` — is one of the quickest ways to ruin an
  analytics setup, because nothing stays queryable. One fixed event (`button_clicked`) with
  the colour in a property keeps every chart intact.
* **Installing analytics and never looking.** The **dashboard graveyard** — a beautifully
  instrumented product nobody opens. Measurement without a ritual to act on it is theatre;
  the operating cadence, a rhythm of who reads what and when, is what keeps the dashboard
  from going quiet.
* **Experiment theatre.** Underpowered tests "validating" a decision already made, wins
  nobody can reproduce, losers never written down. The fix is counting learnings rather
  than launches, and being honest about what your sample size can actually detect.
* **Trusting the Pixel alone.** The browser **Pixel** — Meta's client-side tracking
  snippet — is blocked or lost for a third of users or more, so it under-counts conversions
  badly. The reliable shape is Pixel *and* **CAPI** (Meta's server-side Conversions API,
  which reports conversions straight from your backend), deduplicated, and fed from wherever
  you actually hold identity.
* **Treating server-side as a consent loophole.** Sending conversions from your server
  doesn't sidestep privacy law: a hashed email is *pseudonymous*, not anonymous, so consent
  still has to be captured before you send it.
* **Measuring opens instead of lift.** An email open doesn't prove the email caused
  anything — plenty of those users would have come back regardless. Only a **holdout**, a
  comparable group you deliberately don't message, tells you the journey's real effect.
* **Renting your whole audience.** If every user lives only on someone else's platform, you
  pay again each time you want to reach them. Capturing identity into channels you own —
  email, Discord, Telegram — is what turns a rented audience into one you can reach for
  free.

None of these is exotic; each is just the system run with one of its disciplines skipped.
The machine rarely breaks loudly — it drifts, one dropped habit at a time.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: You're a growth team now
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
The tools were never the hard part — you could
install PostHog, Hogsend, and a pixel in an afternoon. The thing that actually changed,
if you did the work, is how your team *operates*:

* You **measure honestly** — decisions cite the funnel, not the loudest opinion, and
  "activated" means a specific event, not a vibe.
* You **act automatically** — the messages that pull users back to value are journeys in
  your repo, triggered by behaviour, code-reviewed like the rest of the product, and
  proven with holdouts.
* You **own your audience** — every dollar of acquisition lands in a channel you can reach
  again for free, on an identity graph — the web of email, Discord, Telegram and anonymous
  IDs tied to one person — that only gets denser.
* You **learn on a rhythm** — a weekly cadence, a scored backlog, a steady tempo of
  experiments whose results get written down, winners and losers alike.

That's the whole definition of a product-led growth team the course opened with, and
you'll notice none of it required a growth department. It required one owner, one loop, and
a rhythm. A founder who can read a retention curve and ship a journey *is* the growth team —
and the system you've built is what lets a team that small operate like one many times its
size.

It also doesn't matter what your startup is. The tactics flexed by type all the way
through — activation means a first match for a marketplace, a first deploy for a dev tool,
a second purchase for e-commerce, a linked Discord for a community — but the operating
model never changed: instrument, measure, act, own, repeat. That portability is the whole
point. Growth stops being a thing you hope works and becomes a system you run.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
Three things worth doing while this chapter is fresh:

1. **The weekly growth review** — booking it now, 30 minutes recurring with the
   operating-cadence agenda, is probably the highest-leverage calendar invite in the
   course. A rhythm that exists in the calendar survives; one that exists as an
   intention doesn't.
2. **The experiment backlog** — one shared doc, the hypothesis format from the
   experimentation program, ICE scores. Your dashboard's three worst numbers are the
   natural first entries.
3. **The dated rollout** — the full 30/60/90/180-day plan, each phase with its own
   interactive checklist and exit criteria. It's the chapter where all of this becomes a
   sequence with dates on it.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Reforge — Rethinking your operating cadence](https://www.reforge.com/blog/operating-cadence) — the daily/weekly/monthly rhythm as a designed system, not a habit.
* [Brian Balfour — How to run a weekly growth meeting](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/growth-meeting) — the full agenda the operating cadence compresses, from the person who ran it at HubSpot.
* [Reforge — The growth experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system) — backlog + template + velocity metrics; the "tripled our testing velocity" case.
* [First Round Review — Running effective growth sprints](https://review.firstround.com/the-secret-to-running-effective-growth-sprints-follow-this-process-to-learn-faster/) — wrapping experiments in a sprint so each one ends in a written learning.
* [SaaStr — Sean Ellis on building a company-wide growth culture](https://www.saastr.com/sean-ellis-founder-ceo-of-growthhackers-building-a-company-wide-growth-culture-video-transcript/) — high-tempo testing and the growth-master role, in the primary voice.
* [Lenny Rachitsky — When NOT to run an experiment](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment) — the discipline half of tempo.
* [Lenny Rachitsky & Ronny Kohavi — The ultimate guide to A/B testing](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing) — trustworthy results at honest sample sizes.
* [ProductPlan — ICE scoring](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model) and [Conversion.com — PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/) — the two standard backlog-scoring schemes; pick one.
* [YC — Growth for startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6k-growth-for-startups) — the whole loop at company scale, from Airbnb's former growth lead.

growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.title: 9 — Putting it all together
growth-with-posthog/09-putting-it-all-to.text:
> **In this chapter:** the end-to-end system as a single loop, the daily/weekly/monthly
> rhythm that turns it from a project into an operation, how to run experiments at a
> steady tempo without fooling yourself, the patterns that tend to undo it — and a
> return to the question the course opened with: what it takes to run your startup as a
> product-led growth team. The dated build plan itself — the 30/60/90/180-day rollout,
> each phase with its own checklist and targets — is a chapter of its own.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into eight short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. Two carry a short video: read the one-line reason it's worth your
time, watch it (or read the transcript underneath), then read the digest so the point
sticks. Work through them in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [The whole thing, in one loop](./01-one-loop.mdx) — the entire course as a single diagram.
2. [Who does what](./02-who-does-what.mdx) — PostHog detects, Hogsend acts, you decide.
3. [The build order at a glance](./03-build-order.mdx) — four gates, and why the order holds.
4. [The operating cadence](./04-operating-cadence.mdx) — the daily/weekly/monthly rhythm.
5. [The experimentation program](./05-experimentation-program.mdx) — scored backlog, steady tempo, honest rules.
6. [The patterns that undo it](./06-patterns-that-undo-it.mdx) — the failure modes, named.
7. [You're a growth team now](./07-youre-a-growth-team.mdx) — what actually changed.
8. [Before you move on](./08-before-you-move-on.mdx) — three things to do, then the quiz.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter**

* **AARRR** — the five funnel stages: acquisition, activation, retention, referral, revenue.
* **North Star** — the single metric that best captures the value your product delivers.
* **Activation** — a new user's first real taste of value, pinned to a specific event. The **activation event** is that event; the **activation sentence** names it and its window ("activated = a project created within seven days of signup").
* **Identity graph** — the linked record of who a person is across email, Discord, Telegram and their anonymous first visit.
* **Lifecycle journey** — an automated message sequence (welcome, nudge, win-back) triggered by what a user does.
* **Lifecycle chart** — PostHog's view of how many users are new, returning, dormant or resurrecting each period.
* **Dunning** — the automated recovery of failed subscription payments.
* **Holdout / global holdout** — a slice of users deliberately left out of a journey (or every journey) so you can measure its real lift.
* **CAC** — customer-acquisition cost, what you pay to win one customer; **blended CAC** averages it across every channel.
* **Pixel + CAPI** — Meta's browser tracking snippet plus its server-side Conversions API; run together and deduplicated, they count conversions the Pixel alone misses.
* **EMQ** — Event Match Quality, Meta's score for how well your conversion data resolves to a real person.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: How to use this plan
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
A plan you can run beats a plan you can admire, and the difference is almost entirely
about sequence. This one turns the whole course into a build order — measure, then keep,
then grow, then compound — and three rules govern how to read it.

* **The phases are gates, not dates.** "30/60/90/180" assumes roughly a founder-day per
  week of focused growth work. With more time you can compress; with less, stretch —
  the one thing worth protecting is the *order*, because each phase's exit criteria
  are the entry requirements for the next. Shipping ads (phase 3) before retention
  work (phase 2) is the leaky-bucket pattern — pouring acquisition into a product that
  can't yet keep users — now with a budget attached.
* **The plan is stage-agnostic, the targets aren't.** A pre-launch product runs the same
  sequence but treats phase 1 as "instrument before launch" and phase 3 as launch
  channels rather than paid scaling. Where a number matters, this chapter says what it
  gates rather than pretending one benchmark fits every product — the same trap the
  leaky-bucket chapter names about activation: chasing someone else's **magic number**
  (the specific usage threshold that predicts retention for one company) misleads a plan
  the same way a borrowed benchmark misleads a funnel.
* **This is the same first-90-days shape growth operators run when they land in a new
  role** — learn the data first, find early wins in activation and retention, only then
  scale acquisition ([Reforge's first-90-days
  guide](https://www.reforge.com/guides/succeed-in-your-new-growth-leadership-role),
  [Delivering Value's head-of-growth
  plan](https://deliveringvalue.co/growth-essays/head-of-growth)). You're running it on
  your own company.

**What to take from it:**

* The first month is mostly *looking* — sign up as a real user with a plus-address, hit the paywalls, learn how the team and tools actually work before you commit to projects — the same measure-before-you-move instinct as the Measure phase below.
* Pick work in the "Goldilocks zone" — important enough to move a metric *and* squarely inside your existing skills — so you bank quick wins and trust before the harder bets, which is the operator's version of "retention before acquisition".
* Guard your capacity out loud — cap the work in progress, name your quick-wins-versus-experiments split — because the two fastest ways to derail a plan are picking the wrong projects and taking on too many at once.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Day 0 — commit (one hour, no code)
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Day 0 is one hour, no code, with the whole team in the room. It exists to settle the two
decisions that quietly sink most growth efforts before they start: nobody actually owns
the loop, and money flows to acquisition before the product can keep anyone. Settle both
out loud, once, and the next 180 days have something to stand on.

The last two matter more than they look. A recurring 30-minute meeting is the smallest
possible container for a growth *ritual* — the thing the cadence chapter argues separates
a growth team from a team that ran a growth project once and moved on. And putting growth
work in the same backlog as product work is what makes growth *an engineering discipline*
— a system your team builds and measures, not a budget you hand off — real rather than
aspirational.

Two of those commitments are *writing* commitments — the growth owner and the activation
sentence — so put them down now. Both save to [your workbook](/workbook) alongside
everything else you record in the course, each linked back to the lesson it came from.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Days 1–30 — Measure
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
**Goal: see the truth about your funnel.** Nothing in this phase grows the business
directly. All of it decides whether the next five months of work target the real
leak or a guessed one.

Week by week, the shape most teams follow:

| Week | Focus |
|---|---|
| 1 | PostHog live: SDK installed, `identify`/`reset` wired, starter events firing |
| 2 | Tracking plan written; activation event defined; server-side revenue events |
| 3 | Reverse proxy; data QA — watch replays, check identities merge correctly |
| 4 | The daily dashboard built and pinned; alerts to Slack; first honest read |

Weeks 1–3 are the instrumentation chapter's work applied to your own product; week 4 is
the daily-dashboard chapter's. The schedule is a suggestion; the order inside it isn't.

**Exit criteria.** You can open the dashboard and say, with numbers: *where* in the
funnel you lose the most people, *what* your week-4 retention roughly is, and *which*
phase of product-market fit you're in — pre-fit, hunting, or found, read off whether your
retention curve flattens rather than falling to zero (the stages chapter maps these
phases in full). If those three sentences aren't sayable yet, that's the signal to stay
in this phase a little longer — [YC's analytics-for-startups
guidance](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8H-analytics-for-startups) is blunt that
most startups' first analytics problem is not volume of charts but trusting what the
few important ones say.

**The pattern to watch for here** is instrumenting forever. Thirty days is enough to
measure a funnel honestly; it is not enough to build a data platform — a schema
registry in week three is usually growth work quietly traded for tooling work.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Days 31–60 — Keep
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
**Goal: plug the biggest leaks before spending a cent on traffic.** This is the phase
with the highest return per hour in the whole plan — the users it saves are users you
already paid (in money or effort) to acquire.

Journeys are best built in the order of expected impact, which for almost every
product looks like:

1. **Welcome + onboarding nudge** — the gap between signup and activation is nearly
   always the biggest leak, which is why the lifecycle chapter builds this journey
   first. One journey: welcome on signup, a nudge at +48h if the activation event
   hasn't fired.
2. **Dunning** — the automated recovery of failed card payments. If you charge money,
   those failures are pure, recoverable churn, and building the sequence in Hogsend
   (the Hogsend chapter) tends to be the fastest revenue win in the whole course.
3. **Win-back** — dormant-user re-engagement, triggered by an inactivity window, with
   something concrete to come back to.

**Exit criteria.** Either your activation or early-retention number has visibly moved,
or — more honestly — your **holdout comparison** shows the journeys lifting it. A holdout
is a slice of users you deliberately leave *out* of the journey, kept as a baseline; the
gap between them and the messaged group is the **incremental lift**, the change the
journey actually caused rather than one that would have happened anyway. It's the
lifecycle chapter's whole argument in one number: opens and clicks are activity, lift is
proof. The bucket holds water well enough that pouring into it isn't waste.

**The pattern to watch for** is building all eight journeys you can imagine instead of
the three that matter — volume of automation isn't the goal; measured lift is. A
second, subtler one: declaring victory off open rates. Without a holdout, what got
measured was sending, not keeping.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Days 61–90 — Grow
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
**Goal: open the tap, and make every drop land in an audience you own.** Only now does
acquisition spend make sense — and for some products, dev tools especially, "the tap" is
content, launches, and community rather than paid ads at all, a case the paid-traffic
chapter makes in full.

**What to take from it:**

* Show up with your *own* plan — if you don't, someone who's never held your role hands you theirs — and let it get looser as you learn, the mirror image of this chapter's gates easing from day-0 checklists to a day-180 rhythm.
* The 30–60 window is where an operator writes two things worth stealing: a first-cut growth strategy and an operating manual for how growth actually works — the same "put the ritual on paper" move as Day 0 here.
* By day 60–90 the job flips from executing to re-planning from evidence, which is exactly what your day-90 gate hands off to the Compound phase.

The checklist below leans on a few ad-tech terms worth having straight before you spend.
The **Conversions API (CAPI)** sends conversions to Meta from your own server rather than
the browser, so ad-blockers and cookie loss don't quietly drop them; you run it alongside
the Meta Pixel and **deduplicate** on a shared event id so one purchase isn't counted
twice. **Event Match Quality (EMQ)** is Meta's 0–10 score for how confidently it can tie
those conversions back to a real person — feed it email and stable identifiers and it
climbs, which the paid-traffic chapter walks through end to end. A **lookalike seed** is
the list of your best users you hand the platform so it can go find more people who
resemble them.

**Exit criteria.** Traffic is flowing from a channel you chose on purpose;
conversions are tracked accurately enough to optimise on (not Pixel-alone guesswork);
and a visible share of new users end up in a channel you own — a verified email, a
linked Discord or Telegram — rather than only in an ad platform's audience you rent.

**The pattern to watch for** is scaling spend before the measurement is trustworthy.
When EMQ is low or events are missing, the ad platform optimises on noise — and the
tuition comes out of your budget. A day spent on conversion quality routinely beats a
week of creative testing.

Before you commit a budget, check the arithmetic holds against your runway — a channel
that works but pays back slower than you can wait is still a cash problem, not just a
growth one:

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Days 91–180 — Compound
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
The first 90 days build the machine. The next 90 turn it into a *culture* — the
difference between a system that ran once and one that gets a little better every
week. Three threads run in parallel:

**1. An experimentation program, not occasional experiments.** Keep a scored backlog
of growth hypotheses and always pull the next test from the top of it. Sean Ellis's
[ICE](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model) — score each idea by
*impact, confidence, and ease* — is the standard scheme, and
[PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/) (*potential, importance, ease*)
is a close cousin. The reason to keep the tempo high is that
[learning rate, not any single win, is what compounds](https://www.saastr.com/sean-ellis-founder-ceo-of-growthhackers-building-a-company-wide-growth-culture-video-transcript/);
Reforge's [experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system)
packages that same idea with a template and a velocity metric attached. Tempo without
discipline misfires, though — [not everything deserves an experiment](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment),
and an underpowered test that "wins" is [worse than no test at all](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing).

**2. Prove the program pays.** By day 180 you should be reporting **incremental lift**
from the lifecycle program — measured against a **global holdout**, a standing slice of
users kept out of all lifecycle messaging as a permanent baseline (the lifecycle
chapter's main instrument) — and **blended CAC** (customer-acquisition cost averaged
across every channel, paid and organic: total spend over all new customers) including the
referral loop's contribution. Is growth getting cheaper as the owned audience grows?
That's the flywheel from the owned-audience chapter showing up in the finances.

**3. Decide what you are before you scale.** Real acquisition money is worth gating on
evidence of product-market fit — the
[Sean Ellis 40% test](https://pmfsurvey.com/) ("how disappointed would you be if this
product disappeared?") is the standard instrument. It's also worth running the system
yourself for a while before hiring a head of growth to run it for you; First Round's
[founder-led growth playbook](https://review.firstround.com/founder-led-growth-playbook/)
makes the case that premature growth hires fail precisely because the founder skipped
the loop you've just spent 90 days building.

The 40% test is worth actually running, not just citing — survey your active users and
put the numbers in:

The phase itself compresses into eight habits — if these are true by day 180, the
machine is compounding:

**Exit criteria — and the real finish line.** There isn't one, and that's the point:
by day 180 the plan should have dissolved into an operating rhythm — the cadence
chapter's ritual — that no longer needs this chapter. The observable test: growth work
appears in every sprint, decisions cite the funnel, and the team can name last month's
biggest learning from a *failed* experiment without embarrassment.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: What good looks like at each gate
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Every gate in this plan is a yes/no question, not a benchmark. What "good" looks like is
deliberately loose, because the right numbers are your product's own rather than a
competitor's threshold — chasing someone else's **magic number** (the usage bar that
predicts retention for one specific company) is the trap the leaky-bucket chapter warns
about. So each gate is framed as a single question the evidence should be able to answer
with a *yes*:

| Gate | The question | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| Day 30 | Do we trust the data? | Funnel + retention read aloud, replays match events |
| Day 60 | Does the bucket hold? | Holdout-measured lift on activation or early retention |
| Day 90 | Is acquisition landing in owned channels? | Conversion quality good; % of signups verified/linked |
| Day 180 | Is the loop compounding? | Test tempo steady; lift + blended CAC reported monthly |

Read down the Evidence column and the through-line is the same at every gate: a number
you can say out loud and defend, not a chart that merely exists. Two outside yardsticks
keep those numbers honest as the machine grows —
[YC's key startup metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KR-key-startup-metrics)
is a sane preview of what investors will eventually ask of the very same funnel and
retention figures, and [Reforge on North Star metrics](https://www.reforge.com/blog/north-star-metrics)
is the discipline for keeping your **North Star** (the single number that best captures
the value users get) meaningful rather than vanity as you scale. Pass all four gates with
a straight face and the plan has done its job — the gates were never about the calendar,
only about whether the evidence is real.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Three things are worth doing while the plan is fresh, before the week swallows it:

1. **Day 0 itself.** It's an hour with the whole team, and everything else in the plan
   assumes it happened. The [Day 0 checklist](./02-day-0-commit.mdx) keeps your place —
   tick things off as they land and they'll be here when you come back.
2. **The four gate reviews, in the calendar** — day 30, 60, 90, 180 from today. A
   30-minute review against each phase's exit criteria, with that phase's checklist —
   [Measure](./03-days-1-30-measure.mdx), [Keep](./04-days-31-60-keep.mdx),
   [Grow](./05-days-61-90-grow.mdx), [Compound](./06-days-91-180-compound.mdx) — open in
   front of you. Gates that exist in the calendar get held; gates that exist only in a
   chapter don't.
3. **A look at [your workbook](/workbook)** — everything you've written across the
   course (activation sentence, North Star, tracking plan, hypotheses, these
   checklists) in one place, each entry linked back to the chapter that produced it.
   That page *is* your growth plan now.

And if you're happy to share it — where are you starting from?

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Reforge — Succeed in your new growth leadership role](https://www.reforge.com/guides/succeed-in-your-new-growth-leadership-role) — the professional version of this plan: learn the data, early wins, then scale.
* [Delivering Value — the Head of Growth 90-day plan](https://deliveringvalue.co/growth-essays/head-of-growth) — an operator's 30/60/90 with milestones, good for calibrating yours.
* [First Round Review — the founder-led growth playbook](https://review.firstround.com/founder-led-growth-playbook/) — why you run this loop yourself before hiring for it.
* [YC — Analytics for startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8H-analytics-for-startups) — the day-1-to-30 instrumentation phase, from the source most founders trust.
* [YC — Key startup metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KR-key-startup-metrics) — what the day-30/60/90 numbers get measured against eventually.
* [Sean Ellis — the PMF survey](https://pmfsurvey.com/) — the 40% test that gates the day-180 spend decision.
* [Reforge — the growth experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system) — backlog, template, and velocity metrics for the Compound phase.
* [Lenny Rachitsky & Ronny Kohavi — the ultimate guide to A/B testing](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing) — the rigor that keeps a high test tempo honest.
* [Product Marketing Alliance — 30-60-90 plan template](https://www.productmarketingalliance.com/30-60-90-day-plan-template-framework/) — if you want the generic scaffold this chapter specialises.
* [John Doerr — Google's OKR playbook](https://www.whatmatters.com/resources/google-okr-playbook) — a heavier operating cadence for when the team outgrows one weekly meeting.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: The zero-to-one version (if you have no users yet)
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
The plan so far assumes you have a funnel to measure. If you're pre-launch, or you
launched last week and the dashboard would show single digits, it doesn't change
*order* — it changes *shape*. The order still protects you: chasing acquisition before
you can keep anyone is the leaky-bucket mistake (the same one the leaky-bucket chapter
names) whether you have ten users or ten thousand. But at zero-to-one, the Measure phase
isn't "build the daily dashboard" — it's "find evidence a real person wants this at all".

**What each phase becomes before product-market fit:**

* **Measure → talk to users, instrument the one signal.** With no traffic, quantitative
  dashboards are noise. The measurement that matters is qualitative: problem interviews,
  and watching the first handful of people try the product. Instrument exactly one thing —
  the activation event that would mean "they got it", the one the leaky-bucket chapter
  has you define — so the moment you *do* have users, you can already see whether they
  reach value.
* **Keep → make the first ten users successful by hand.** This is the phase Paul Graham
  means by [*Do Things That Don't Scale*](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html): onboard people
  personally, fix their problems over a call, do the unscalable work that a lifecycle
  journey will later automate. You're not building the Keep machine yet — you're learning,
  by hand, what it will eventually need to do.
* **Grow → founder-led distribution, not paid.** Launches, communities, writing, direct
  outreach, being useful in the places your users already are. Paid acquisition into a
  product with no proven retention is the most expensive way to learn you don't have
  product-market fit (the paid-traffic chapter says as much). Keep the tap off until the
  bucket holds a little water.

**The trap at this stage** is building the whole machine before anyone is using it — a
schema registry, eight lifecycle journeys, a paid funnel — because building feels like
progress and talking to five users feels like it isn't. It's the "instrumenting forever"
anti-pattern, moved earlier: the work that looks like growth work is quietly standing in
for the uncomfortable work of finding out whether anyone cares.

**What "good enough to start the real plan" looks like** is a pulse: a small group of
users who would be genuinely disappointed if the product vanished (the seed of the Sean
Ellis 40% test you'll run properly at day 180), and a retention curve — even over a dozen
users — that doesn't fall to zero. That's the retention flattening the leaky-bucket
chapter describes, expressed in miniature. The stages chapter maps these in full.

Once you have that pulse, start at [Day 0](./02-day-0-commit.mdx) and run the plan as
written. The phases are the same; now you have something real to measure.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Which version of the plan are you on?
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
The plan is one sequence, but you're not running it from a standing start unless you're
truly at zero. Most teams pick it up somewhere in the middle, and where you pick it up
changes how much time each phase deserves and what its exit gate should say. The order is
fixed; the *weighting* is yours.

* **Pre-launch / just launched.** Phase 1 is "instrument before launch", phase 3 is
  "launch channels", and paid barely features. You spend most of your energy in Measure
  and Keep, by hand (see [the zero-to-one version](./09-zero-to-one.mdx)). The day-30 gate
  isn't "trust the dashboard" so much as "we can see whether the first users reach value".
* **Early traction.** You have users and some retention; the machine is worth building
  properly. This is the case the plan's default timings assume — the four phases, roughly
  the pace given, with the gates read against real numbers.
* **Scaling a working channel.** You already have one channel that works and cash to
  spend. Measure and Keep are a tune-up, not a build; the real weight moves to the Compound
  phase — an experimentation program, proving incremental lift, and the discipline not to
  over-scale before the evidence supports it. Your gates are about *efficiency* — blended
  CAC (cost per acquired customer across all channels), **payback** (the months of margin
  it takes to earn that cost back), and lift — not existence.

The one rule that survives every stage: **don't skip a phase because your stage lets you
feel past it.** A scaling team that never verified its measurement is optimising on noise;
a just-launched team that jumps to ads is buying churn. The gates exist precisely to catch
the stage-appropriate version of that mistake.

Which stage you're in is worth getting right, because it sets which numbers your gates
read against for the next six months. The stages chapter goes deeper on each — the one
question that matters, the metric that lies, and what "good enough to advance" looks
like — if you want the fuller map before you settle on a weighting.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: Who's running this — and how the plan bends to fit
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
Growth is a hat before it's a hire — a role someone on the team wears, not a person you
recruit — so whoever wears it shapes how the plan actually runs. The sequence doesn't
change, but knowing where *you* will be slow lets you plan for it instead of stalling
there.

* **The solo / non-technical founder.** Your risk is the instrumentation phase: the
  measure step has real engineering in it. Lean on **autocapture** (PostHog's zero-config
  mode that records clicks and pageviews without you instrumenting each event) and no-code
  installs, both from the instrumentation chapter; keep the tracking plan to ten events,
  and treat "good enough to trust" as the bar, not "complete". Your advantage is the Keep
  phase — you can do the unscalable onboarding by hand, and you already know the customer.
  Delegate or contract the pipeline work; don't let it become the thing that stops you
  reaching the dashboard.
* **The technical cofounder.** Instrumentation is your comfort zone, which is exactly the
  trap: the "instrumenting forever" anti-pattern (from the Measure phase) is most seductive
  for engineers, because building the perfect event pipeline feels better than reading the
  imperfect one. Time-box Measure hard. Your real growth edge is that Keep and Grow are
  *engineering* here — journeys as code, holdouts, first-party tracking — so the leverage
  is in shipping the loop, not polishing the ingestion.
* **The first growth hire.** You've landed into someone else's product and data. Your first
  30 days are mostly learning — the funnel, the activation metric, what's already been
  tried — before you commit to projects; this chapter's Measure phase *is* your onboarding
  ([Reforge's first-90-days guide](https://www.reforge.com/guides/succeed-in-your-new-growth-leadership-role)
  is the professional version). Show up with your own version of this plan rather than
  waiting to be handed one, and bank a visible early win in activation or retention before
  reaching for acquisition.
* **The marketer learning to code.** Your instinct is acquisition; the plan deliberately
  makes you earn it. Your growth is in getting comfortable reading a retention curve and a
  holdout — the measurement literacy that turns "I ran campaigns" into "I run a loop"
  (the career chapter). The code you need for the Keep phase is smaller than it looks; the
  judgement is the hard part, and you already have half of it.

Whoever holds it, the plan works best when the *hard part for that person* is named out
loud and supported — a contractor for the pipeline, a time-box on instrumentation, a
mentor for the measurement — rather than left to quietly become the place the plan dies.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: The weekly growth review (your operating ritual)
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
The single most load-bearing commitment in the whole plan is the one that looks smallest:
the recurring 30-minute growth review you booked on Day 0. A plan is a document; a review
is a *ritual*, and the ritual is what the cadence chapter argues separates a growth team
from a team that ran a growth project once and moved on. Meetings that exist in the
calendar get held; intentions that live in a chapter don't.

Keep it to 30 minutes with a fixed agenda, so it survives busy weeks:

The "anomaly" line is where **cohorts** earn their place — a cohort is a group of users
bucketed by when they signed up, and watching one week's cohort retain (or not) over the
following weeks catches a decay that a topline average would smooth away.

Two disciplines keep it honest. **It's a review, not a status theatre** — the point is to
*decide* (keep going, change course, kill something), not to admire dashboards. And **the
losing experiments get airtime**, because a review where only wins are reported quietly
teaches the team to stop taking risks, and the learning rate is the thing that compounds
(the experiments chapter). A team that can name last week's most useful *failed* test without
embarrassment is running the loop the way it's meant to run.

The most useful thing a review can have is a standing opening question — one sentence the
meeting always starts with, that points everyone at the same goal. "Is the bucket holding?"
early on; "Is growth getting cheaper?" once the flywheel is turning. Pick yours.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: The experiment doc (a template you can reuse)
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
An experiment you can't write down on one screen isn't ready to run. The
[compounding phase](./06-days-91-180-compound.mdx) of this plan asks for a steady tempo
of experiments, each with a hypothesis, a primary metric, and a written result —
*including the losers* — and the way to make that routine rather than heroic is a
template small enough to fill in before you start and after you finish. The experiments
chapter covers the craft of running experiments well; this is the one-screen artifact you
reuse each time.

The fields that force clarity before you spend the traffic:

* **Hypothesis.** *We believe \[change] for \[audience] will cause \[outcome], because
  \[reasoning].* If you can't finish the sentence, the experiment isn't ready.
* **Primary metric + guardrails.** One metric decides it; a couple of guardrails make sure
  you didn't win it by breaking something else. Watching ten metrics means the winner is
  whichever one moved by chance.
* **Minimum detectable effect + how long it runs.** Decided *up front*, so you don't peek
  and stop the moment it looks good — an underpowered "win" is worse than no test
  (the experiments chapter).
* **Result + decision.** Written after: what happened, what you learned, and ship / kill /
  iterate. The losers are the point as much as the winners.

Which experiment to run first is a scoring question, not a loudest-voice question. Score
each idea by impact, confidence, and ease and pull from the top:

Then draft the experiment itself in the template — one screen, reused every time:

Keep the finished docs somewhere [the weekly review](./12-the-weekly-review.mdx) can see them. A backlog of
scored ideas and a shelf of written results — wins and losses — *is* the experimentation
program; everything else is just remembering to look at it.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: After 180 days — the next horizon
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
By day 180 the plan should have dissolved into an operating rhythm — growth work that
just happens week to week, without a checklist prompting it (the
[Compound phase](./06-days-91-180-compound.mdx) is where that starts). That's the finish
line, and it's also the start of the next stretch. Three moves define the six months
after:

* **Add the second channel — only once the first genuinely works.** A single working
  channel is a single point of failure, and platforms change their rules on their own
  schedule. But adding a channel before the first is measured and profitable just splits
  your attention across two things you don't understand yet. The signal to diversify is a
  channel with a stable, known CAC (cost per acquired customer) and payback period (the
  months of margin it takes to earn that cost back) — then you add the next one
  deliberately, and hold it to the same gate. Brian Balfour's
  [growth-loops framing](https://brianbalfour.com/four-fits-growth-framework) is the map for
  which channels actually fit your model rather than just being available.
* **Your first growth hire — now that you know what to hire for.** Having run the loop
  yourself, you can write the job description from experience and evaluate a candidate's
  answers against reality instead of buzzwords. First Round's
  [founder-led growth playbook](https://review.firstround.com/founder-led-growth-playbook/)
  makes the case that premature growth hires fail precisely because the founder skipped the
  loop and can't tell a good hire from a plausible one. Hire to *scale* the loop you
  understand, never to *skip* it.
* **Raise the tempo and defend the flywheel.** The experimentation program (the experiments chapter) is
  where compounding now lives — more tests, faster learning. And the owned-audience
  flywheel — the referral and owned-channel loop the owned-audience chapter builds —
  should be showing up in the finances: blended CAC falling as referrals and the owned
  channel grow. The job shifts from *building* the machine to *defending* it — keeping the
  culture and the loop intact as the team grows (the stages chapter's scaling stage is about
  exactly this).

What you care about also changes. The first 180 days were about *existence* — does the
data hold, does the bucket hold, does acquisition land in owned channels. From here it's
about *efficiency and defensibility*: is growth getting cheaper, is the moat (owned
audience, retention, referral) getting deeper, and can the machine survive more people
running it.

One more thing, and it's not about the company. Running this loop end to end is rare and
valuable — it's the difference between having done a growth project and being able to run a
growth program. That's career capital, and [Growth as career capital](../14-growth-as-career-capital) is
about what to do with it.

growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.title: 10 — Your 30/60/90/180-day plan
growth-with-posthog/10-your-30-60-90-180.text:
> **In this chapter:** the course turned into a plan you can actually run — day 0
> commitments, then four phases (measure, keep, grow, compound) each with a goal, a
> checklist, exit criteria, and the anti-patterns that blow it up. The checklists and
> writing exercises below are interactive: everything you tick or write saves to
> [your workbook](/workbook) and is here when you come back.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into fourteen short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read, and every checklist, calculator, and writing box you touch saves to
[your workbook](/workbook). A few pieces carry an operator's video — watch it (or read the
transcript underneath), then read the short digest so the point sticks. Work through them
in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [How to use this plan](./01-how-to-use.mdx) — phases are gates, not dates; protect the order.
2. [Which version are you on?](./10-which-plan-are-you-on.mdx) — the same plan, weighted by your stage.
3. [Who's running this](./11-role-based-plans.mdx) — how it bends to a founder, a cofounder, a first hire, a marketer.
4. [The zero-to-one version](./09-zero-to-one.mdx) — the pre-product-market-fit shape, if you have no users yet.
5. [Day 0 — commit](./02-day-0-commit.mdx) — one hour, no code: name the owner, agree the order.
6. [Days 1–30 — Measure](./03-days-1-30-measure.mdx) — see the truth about your funnel.
7. [Days 31–60 — Keep](./04-days-31-60-keep.mdx) — plug the biggest leaks before you spend on traffic.
8. [Days 61–90 — Grow](./05-days-61-90-grow.mdx) — open the tap into an audience you own.
9. [Days 91–180 — Compound](./06-days-91-180-compound.mdx) — turn the machine into a culture.
10. [The weekly growth review](./12-the-weekly-review.mdx) — the 30-minute ritual that keeps the plan alive.
11. [The experiment doc](./13-the-experiment-doc.mdx) — a reusable template, and how to score what to run next.
12. [What good looks like at each gate](./07-what-good-looks-like.mdx) — the four yes/no gate questions.
13. [After 180 days](./14-after-180-days.mdx) — the second channel, the first hire, defending the flywheel.
14. [Before you move on](./08-before-you-move-on.mdx) — commit to a start date, then the quiz.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter.** The plan leans on a handful of growth terms. Each
is defined again the moment it first appears, but here they are in one place:

* **North Star metric** — the single number that best captures the value your product delivers (weekly active teams, projects shipped); everything else is an input to it.
* **Holdout** — a slice of users you deliberately *don't* message, kept as a baseline so you can prove a journey caused a change rather than merely coinciding with one.
* **Incremental lift** — the extra outcome a message or journey caused over that held-out baseline. Opens and clicks are activity; lift is proof.
* **Blended CAC** — customer-acquisition cost averaged across every channel, paid and organic: total acquisition spend divided by all new customers.
* **Payback** — how many months of a customer's margin it takes to earn back what you spent acquiring them.
* **Cohort** — a group of users bucketed by something they share, usually the week they signed up, so you can watch one week's users retain over time.
* **Autocapture** — PostHog's zero-config mode that records clicks and pageviews automatically, without you instrumenting each event by hand.
* **CAPI (Conversions API)** — sending conversion events to an ad platform from your own server, so ad-blockers and cookie loss don't quietly drop them.
* **EMQ (Event Match Quality)** — Meta's 0–10 score for how confidently it can tie a conversion you send back to a real person on its platform.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Why stage matters
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
A growth lever isn't good or bad on its own — it's good or bad *for where your product
is right now*. Take paid acquisition. Point it at a product that already keeps its users
and you've built a machine that turns spend into a bigger active base. Run that same
spend a few months earlier, before the product retains anyone, and you're just buying
churn at ad-market prices. The lever didn't change — the *bucket* underneath it did: a
product that can't yet hold the users it wins leaks them straight back out.

That's the whole idea of this chapter. At every stage of a product's life, one problem
matters more than all the rest — the one that, left unsolved, turns work on everything
else into wasted motion. Pre-launch, it's whether anyone wants the thing at all. Just
after launch, it's whether new users reach value. Later, it's whether one channel
repeats. What to care about changes because that problem moves, and pointing your effort
at the last stage's problem is how a well-run team still stalls.

This is also why copying a later-stage company's playbook is a trap. The case study you
read — a Series B company's referral loop, its paid-and-lifecycle engine, its
growth-team org chart — is the answer to a problem that company had *at the time it wrote
the essay*. Run it against your problem and you spend the one thing you can't get back,
time, on the wrong work.

Two forces make this worse. The playbooks you can read are written by the companies that
survived — you never see the identical playbook that sank an earlier-stage startup. And
they're written after the fact, once the messy pre-fit years have been tidied into a
clean narrative. Paul Graham's
[*Do Things That Don't Scale*](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html) is the counter-argument
in one essay: the early playbook is *supposed* to look nothing like the scaled one.

So the honest first move isn't picking a tactic — it's naming where you actually are.

**What to take from it:**

* Front-load the "Goldilocks zone" — projects that are both high-impact *and* squarely
  inside your existing skill set — to bank a few quick wins before reaching for unfamiliar
  bets. The same logic as picking the lever that fits your stage, not the one that worked
  for someone else's.
* The plan is deliberately more prescriptive early (a learning roadmap: the product, the
  customers, the growth model) and looser later — you diagnose before you prescribe, the
  personal version of "name where you are before you pick a tactic."
* Guard capacity on purpose. The two things that derail a strong start are picking the
  wrong projects and saying yes to everything until you burn out — so sequence the work,
  don't run it all in parallel.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Idea and pre-launch: does anyone want this?
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
This is the very start — an idea, maybe a rough prototype, and no users yet. Because you
have no users, there's almost nothing quantitative worth measuring. A dashboard built now
just charts noise: ten signups from friends, a launch-list that clicked out of politeness.
The instinct to instrument early — to wire up analytics and start charting behaviour — is
the right one for *after* launch, once there's real usage to read; here it's mostly a way
to feel productive while dodging the only question that counts.

That question is simple: do **people actually want this?** Not "would this be cool" —
whether a real person has the problem badly enough to change what they do about it. The
way you find out is conversation: problem interviews, watching how people cope today, and
the strongest signal of all — someone already hacking a workaround, because a workaround
is demand that existed before you showed up.

Say you're building a tool that helps freelance designers chase late invoices. The
question isn't whether people nod when you describe it. It's whether a real designer is
already, today, nagging clients with a messy spreadsheet and a pile of calendar
reminders. That clumsy workaround is the demand — it means the problem hurts enough to
act on. Paul Graham's
[*How to Get Startup Ideas*](https://paulgraham.com/startupideas.html) is the reference
for finding a real problem like that rather than a plausible-sounding one.

The trap has two faces, and both are ways of not asking the question. One is building in
the dark: months of code, no conversations, a launch that meets a market that was never
there. The other is premature vanity metrics — treating landing-page signups or waitlist
size as proof of demand, when an email address costs nothing to give and predicts almost
nothing. CB Insights' running analysis of startup post-mortems repeatedly puts *"no
market need"* at or near the top of why startups die *(directional — the exact share
moves cohort to cohort, but the rank is stable)*.

You're good enough to advance when you can name the problem, name the person who has it,
and you've talked to enough of them that the demand is observed rather than assumed.
You're not looking for a statistically clean survey — you're looking for a handful of
people who'd be genuinely put out if your solution disappeared, and ideally one or two
using a rough version already. That's the ground the next stage stands on.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Just launched: hunt the activation signal
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
Launch turns the pre-launch question — *does anyone want this?* — into a sharper one you
can finally measure: do the people who show up reach the point where it's useful? That
point is **activation**, the first time a new user feels the product's value. It's the
one metric that matters now, and it's worth protecting from the two numbers that will try
to stand in for it.

A signup is a person raising their hand; an activation is a person actually getting
something out of the product. Early on, the gap between the two is enormous — and it's
exactly where nearly every launched product leaks. Say 500 people sign up in your first
week and only 40 ever reach the moment the product pays off: the number worth staring at
is the 40, not the 500. If you only watch one number this stage, watch the share of new
users who hit your first-value moment — and find that moment in your own product's data,
the action your best early users take before they stick, rather than borrowing someone
else's. You don't need to chart every step yet; you need this one step measured honestly.

Two traps sit right at launch, both tempting. The first is celebrating signups: the
launch-day spike feels like proof, but a spike of people who never come back is a vanity
metric with good timing. The second is over-instrumenting — freezing the launch to build
the perfect event schema, tracking forty actions when you can't yet read the one that
matters. That second trap bites double here: thirty events you don't look at are worth
less than one you act on.

You're good enough to advance when you have a repeatable activation moment — you can say
what it is, you capture it as an event, and a meaningful share of new users reach it
without you hand-holding each one. A few of them come back a second time unprompted. That
early return is the first faint signal of the thing the next stage is entirely about:
whether the product retains.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Finding product-market fit
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
Product-market fit — PMF — is the point where you stop having to push the product and the
market starts pulling it out of your hands. Everything before this stage was setting it
up; everything after it amplifies it, so it's worth slowing down and getting right.

The term was popularised by Marc Andreessen, who described it bluntly as the only thing
that matters before you have it, and the only thing worth working on until you do. The
hard part is that it's a feeling founders mostly describe in hindsight — *"the market
pulling the product out of the startup"* — so the useful move is to turn that feeling into
something you can measure. Here are three ways to read it, from the cheapest to the most
rigorous:

**1. The retention curve that flattens.** Plot the share of a signup cohort — everyone
who joined in the same week, tracked together — still active by week, and watch the shape.
If it decays to zero, no amount of acquisition saves it — you're refilling a bucket with
no bottom. If it *flattens above zero*, some group made the product a habit, and that
plateau is fit expressed as data. This is the reading you already have the instrument for.

**2. The Sean Ellis 40% test.** Survey users with one question — *"how would you feel if
you could no longer use this product?"* — and measure the share who answer *"very
disappointed."* Ellis found, across roughly a hundred startups, that products clearing
about **40%** tended to have the pull to grow, and those under it usually didn't
*(rule of thumb — a useful benchmark, not a law of physics)*. Run yours:

**3. Rahul Vohra's PMF engine.** Superhuman's founder turned that one survey into a
system: segment to the users who'd be very disappointed, learn what they value, and steer
the roadmap to convert the "somewhat disappointed" fence-sitters into them — raising the
40% number deliberately rather than waiting for it. His
[First Round essay](https://review.firstround.com/how-superhuman-built-an-engine-to-find-product-market-fit/)
is the fullest write-up of treating PMF as a process, not a lightning strike.

The trap here is the most expensive in the course: **scaling spend before fit.** Paid
acquisition amplifies whatever you already have. Point it at a product with real pull and
it compounds. Point it at one people can take or leave and it just buys churn faster — the
same leaky bucket, a product losing users about as fast as it wins them, now with a budget
bolted on. Fit is the gate that decides which of those you get — so keep the tap turned
down until it's real.

You're good enough to advance when the retention curve plateaus, ideally corroborated by
the 40% test, on a segment you can name. Not perfection everywhere — durable pull with
*someone*. That's what the next stage tries to make repeatable.

**What to take from it:**

* The felt signal: *with* fit, customers pull the product out of you — "beating a path to
  your door"; *without* it you're pushing a rock uphill, and users are too polite to say no
  while quietly not coming back. The retention curve is that feeling turned into data.
* Finding it is iteration, not inspiration. Weebly took 18 months, and Rusenko budgets for
  roughly *10× the iterations you expect* — so listen to customers' **problems**, not their
  proposed solutions, and keep burn low enough to afford that many loops.
* His fit signals line up with this atom's readings: returning cohort usage above all, then
  NPS — Net Promoter Score, the standard "would you recommend this?" measure — sitting
  around 40–50, then paying-renewal rates; and he's blunt that signups are a vanity number
  that flatters a product with no retention underneath it.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Early traction: make one channel repeat
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
With fit in hand, the job changes from *does this work?* to *can I make it happen again on
purpose?* Early traction is the search for repeatability: a way to bring in activated
users that you can run again next month and roughly predict the result. The temptation
here is to run everywhere at once, chasing every channel you've read a success story
about. The discipline of this stage is the opposite — go narrow before you go wide.

The metric that matters is **one channel that repeats** — not five channels dabbled in,
but one that reliably produces users who activate, at a cost you can live with. Brian
Balfour's [*channel/product fit*](https://brianbalfour.com/essays) argument is that
channels aren't interchangeable: a given product has a small number of channels that
actually suit it, and most of the ones that work for other companies won't work for you.
The book-length version is Weinberg and Mares' *Traction* and its Bullseye framework —
test many channels cheaply, then pour effort into the one that responds. The goal is a
single channel proven before you take on the complexity of a second.

Unit economics start to matter now. Pre-fit, cost-per-acquisition — CAC, what it costs in
marketing spend to win one customer — was noise; here it becomes a real constraint,
because "repeatable" only counts if you can afford to repeat it. Before you call a channel
scalable, it has to survive its own numbers:

The trap is channel-hopping. Trying six channels for two weeks each teaches you almost
nothing — every channel has a learning curve, and abandoning one before you've climbed it
looks like "it didn't work" when the truth is "I didn't finish." Too many simultaneous
bets also means no single channel gets the attention it needs to actually turn. Depth on
one beats breadth across many at this stage.

You're good enough to advance when you have one channel you can turn on and roughly
predict what comes out — activated users, at a CAC your model and runway can carry. Now
adding a second channel is a considered next step rather than a scramble, which is exactly
what scaling asks for.

**What to take from it:**

* His own cautionary tale is channel-hopping in miniature: he spent ~$5k on generic,
  top-of-funnel content *before* launch — the **funnel** being the path from first
  hearing of you down to buying, with "top" the strangers and "bottom" the nearly-sold —
  and got "crickets": not because content doesn't work, but because he had no framework
  for which content to make, so the effort scattered.
* The fix is depth-first. Pick the channel, then prioritise with the five stages of
  awareness and **start at the bottom** — the most-aware buyer (comparison pages,
  alternatives, case studies) — and work up. Bottom-of-funnel content converts soonest, so
  the channel pays back before you've climbed the whole funnel.
* A bonus of going deep on one channel: bottom-of-funnel content doubles as sales
  collateral — it earns its keep even with zero marketing, which is what "a channel that
  repeats" looks like once you've actually committed to it.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Scaling: build the machine
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
Scaling is the stage where growth stops being a run of one-off wins and turns into a
system that produces them on purpose. You already have one channel that repeats and a
product that keeps people. The work now is compounding what you've got — adding capacity
without adding chaos. Three things tend to arrive together here.

The first is a second channel. With one channel proven and its economics understood, a
second is a calculated diversification rather than a scramble — the earlier discipline of
going narrow was for *before* your first channel turned, and now one has. Two working
channels also reduce the risk that a single platform change — an algorithm tweak, a price
rise — takes your whole flow of new users with it.

The second is the first dedicated growth hire. Until now growth has been a hat someone
wears part-time. At scale the loop is big enough to be someone's whole job — owning the
acquisition-to-retention flow, the experiment backlog, and the lifecycle system end to
end. Hire *into* a working loop, not to go find one: a growth hire inherits a machine to
run faster, not a blank page to invent product-market fit on.

The third is an operating cadence. The thing that separates a team that scales from a
team that had a good quarter is ritual: a standing review, a scored experiment backlog,
and decisions that cite the numbers instead of the loudest voice. The cadence is what
keeps a growing team pointed at the same numbers (the later chapters on running growth
turn this into a weekly rhythm).

The trap is scaling chaos. Volume is a magnifier — it makes a good loop bigger and a
broken one break louder. Two specific failures show up here. The first is pouring spend on
top of a retention leak that fit *mostly* fixed but didn't — that's buying churn all over
again, just at a bigger scale. The second is losing the culture that got you this far: the
shared language, the retention-first instinct, the close contact with users, all of it
quietly thinning out under the weight of new people and new channels. The machine is only
ever as good as what it's built on.

Before you pour volume in, it's worth running down the signals that say the base is solid
— retention holding, one repeatable channel, and the unit economics stacking up, so a
customer's **lifetime value** (LTV, the total revenue they bring before they churn)
comfortably clears the cost of winning them:

There's no "advance" past scaling: the job just becomes keeping the machine honest as it
grows, which is what the later chapters on operating cadence and the day-by-day plan are
about.

**What to take from it:**

* The plan assumes you're hired *into* a working growth function, not to invent
  product-market fit. Month one is a learning roadmap — the growth model qualitatively and
  quantitatively, jobs-to-be-done, actually using the product — because you inherit a
  machine and learn it before you touch it.
* The days-30–60 output is the operating cadence this atom asks for: a "growth operations
  manual" defining team structure now and 12–18 months out, operating principles for
  decisions made when you're not in the room, and how the growth team's rituals differ from
  the rest of the company.
* By days 60–90 the job is to re-plan from evidence — revise the strategy, refine the model
  and the dashboards as reality corrects your first guesses. That's the "keep the machine
  honest as it grows" work scaling never finishes.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Metrics that lie by stage
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
A metric doesn't lie on its own — it lies *at a stage*, when it's the wrong thing to be
reading for the problem you're actually facing. The same signup count that's meaningless
at launch becomes a perfectly good measure of incoming interest later, once activation and
retention underneath it are solid. The skill is spotting which number is quietly standing
in for the one that actually matters.

The pattern runs the whole length of the arc — a different number tries to fool you at
each stage:

* **Pre-launch** — waitlist and landing-page signups pose as demand. But an email address
  is free to give, so a big list predicts almost nothing.
* **Just launched** — the signup count and the launch-day spike pose as traction. A spike
  of people who never return is activity with good timing, not a base.
* **Finding fit** — revenue can rise while the base churns underneath it. New sales mask a
  leaky bucket, so read retention alongside **MRR** — monthly recurring revenue, the
  predictable subscription income you book each month — never instead of it.
* **Early traction** — blended CAC (customer-acquisition cost — what one customer costs to
  win, averaged across every channel) folds cheap organic in with expensive paid, which
  makes paid look affordable when it isn't. Break CAC out by channel before you scale one.
* **At scale** — opens and engagement pose as proof a **journey** (an automated sequence of
  lifecycle messages, like an onboarding email series) works. But activity isn't lift: only
  a **holdout** — a slice of users deliberately kept out of the journey as a comparison
  group — lets you attribute any change to it.

Underneath all of them is Eric Ries' distinction from *The Lean Startup* between *vanity*
and *actionable* metrics. A vanity number tends to only go up (cumulative totals never
fall), can't be tied to a decision, and measures activity instead of an outcome.
Actionable numbers are rates, they're comparable over time, and they change what you'd
actually do next. When a chart makes you feel good but wouldn't change a decision, treat
that as a warning.

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth/.text:
The arc of this chapter is one idea, repeated at every stage: a different thing is the
problem that matters most, so what you should care about changes as you grow. And the
fastest way to stall is to run a later stage's playbook against an earlier stage's
problem. Each stage comes down to one honest question:

* **Idea and pre-launch** — *Does anyone actually want this, enough to change what they
  do?* The instrument is conversation, not a dashboard.
* **Just launched** — *Do the people who show up reach value, or do they just sign up?*
  Activation, not the signup count.
* **Finding product-market fit** — *Would my users be genuinely disappointed if this
  vanished — and does the retention curve flatten?* Pull, read as data.
* **Early traction** — *Can I turn on one channel and roughly predict what comes out?*
  Repeatability, held to its unit economics.
* **Scaling** — *Is the machine compounding on something solid, or is volume hiding a
  leak?* Capacity without chaos.

And running underneath all five, one more: *which stage am I actually in — and am I
solving its problem, or borrowing someone else's answer?*

***

## Go deeper

* [YC — Startup Library](https://www.ycombinator.com/library) — the founder-trusted playbooks, sortable by the stage you're in.
* [Paul Graham — Do Things That Don't Scale](https://paulgraham.com/ds.html) — why the early-stage playbook is *supposed* to look nothing like the scaled one.
* [Paul Graham — Startup = Growth](https://paulgraham.com/growth.html) — what "growth" actually means, and why stage frames every decision about it.
* [Paul Graham — How to Get Startup Ideas](https://paulgraham.com/startupideas.html) — finding a real problem worth the pre-launch stage.
* [Sean Ellis — the PMF survey](https://pmfsurvey.com/) — the 40% test that gates the tap, from the source.
