* [Rahul Vohra — how Superhuman built an engine to find product-market fit](https://review.firstround.com/how-superhuman-built-an-engine-to-find-product-market-fit/) — treating PMF as a repeatable process, not a lightning strike.
* [Brian Balfour — essays](https://brianbalfour.com/essays) — channel/product fit and why the channel that worked for another company probably won't work for you.
* [Lenny's Newsletter](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/) — operator essays across every stage, including how to find your activation metric.
* Marc Andreessen — "The Only Thing That Matters" (2007) — the essay that popularised the term product-market fit (widely archived; search the title).
* Gabriel Weinberg & Justin Mares — *Traction* — the Bullseye framework for testing many channels cheaply and committing to the one that responds.

If you'd rather go deeper than a link, here's the shelf behind this chapter — tick them
off as you read:

growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth.title: Stages of growth: what to care about when
growth-with-posthog/11-stages-of-growth.text:
> **In this chapter:** why a growth lever that helps at one stage hurts at another,
> the five stages a product moves through — idea, just-launched, finding
> product-market fit, early traction, and scaling — and for each one the single
> question worth answering, the one metric that matters, the trap most teams fall
> into, and what "good enough to advance" looks like. Then a synthesis of the
> metrics that mislead at every stage. By the end you'll be able to name where you
> actually are, and stop borrowing a later-stage company's answers to questions you
> don't have yet.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into eight short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. The middle five walk the stages in order; the shape repeats each
time (the question, the metric, the trap, the advance criteria) so the pattern sticks.
Work through them in order, or jump to the stage you're in:

1. [Why stage matters](./01-why-stage-matters.mdx) — the same lever helps here and hurts there.
2. [Idea and pre-launch](./02-idea-and-pre-launch.mdx) — one question, almost no dashboards.
3. [Just launched](./03-just-launched.mdx) — hunt the activation signal, not the signup count.
4. [Finding product-market fit](./04-finding-pmf.mdx) — the flattening curve and the 40% test.
5. [Early traction](./05-early-traction.mdx) — one channel that works before you add a second.
6. [Scaling](./06-scaling.mdx) — the machine, the first hire, and keeping the loop.
7. [Metrics that lie by stage](./07-metrics-that-lie-by-stage.mdx) — which number to distrust when.
8. [Before you move on](./08-before-you-move-on.mdx) — the honest question per stage, then a quiz.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter.** A handful of terms recur across the stages —
here they are in one place, though each is also defined where it first appears:

* **Activation** — the first time a new user reaches the product's value, pinned to a specific action inside a time window.
* **Product-market fit (PMF)** — the point where the market starts pulling the product out of your hands instead of you having to push it.
* **Cohort** — a group of users tracked together because they joined in the same period (a signup week, say).
* **CAC (customer-acquisition cost)** — what it costs in marketing and sales spend to win one paying customer.
* **LTV (lifetime value)** — the total revenue a customer brings before they leave; healthy when it comfortably clears CAC.
* **MRR (monthly recurring revenue)** — the predictable subscription income you book each month.
* **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** — the standard "how likely are you to recommend this?" measure, scored −100 to +100.
* **Holdout** — a group deliberately left out of a change so its true effect can be measured against them.
* **Journey** — an automated sequence of lifecycle messages, like an onboarding email series, triggered by what a user does.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: What deserves an experiment
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
An experiment is not free. It costs **traffic** — you split it, so each variant learns
slower and you need more users to reach an answer. It costs **time** — design, build,
wait for a result you can trust, read. And it costs **opportunity** — the change you
didn't ship while this one ran. Testing is a way to *buy certainty* about a decision, and
like anything you buy, it's only worth it when the certainty is worth more than the price.

Two questions decide it:

* **Is the change cheap and reversible?** Then ship it. If a clearer button label is
  obviously better and trivial to roll back, a two-week test to confirm it is time you
  won't get back — and at startup traffic the effect is probably too small to detect
  anyway (the power piece works through why small effects are so expensive to prove).
* **Is the decision expensive to get wrong, and hard to undo?** Then test it. Pricing
  changes, removing an onboarding step, a new activation flow — these are the decisions
  where being wrong costs months, and where certainty is worth paying for.

Lenny's [guide to when *not* to run an experiment](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment)
is a good checklist: ship without a test when the change is obviously good, too small to
measure at your traffic, or easily reversed; test when the stakes and the uncertainty are
both high.

There's a humbling reason to test the expensive ones at all: you're bad at predicting
winners, and so is everyone else. At Microsoft, only about **one-third of experiments moved the metric
they were designed to move** in the intended direction — another third did nothing, and a
third made things worse (Ronny Kohavi's data from Bing/Microsoft) *(well-attested)*. The
famous example is a Bing ad-headline change one engineer left in the backlog for months
because it looked trivial; it turned out to be worth roughly **$100M a year** (Kohavi,
["The Surprising Power of Online Experiments," HBR](https://hbr.org/2017/09/the-surprising-power-of-online-experiments))
*(well-attested)*. If experts guessed right, you wouldn't need the test. The point of
experimentation isn't to rubber-stamp good ideas — it's to catch the two-thirds that
would have quietly cost you.

So the rule isn't "test everything." It's: ship the cheap, reversible, obvious changes
fast, and spend your limited testing capacity on the decisions that are expensive to get
wrong and where your intuition genuinely might be wrong.

**What to take from it:**

* Intuition fails *more* the harder you optimise — about two-thirds of ideas miss at
  Microsoft, ~85% at Bing, 92% at Airbnb search — which is precisely why the expensive
  decisions earn a test.
* Whether to test comes down to sample size: below tens of thousands of users the
  statistics see only large effects and the "magic" starts around 200k, so a small startup
  tests big swings rather than button colours — and a one-time, irreversible call can't be
  A/B tested at all.
* Two habits fall out of the same humility: don't ship on a flat result (no difference
  still adds code to maintain, so it's a no-ship, not a free launch), and trust Twyman's
  law — a result that looks too good is probably a bug worth investigating before you
  celebrate.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: The hypothesis format
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
An idea like "let's redesign onboarding" isn't testable — there's no prediction in it. A
**hypothesis** is the same idea written so reality can prove it wrong. The format that
forces this:

> **We believe** \[change] **for** \[audience] **will cause** \[outcome], **measured by**
> \[metric], **because** \[reasoning].

Each slot does work:

* **change** — the specific thing you'll build. If you can't name it in one line, it
  isn't ready to test.
* **audience** — who sees it: new signups? mobile users? one segment? A change can win
  for one group and lose for another, so naming the audience keeps you honest about which
  you're measuring.
* **outcome** — the behaviour you expect to shift, *with a direction*. "Increases" or
  "decreases," not "affects."
* **metric** — the single number that will settle it. Naming it before you build is what
  stops you picking, afterwards, whichever number happens to look good.
* **because** — your reasoning. This is the most valuable slot: it's the *belief* being
  tested, so when the result comes back you learn whether the belief was right — not just
  whether the button moved.

Filled in, it should read like a sentence you could turn out to be wrong about: *We
believe adding a progress bar to onboarding, for new signups, will cause more of them to
finish setup, measured by the setup-completion rate, because seeing how close they are
pulls them through the last steps.* Now there's a real prediction on the table — and
something to learn whichever way the result lands.

Writing the "because" first is what separates a program from a pile of A/B tests. A test
pinned to a belief still teaches you something when it fails; a test with no reasoning
leaves you holding a number and no idea what it meant. Teams that skip the reasoning tend
to run the same failed experiment twice, because nobody wrote down *why* they thought it
would work.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: One primary metric, and guardrails
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
The most important choice about a test happens before it runs: what would count as
success? The discipline is to answer with exactly *one* metric. Kohavi calls this the **Overall Evaluation Criterion (OEC)** — the single measure the
experiment is optimising, agreed up front. One metric, chosen in advance, is what stops
the after-the-fact hunt for whichever number happens to look good.

The trap is watching everything. If you track fifteen metrics and declare victory when
*any* of them is up, you'll "win" almost every test — some metric is always up by chance.
More metrics feels more rigorous; it's the opposite.

But you can't watch *only* the primary metric, because a change that lifts your target can
quietly wreck something else. That's what **guardrail metrics** are for: a short list of
things the test is not allowed to break, whatever it does to the primary. Common
guardrails are revenue, unsubscribe/opt-out rate, latency and error rates, support
tickets, and core retention. A new onboarding flow that lifts **activation** — the share
of new users who reach first real value — but doubles the unsubscribe rate hasn't won; the
guardrail caught a bad trade before you shipped it.

Two more properties of a good primary metric:

* **Sensitive enough to move.** Revenue and 30-day retention are true but slow — they
  barely budge inside a two-week test. Pick a leading indicator you can actually detect
  moving: activation rate, a key action in week one, the step-completion you're targeting.
* **Tied to the hypothesis.** The primary metric is the "measured by" slot of your
  hypothesis. If the change is about onboarding, the primary metric is an onboarding metric
  — not a downstream number the change only touches faintly, where any movement is mostly
  noise.

One metric that decides, a few that protect: that's the whole discipline, and it's worth
writing down before the test starts rather than after the result tempts you.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Power and sample size
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
**Statistical power** is the chance a test *detects* a real effect when one genuinely
exists. It's the least intuitive number in experimentation and the one that quietly wrecks
small-team tests: a test at low power is a smoke detector with a flat battery — the thing
can be happening and the alarm stays silent.

Underpowered tests are the quiet failure mode of small-team experimentation, and they fail
in two ways. They miss real wins — reporting "no difference" when there really was one.
And they hand you **false wins**: results that look significant but won't replicate. Acting
on a false win is worse than running no test at all, because you ship the change *and*
trust it.

Three levers set how much traffic you need:

* **Baseline rate** — where the metric starts.
* **Minimum detectable effect (MDE)** — the smallest change you care to catch. This one
  dominates: required sample grows with roughly the *square* of how small an effect you
  want to see, so detecting a 1% lift needs about **four times** the traffic of detecting
  a 2% lift *(well-attested — it's arithmetic, not opinion)*. Small effects are enormously
  expensive to prove.
* **Confidence and power** — the two thresholds you fix in advance: conventionally 95%
  confidence (you'll accept a one-in-twenty chance of calling a win that isn't real) and
  80% power.

The practical consequence for a startup: you usually can't detect small effects, so **test
big swings** — whole onboarding flows, whole lifecycle journeys, pricing — not button
colours. And
decide the sample size and run length *before* you start, with a calculator like
[Evan Miller's](https://www.evanmiller.org/ab-testing/sample-size.html), so "how long do
we run this?" has an answer that isn't "until it looks significant."

The cardinal sin of all this is **peeking**, and it's worth starting with the two words
the problem turns on. A **p-value** is the probability of seeing a result at least this
large if the change did nothing at all; a result is **statistically significant** when
that probability falls below a line you set in advance — conventionally 5%, written
p \< 0.05. The temptation is to watch a running test and stop the moment it crosses that
line, and doing so badly inflates your false-positive rate: a test with *no* real effect
will touch significance surprisingly often if you check it every day. The p-value is only
trustworthy at the sample size you committed to. Pick the finish line first, then look when
you reach it. Evan Miller's
["How Not to Run an A/B Test"](https://www.evanmiller.org/how-not-to-run-an-ab-test.html)
shows exactly how much peeking distorts the result.

**What to take from it:**

* False wins are the rule, not the exception. Kohavi's headline Bing metric — sessions per
  user — improves in only about 1 in 5,000 attempts, so because a genuine move is that rare,
  roughly 99.6% of the *significant* (p \< 0.05) results on it are false positives. His
  certification committee's standing line: don't celebrate one significant result, replicate
  it first.
* Sample-ratio mismatch is the trust-killer he checks before anything else — the single most
  common alarm at Microsoft. A test built to split traffic 50/50 that comes back even
  slightly lopsided (his live example: 821,588 vs 815,582 users, about a 1-in-500,000
  imbalance by chance) has a flaw in its execution, and no p-value from it can be trusted.
* Peeking and ramp-ups distort the answer directly: combine data while the treatment's
  traffic share is still changing and a winner can flip to a loser (Simpson's paradox). The
  discipline is to fix the finish line first, run A/A tests to sanity-check the system — they
  should "fail" about 5% of the time, and one vendor's once failed 30%, exposing a broken
  engine — and remember Twyman's law: a result that looks amazing is usually a bug.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Holdouts and incrementality
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
An A/B test compares variant A against variant B of a *single* change. A **holdout** asks
something broader: is this whole *program* producing anything at all? The program here is
your **lifecycle program** — the standing set of automated messages that reach users as
they move through their life with the product: the welcome sequence, the activation
nudges, the win-back **journeys** (a journey being one automated message sequence
triggered by what a user does). A holdout keeps a random slice of users out of all of it —
no lifecycle emails, no journeys, nothing — and compares them to everyone who got the full
treatment. The gap between the two groups is **incremental lift**: the outcome that
happened *because* of the program and wouldn't have happened anyway.

This is why **opens are activity; lift is proof.** A 62% open rate tells you the email was
sent and rendered. It tells you nothing about whether the program *changed* anyone's
behaviour — some of those users would have come back regardless. Only a held-out group,
living without the program, shows you the **counterfactual** — what would have happened
anyway. Without it you can't tell "the program worked" apart from "these were people who'd
have converted regardless."

Two flavours worth naming:

* **A/B test** — for a single change. It runs for a while, you ship the winner, and the
  test ends.
* **Global holdout** — a small, *persistent* control (often 1–5% of users) held out of the
  entire lifecycle program continuously, so at any moment you can read the program's total
  incremental contribution. It turns your standing report from "we send a lot of email"
  into one honest line: incremental lift this month, not open rates.

The cost is real and worth saying out loud: a holdout means deliberately *not* treating
some users, which feels like leaving money on the table. It isn't — it's the price of
knowing whether the program pays for itself. A team that can't say what its lifecycle work
adds *over doing nothing* is flying blind, however good the open rates look.
Incrementality is what turns "we send a lot of email" into "the program is worth $X a
month," and only the second sentence survives a budget review.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Reading results honestly
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
Getting a number is easy; not fooling yourself with it is the hard part — and the ways to
fool yourself are well-catalogued. The first is the **multiple-comparisons trap**: test
enough metrics or enough variants and something will look significant by pure chance,
because at 95% confidence roughly one in twenty comparisons is a false positive even when
nothing is going on. Slice a result twenty ways — "it didn't work overall, but look, it
won for left-handed users in Canada" — and you'll always find a winner. XKCD
[drew this perfectly](https://xkcd.com/882/) with green jelly beans. The defence is the one
primary metric you committed to before the test: it decides the result, and everything
else is a hypothesis for *next* time, not a victory to claim now.

The same disease wears other names. Peeking at a running test, dropping "bad" days,
redefining the metric after the data lands, running until significant — all of it is
**p-hacking**, and all of it manufactures wins. The rule is to fix the metric, the
audience, the run length, and the success bar *before* the test, then read only what you
agreed to read. Kohavi's **Twyman's law** is the right reflex: any figure that looks
surprisingly good is probably wrong until you've ruled out a bug or a fluke.

The losers deserve as much attention as the winners, and there will be more of them. A
flat or negative result is not a wasted test — it's most of what you'll get, since only
about a third of ideas win *(well-attested)*. "No detectable difference" tells you the
effect is smaller than you hoped, which is a real finding: it saves you from shipping the
change and believing it did something. The losers feed the backlog, too — *why* didn't it
move: wrong audience, too small, or a bad belief?

Every test then ends in one of three decisions, and the discipline is to name it out loud:
**ship** it (a clear win, guardrails intact), **kill** it (flat or negative), or
**iterate** (promising but inconclusive — sharpen the change and rerun). Then record it:
hypothesis, result, decision, what you'd try next. The write-up costs thirty seconds;
re-running a forgotten experiment costs a month. And a documented failure is the one kind
of failure a growth team can say out loud without embarrassment.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Tempo and the backlog
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
The difference between a team that A/B tests and a team with a *program* is two rituals: a
scored backlog and a steady tempo.

The first ritual is the backlog. Every growth idea — a new journey, a pricing change, an
onboarding step removed — goes into one list as a *hypothesis*, not a task. Score each on
**[ICE](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model)** — impact, confidence,
ease, each 1–10 — and let the scores order the list.
[PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/) (potential, importance, ease) is
the same idea with different letters; either works, and the only real mistake is arguing
frameworks instead of running tests. The score's job isn't precision — it's to make "this
would be huge" meet "how confident are we, actually?", and to give the weekly meeting a
top of the list to pull from instead of the loudest idea in the room.

The second ritual is tempo. Sean Ellis — who coined "growth hacking" and popularised the
**North Star metric** (the single number that best captures the core value a product
delivers) — argues the compounding asset is your **velocity of tests**. More shots on goal
means faster learning, and the learning is what compounds even when individual tests fail
([high-tempo testing](https://www.saastr.com/sean-ellis-founder-ceo-of-growthhackers-building-a-company-wide-growth-culture-video-transcript/)).
Reforge's [experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system)
puts a number on it — a standard template plus a visible backlog roughly tripled one
team's testing velocity *(directional)*. For a startup, "high tempo" might mean one
experiment a week. That's fine: one a week, every week, with results written down, beats a
burst of ten followed by a quarter of nothing.

The thing that holds it together is a 30-minute **weekly review**: groom the backlog,
launch the top-scored test, read last week's, record the decision.

**What to take from it:**

* Ellis leads with the Amazon framing — Bezos: success is "a function of how many
  experiments we run per day, per month, per year." Volume of tests, not the brilliance of
  any one, is the thing that compounds.
* The mistake he warns against is obsessing over one "perfect" test. Because nobody
  reliably knows which idea wins, three-to-five smaller shots a week beat betting the
  quarter on a single swing — the shots-on-goal (Gretzky) logic behind this atom's tempo.
* Velocity only pays off when it's aimed. He starts every cycle with qualitative analysis
  of *who loves the product and why*, so the backlog is fed by real understanding of value,
  not random ideas — which is the ICE-scored hypothesis list this atom builds.

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
Two ideas hold this chapter together. First, experiments are a *cost* — traffic, time,
opportunity — so you ship the cheap and reversible changes and save testing for decisions
where being wrong is costly and your intuition might genuinely be mistaken. Second, a test
is only worth running if you can trust the answer, which means: one hypothesis, one primary
metric with guardrails, enough power to see the effect, a control that proves
incrementality, and an honest read that resists the traps. Wrap those in a weekly tempo,
and the learning rate — not any single win — is what compounds.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [Ronny Kohavi on Lenny's Podcast — the ultimate guide to A/B testing](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing) — trust, invalid results, and when not to test at all, from the person who ran experimentation at Microsoft, Amazon, and Airbnb. ([Spotify](https://open.spotify.com/episode/3vD3c87FT7RKnvZUJRwOb4), [YouTube](https://youtu.be/hEzpiDuYFoE))
* *Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments* (Ronny Kohavi, Diane Tang & Ya Xu) — the standard reference on running A/B tests you can believe: the OEC, guardrails, the peeking problem, Twyman's law, and sample-ratio mismatch.
* [Kohavi — "The Surprising Power of Online Experiments," HBR](https://hbr.org/2017/09/the-surprising-power-of-online-experiments) — the Bing example and the hard evidence that experts can't predict winners.
* [Reforge — the growth experiment management system](https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-experiment-management-system) — backlog, template, and velocity metrics that turn occasional tests into a program.
* [Lenny's Newsletter — when *not* to run an experiment](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/not-running-an-experiment) — the checklist for shipping without a test.
* [Sean Ellis on high-tempo testing](https://www.saastr.com/sean-ellis-founder-ceo-of-growthhackers-building-a-company-wide-growth-culture-video-transcript/) — why the velocity of tests is the compounding asset.
* [ProductPlan — the ICE scoring model](https://www.productplan.com/glossary/ice-scoring-model) and [Conversion.com — PIE](https://conversion.com/framework/pie-framework/) — two ways to score the backlog; pick one and stop debating.
* [Evan Miller — "How Not to Run an A/B Test"](https://www.evanmiller.org/how-not-to-run-an-ab-test.html) and his [sample-size calculator](https://www.evanmiller.org/ab-testing/sample-size.html) — the peeking problem, and deciding run length up front.

And the books, if you want the craft in depth — tick them off as you read:

growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.title: Running experiments well
growth-with-posthog/12-running-experimen.text:
> **In this chapter:** which changes earn the fixed cost of a test and which you should
> just ship, the hypothesis format that turns an opinion into a claim reality can prove
> wrong, the one primary metric and the guardrails that protect the rest of the
> business, why an
> underpowered "win" is worse than no test at all, holdouts and the incrementality they
> prove, how to read results without fooling yourself, and the ICE-scored backlog and
> weekly tempo that make the learning rate — not any single win — the thing that
> compounds.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into eight short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. The first six build the discipline of a single trustworthy
experiment; the last two turn it into a weekly habit and check you on the whole thing.
Work through them in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [What deserves an experiment](./01-what-deserves-an-experiment.mdx) — every test has a fixed cost; not every change earns it.
2. [The hypothesis format](./02-the-hypothesis-format.mdx) — the sentence that forces clarity before you build.
3. [Primary metric and guardrails](./03-primary-metric-and-guardrails.mdx) — one number to move, a few you refuse to break.
4. [Power and sample size](./04-power-and-sample-size.mdx) — why an underpowered win is worse than no test.
5. [Holdouts and incrementality](./05-holdouts-and-incrementality.mdx) — opens are activity; lift is proof.
6. [Reading results honestly](./06-reading-results-honestly.mdx) — p-hacking, the losers, and ship/kill/iterate.
7. [Tempo and the backlog](./07-tempo-and-the-backlog.mdx) — an ICE-scored ritual, because learning rate compounds.
8. [Before you move on](./08-before-you-move-on.mdx) — recap, flashcards, and a quiz to check yourself.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter**

* **hypothesis** — a change written so reality can prove it wrong: a change, an audience, an expected outcome, a metric, and the reasoning behind it.
* **primary metric (OEC)** — the single number, fixed before the test, that decides whether it won; Kohavi's Overall Evaluation Criterion.
* **guardrail metric** — a number the test isn't allowed to break, whatever it does to the primary — revenue, unsubscribe rate, latency.
* **statistical power** — the chance a test detects a real effect when one exists; low power both misses wins and manufactures false ones.
* **minimum detectable effect (MDE)** — the smallest change you care to catch; halve it and you need roughly four times the traffic.
* **p-value / statistical significance** — the odds a result this large is pure chance; "significant" means those odds sit below a line you set first (usually 5%).
* **holdout** — a slice of users kept out of the whole program, so the gap to everyone else reveals what the program actually added.
* **incrementality** — the outcome that happened because of the program and wouldn't have happened anyway.
* **ICE** — a backlog score — impact, confidence, ease, each 1–10 — used to order which experiment to run next.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Who cares about what
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
The first skill in talking about growth is realising you're not talking to one audience.
Four groups will ask "how's growth going," and each means something different by the
question. Answer them all the same way and you'll bore three of them and mislead the
fourth.

* **Investors and the board.** They operate at the highest altitude: a handful of
  *outcome* metrics that predict whether the company is worth more than last quarter —
  growth rate; retention, and the **net revenue retention** (the revenue you keep and
  expand from existing customers, after churn) underneath it; **CAC payback** and
  **LTV:CAC** — how many months of margin it takes to earn back the **CAC**
  (customer-acquisition cost), and how that cost compares to a customer's **LTV**
  (lifetime value); and burn and runway, how fast the cash goes out and how long it lasts.
  Andreessen Horowitz's [16 startup metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/)
  is the canonical list of what this audience reads, and the thing it repeats is *know
  which number you mean* — **GMV** (gross merchandise value, the total worth of goods sold
  through a marketplace) is not revenue, cumulative is not active. A board update is a
  trend and a few ratios with one narrative, not a tour of your dashboard.
* **Cofounders.** They read at exactly your altitude: they want the whole picture — the
  leak you're working on, the bet you're making, the thing that isn't working yet. This is
  the one audience you can be completely unpolished with, and the one where hiding a
  problem does the most damage, because they'll act on the rosy version too.
* **The team.** They care about the numbers they can actually move *this week* — the
  input metrics: **activation rate** (the share of new users who reach first value),
  journey lift, experiment results, the funnel step someone owns. An outcome like **MRR**
  (monthly recurring revenue) is motivating once a quarter and useless as a weekly target,
  because no one can point to the line of work that moves it directly.
* **Customers.** They do not care about your growth numbers at all. They care about the
  outcome *for them*. "10,000 teams use us" reaches a customer only as a trust signal —
  shorthand for "this is safe to rely on." The same number is doing a completely
  different job than it does on a board slide.

The mistake that quietly costs you credibility is **writing one report for all four.**
Send the board your weekly input metrics and they can't find the signal; give the team
the board's tidy outcome summary and they've nothing to act on; show a customer either
and you've bored them. It's a **dashboard graveyard** in a new form — the right data,
pointed at the wrong audience, changing no one's mind.

The fix is a habit: before you pick a single number, decide the audience's *altitude* and
the *one thing they're trying to learn*. Say your board asks how growth is going — the
altitude is high, and the one thing they want to know is whether the company is worth more
than last quarter, so you lead with the trend and a couple of ratios, not this week's
activation experiments.

The same move — choose the audience, then the one thing that lands for them — is what good
*positioning* does for a customer, and April Dunford has the sharpest version of it.

**What to take from it:**

* Good positioning isn't whether *you* get the pitch — it's whether the right buyer lands
  on it and thinks *of course, I need that*; the same test as framing a number for the
  board versus the team.
* Weak positioning shows up as *misalignment*, founder and sales and product each telling
  the story slightly differently — the mirror image of this atom's warning, where one
  story stretched across four audiences serves none of them.
* "Better" means nothing without "better for whom," so you name the value and the audience
  it's for together — the same pairing that makes each of this chapter's reports land.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: The two-number story
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
A single number is easy to argue with, because on its own it can't show cause.
"Activation is 44%" invites *so what?* — up or down from what, and did anything you did
move it? The unit that actually persuades is a **pair**: a *leading* metric you control,
and the *lagging* outcome it's supposed to move.

* A **leading** metric is an input — something a person on the team can act on this week.
  Onboarding completion, welcome-journey lift, invites sent per new user. It moves
  quickly, and it's yours to change.
* A **lagging** metric is an outcome — retention, revenue, the North Star. It moves
  slowly, it's what the business is actually made of, and it isn't directly movable: you
  reach it *through* the inputs. Your **North Star** — the single metric that reflects
  delivered value — is the lagging number you work on by moving the 3–5 input metrics
  beneath it, now pointed at how you *talk* about it.

The story lives in the gap between them. "We shipped the onboarding checklist;
completion went from 51% to 68% (leading, controllable), and week-4 retention on that
cohort is +3 points (lagging, the outcome we care about)." That sentence persuades
because it names the thing you did, the input it moved, and the outcome that followed — a
mechanism, not a coincidence. One number can't carry a mechanism; two, with the causal
link stated between them, can.

It also protects you both ways. A leading metric that moved with no lagging follow-through
yet is an honest "not proven yet" — you did the work, the outcome hasn't landed, keep
watching. A lagging metric that moved with no leading metric behind it is a *warning*: you
can't explain why, so you can't repeat it, and you shouldn't take credit. a16z's rule for
the board version is [know exactly which metric you mean](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/).
The same discipline sits underneath the two-number story: the pair is only honest if each
half is defined precisely enough to check.

The habit worth dropping is presenting the outcome alone as proof of your work.
"Revenue is up" is true and unattributable. "We did X, the input moved, revenue followed"
is a claim you can stand behind — or be proven wrong on, which is exactly the point of
making it.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Two cadences, two reports
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Four audiences sort into two standing reports, each on its own rhythm — a weekly one and a
monthly one. Keeping them separate is what stops the weekly meeting from ballooning and the
board update from drowning in detail.

The weekly team review is the operational one: thirty minutes, a fixed agenda, input
metrics and live work — the weekly growth ritual itself. Its job is *decisions and learnings
this week*, not status. Brian Balfour's [weekly growth meeting](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/growth-meeting),
run at HubSpot and Reforge, is the template worth compressing: anomalies get an owner
rather than a live group debugging session, because group debugging is how a 30-minute
meeting becomes a two-hour one.

The monthly or board update is the outcomes document: an hour to write, once a month
(quarterly for the board) — the monthly zoom-out. It carries outcomes and trend, not this
week's inputs: the **North Star** trend (the one metric that best proxies the value users
actually get from you), retention, the incremental lift from the lifecycle program — its
**global holdout**, a random slice of users held back from every lifecycle message so the
gap between them and everyone else shows what the program actually added — the **blended
CAC** (what a customer costs on average across all channels, paid and free) and the
referral loop's contribution, and exactly one learning worth the reader's attention. A
board update is a **trend and a handful of ratios with one narrative**, never a data dump.

The two reports share almost no rows, on purpose. If your board update reads like your
weekly review, you've handed the highest-altitude audience the lowest-altitude data — and
asked them to find the signal you were supposed to find for them. The next pieces are how
to make the few numbers that survive to the board update honest ones.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: How do you know it worked?
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Sooner or later someone — an investor, a skeptical cofounder, a numbers-literate
customer — asks the one question that separates a growth team from a marketing team: *how
do you know it worked?* How do you know the welcome journey *caused* the retention bump,
rather than those users being the kind who'd have stuck around anyway?

The honest answer is a **holdout** — a group you deliberately leave untouched, as a
comparison. The skill here is saying it without a statistics lecture, because the moment
you reach for "p-values" and "confidence intervals" you've lost the room. The version that
lands is plain and concrete:

> "Of everyone who qualified for the welcome journey, we held back a random slice — say
> 10% — and sent them nothing. Everyone else got the emails. A month later the messaged
> group had upgraded at 18%, the held-back group at 11%. That 7-point gap is what the
> journey added, because the two groups were identical except for the emails."

Three moves make that explanation work:

* **Lead with the control group, not the math.** The intuition people already have is the
  drug-trial one: some patients get the placebo. "We kept a group who got nothing, on
  purpose" is a sentence anyone follows. Reforge and Eppo call this *incrementality*; your
  stakeholder doesn't need the word, they need the placebo.
* **Name the gap as the credit.** Not "the journey has an 18% conversion rate" (that's the
  messaged group alone, and it includes people who'd have converted anyway) but "the
  journey *added* 7 points over doing nothing." The incremental number is smaller and far
  more defensible — [Eppo's incrementality primer](https://www.geteppo.com/blog/marketing-incrementality-101)
  is the fuller version for anyone who wants it.
* **Say what would have changed your mind.** "If the held-back group had converted at the
  same rate, we'd have killed the journey." A stakeholder trusts a measurement far more
  once they hear it *could* have come out against you — that's the tell that you weren't
  just building a case.

The same logic runs at the scale of a whole ad channel, not just one email journey — which
is where the paid-media version makes the intuition impossible to miss.

**What to take from it:**

* The paid-media version of the same holdout: a *geo holdout* turns media off in a few
  matched regions, leaves it on everywhere else, and reads the channel's true contribution
  from the revenue gap — a controlled experiment, not attribution laid over history.
* Reported and *incremental* **ROAS** (return on ad spend — revenue divided by what you
  spent to earn it) routinely diverge; in their example Meta was under-reporting, while
  Google brand search's incremental value was only ~23% of its reported number, the
  platform taking credit for buyers who'd have converted anyway.
* The detail that defuses a nervous stakeholder: the held-back spend isn't burned but
  reallocated to the live regions, so the test costs almost nothing while buying a causal
  read.

Why rehearse this conversation at all? Because it's the whole distance between "we sent
3,400 emails" (activity) and "the program added 7 points of activation, measured against a
control" (proof). The first invites more questions; the second ends the debate — which is
exactly what you want a report to do.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Presenting a loss
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Most experiments lose. Ronny Kohavi, who ran experimentation at Microsoft and Bing,
reports that only about **a third** of well-designed experiments there improved the metric
they were built to move; many of the rest did nothing, and some made it worse. At other
top experimentation teams the quoted win rate runs lower still. If most tests fail *at the
companies best at testing*, a losing result on your team is not a failure of the team —
it's the normal output of a working experiment loop.

That reframe is the whole conversation: a loss is a **learning you already paid for.**
You've spent the time and the traffic either way — the only way to waste that spend is to
hide what it taught you. So how you present a loss matters as much as the result itself:

* **State the hypothesis first, then the result.** "We believed a second onboarding email
  would lift activation; it didn't move — flat within noise." The hypothesis makes the
  loss legible: you tested a belief, and now you know something. A result with no stated
  hypothesis just looks like something broke.
* **Say what it ruled out.** A flat result narrows the search: "so the activation leak
  isn't email volume — it's earlier, in the first-run experience." That sentence is the
  asset the experiment bought. The rule *write the losers down too* exists because a
  learning that lives in one head gets re-paid-for six months later.
* **Show your tempo, not just this test.** One loss in isolation looks like a miss; the
  same loss as "test 7 of the quarter, here's what the last six taught us" looks like a
  machine working. Sean Ellis's case for high-tempo testing is exactly this: velocity
  compounds *because* individual tests fail, not despite it.

**What to take from it:**

* Capland makes failure safe *structurally*, not with vibes: the team's values go into an
  operating manual, and the first one is an explicit "we experiment to find out what works
  and what doesn't" — so a loss is the process working, not a person failing.
* You make it real by launching it like a feature — to execs, then the team, then the
  company — and reinforcing it weekly, in planning meetings and 1:1s that ask "what did you
  get to experiment on this week?" until reporting a loss becomes a routine answer.
* The accelerant is the leader dogfooding failure: standing up in front of the company with
  an experiment where their *own* hypothesis was dead wrong — the move that gives everyone
  else permission to present a flat result.

What you must never do: bury the loss, quietly stop reporting a journey that isn't
working, or go hunting through the data for the one sub-segment where it "won" so you have
a positive to show. That last one — p-hacking a win out of a loss — is the fastest way to
lose a numbers-literate stakeholder for good. They *can* smell it, and once they have,
every future win you present gets discounted too.

The counter-intuitive payoff sits one level above any single result: a leader who presents
their own flat test buys more credibility than a clean win would. It's proof the numbers
aren't being managed for the audience — and that proof is the only thing that makes the
wins believable.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Vanity metrics
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
The most common way to talk about growth *badly* is the **vanity metric** — a number
that reliably goes up, feels like progress, and tells you nothing you can act on. Eric
Ries named the category in *The Lean Startup*, splitting metrics into *vanity* and
*actionable*, and the test he proposed is the one to keep: when this number moves, does
it tell me what to do differently? If not, it's vanity.

The usual suspects share a shape:

* **Cumulative totals.** Total registered users, signups ever, **GMV** (gross merchandise
  value) to date. A running total can only go up — it literally cannot report a bad month —
  so it can't inform a single decision. a16z's [16 startup metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/) makes
  the point bluntly: show *active* users, not cumulative registered ones; the gap between
  the two is where the truth lives.
* **Raw reach.** Impressions, pageviews, follower counts. Big numbers that impress in a
  deck and disconnect entirely from value delivered — [Amplitude's definition](https://amplitude.com/blog/vanity-metrics)
  is that a vanity metric is one that can rise while the business goes nowhere.
* **Registered, not active.** "50,000 users" that's really 50,000 signups and 800
  weekly-actives. The number is true; the impression it creates is false.

Two tests catch all of them:

* **The "so what?" test.** After any number, ask: *what would I do differently if this were
  half, or double?* If there's no answer, it's context at best and theatre at worst.
* **Ratio and cohort over cumulative count.** A vanity metric becomes actionable the
  moment you make it a *rate* (activation %, week-4 retention, paid-conversion %) or split
  it by *cohort* (how does the group that joined in May retain, vs April?). Rates can go
  *down*, which is exactly what makes them useful; cohorts show whether the product is
  getting better or you're just adding more people to a leaky bucket.

The trap is that vanity metrics aren't lies — "10,000 signups" is true. They're *true
numbers that point away from the decision,* which is why they survive: they're defensible,
they flatter, and everyone nods along. The discipline is to lead with the ratio and keep
the big cumulative number where it belongs — as a trust signal for customers, never as
evidence for the team. It's the same honesty you'd apply to reading your own dashboard,
now turned on what you show other people.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Growth theatre and over-claiming
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Vanity metrics mislead by *choosing* the flattering number. A close relative misleads the
other way — by *overstating* what a real number proves. It shows up in two forms.

**Growth theatre.** Activity performed because it *looks* like growth work — the referral
program launched for the announcement, the "growth hack" copied from a thread, the
dashboard built to be shown rather than read. It's the **tactic collector** in a suit —
someone forever chasing the next growth hack with no stated leak to plug: constant motion,
nothing measured against a control. The tell is the same one — ask *which leak does this
plug?* and there's no answer, only *it's what growth teams do.*

**Over-claiming attribution.** The more damaging form, because it looks rigorous. "Our
Product Hunt launch drove 10,000 signups." "The tweet that went viral got us
500 customers." These are **last-click hero stories** — they credit the final touch,
ignore everything that primed it, and almost always present a *correlational* number as if
it were *causal*. The tool that exposes this is the holdout: what a channel *reports* and
what it actually *added* over doing nothing are different numbers, usually by a lot. A
channel can post a **ROAS** (return on ad spend — revenue divided by what you spent to earn
it) of 2.31 while its *incremental* ROAS, the return it truly added, is 2.78 — or half
that. Sometimes reality is higher, often it's lower; it is never automatically the reported
figure.

Why under-claiming is the better long-run strategy:

* **Over-claiming has a compounding cost.** The first time a stakeholder catches a hero
  story that doesn't survive a holdout, *every* future number you present gets a mental
  discount. Credibility is the currency a report runs on, and it's priced by your least
  defensible claim, not your best one.
* **Attribution honesty is a differentiator.** "We think the launch helped, but we can't
  cleanly separate it from the press that week — here's what we *can* measure" sounds
  weaker and lands stronger. It signals you know the difference between correlation and
  cause, which is precisely what makes your causal claims — the holdout ones — believable.
* **Hold the hero stories to the holdout bar.** Not every number needs an experiment, but
  any number you're using to *justify spending more* does. If you're about to triple a
  budget on the strength of "it drove 10k signups," that is exactly the claim to run
  incrementality on first.

The through-line with vanity metrics: both are ways of talking about growth that feel good
in the room and cost you later. The antidote is the same one — say the smaller, defensible
number, and say plainly what you can't yet prove.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: The one report you share
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Everything the course has built points at one artifact: a **single monthly one-pager** —
the board slide, the investor-update section, the thing you'd screenshot into the team
channel. If you can fill it honestly, the growth system is real. If you can't, it tells
you exactly which part isn't real yet.

It has four blocks and one sentence, in this order:

1. **North Star + trend.** The one metric that reflects value delivered — your North Star —
   shown as a *line over 3–6 months*, not a single number. The trend is the story; the
   point is trivia.
2. **Activation + retention.** The proof the bucket holds: activation rate for recent
   cohorts, and the retention curve or its key cohort number. This is the half of the page
   that says whether growth is real or churn-and-replace.
3. **Blended CAC + referral contribution.** The acquisition economics: **blended CAC** —
   what a customer costs on average across every channel — and the share of new users
   arriving through the owned audience and referral loop. Falling blended CAC as the owned
   audience grows is the flywheel showing up in the finances.
4. **One experiment learning.** Exactly one — stated as what you'll do differently, win or
   loss. One real learning beats ten bullet points, because it proves the loop turned this
   month.

Plus the sentence most decks omit: **the one thing you're worried about.** For a board
especially, naming the risk you'd want help on builds more trust than a flawless slide —
it's the difference between reporting *to* your board and working *with* it.

Notice what's *not* on it: total registered users, emails sent, impressions, pageviews. A
one-pager that survives the "so what?" test on every line is the entire course expressed
as a single page — one someone else can read in two minutes. And it's the report your
monthly cadence exists to produce — the one page the whole loop has been building toward.

It's worth seeing one filled in: here's an operator walking through exactly what goes into
a monthly investor update, and which metrics carry it.

**What to take from it:**

* Lead with the trend, not the snapshot — *investors invest in lines, not dots* (Mark
  Suster), so you send updates early and repeatedly and show at least a year of charts,
  exactly this atom's rule that the North Star is a line over 3–6 months, not a point.
* Put every metric next to its month-over-month change and name the lowlights — the
  template insists the lows go in as bullets ("tested a new social strategy, spent $2,500,
  results flat, turned it off"), which is this atom's one learning, win or loss, and its
  one-sentence worry.
* The metrics that carry the update are the economic levers — **CAC** (customer-acquisition
  cost, driven *down*, e.g. by a referral program), **LTV** (lifetime value, driven *up*,
  e.g. by annual billing or packaging), and churn — and the job is widening the gap between
  CAC and LTV, the blended-CAC-and-referral block of the one-pager here.

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
Two things worth doing while the chapter is fresh:

1. **Fill the one-pager for real.** The one-pager template only earns its keep once it
   has your numbers in it — and the blanks you *can't* fill are the most valuable output,
   because they name next month's work precisely.
2. **A look at [your workbook](/workbook).** The board line, the two-number story, the two
   cadence skeletons, the holdout explanation, the honest attribution rewrite, and the
   one-pager draft now sit together. That collection *is* how you'll talk about growth —
   in one place, each entry linked back to the piece that produced it.

The chapter in one line: **decide the altitude before you pick the number, pair every
claim with its mechanism, and say the smaller, defensible thing.** Talking about growth
honestly isn't a soft skill bolted onto the real work — it's the last stage of the same
loop, and the one that decides whether anyone acts on what you found.

***

***

## Go deeper

* [a16z — 16 startup metrics](https://a16z.com/16-startup-metrics/) — the canonical list of what investors read, and the "know which number you mean" discipline behind the two-number story.
* [YC — Key startup metrics](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/KR-key-startup-metrics) — what the outcome numbers on your one-pager eventually get measured against.
* [Brian Balfour — the weekly growth meeting](https://brianbalfour.com/essays/growth-meeting) — the fixed-agenda template the weekly-review skeleton compresses.
* [Reforge — operating cadence](https://www.reforge.com/blog/operating-cadence) — deciding what gets read daily, weekly, and monthly, and by whom.
* [Amplitude — vanity metrics](https://amplitude.com/blog/vanity-metrics) — the blunt definition: a number that rises while the business goes nowhere.
* [Eric Ries — The Lean Startup](http://theleanstartup.com/) — where the vanity-versus-actionable-metrics distinction comes from.
* [Eppo — marketing incrementality 101](https://www.geteppo.com/blog/marketing-incrementality-101) — the fuller version of "how do you know it worked," and why reported attribution overstates lift.
* [Lenny Rachitsky & Ronny Kohavi — the ultimate guide to A/B testing](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/the-ultimate-guide-to-ab-testing) — trust, invalid results, and why most experiments don't win.
* [First Round Review](https://review.firstround.com/) — an operator's library on board management and the metrics conversations founders most often get wrong.

And the books worth keeping on the shelf — tick them off as you read:

growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.title: Talking about growth (and how not to)
growth-with-posthog/13-talking-about-gro.text:
> **In this chapter:** who cares about which number and at what altitude, why a single
> number never persuades and the two-number story that does, and the two reporting
> cadences (weekly team review vs monthly board update). Then: how to explain a holdout
> to a skeptic without a stats lecture, how to present a losing experiment as the learning
> you already paid for, how to spot the vanity metrics and growth-theatre that mislead,
> and how to assemble the one monthly report the whole course has been building toward.
> This is the human skill the loop depends on — the stage that decides whether anyone acts
> on what you found.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into nine short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read, each with a prompt or a checklist that turns the idea into something
in your workbook. Work through them in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [Who cares about what](./01-who-cares-about-what.mdx) — four audiences, four altitudes, and the one-report-for-all mistake.
2. [The two-number story](./02-the-two-number-story.mdx) — pair a leading metric with its lagging outcome; one number never persuades.
3. [Two cadences, two reports](./03-reporting-cadence.mdx) — the weekly team review vs the monthly board update, with a skeleton for each.
4. [How do you know it worked?](./04-explaining-incrementality.mdx) — explaining a holdout to a skeptic, without a stats lecture.
5. [Presenting a loss](./05-presenting-a-losing-experiment.mdx) — a losing experiment is a learning you already paid for.
6. [Vanity metrics](./06-vanity-metrics.mdx) — the numbers that impress and mislead, and the "so what?" test that catches them.
7. [Growth theatre and over-claiming](./07-growth-theatre-and-over-claiming.mdx) — performing growth, last-click hero stories, and the credibility cost.
8. [The one report you share](./08-the-one-report-you-share.mdx) — assemble the single monthly one-pager the whole course points at.
9. [Before you move on](./09-before-you-move-on.mdx) — recap, then a scenario quiz on the whole chapter.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter.** The finance shorthand the board and investors talk
in, plus the one measurement idea the whole chapter leans on. Each is also defined inline
the first time it appears:

* **CAC** — customer-acquisition cost: what you spend to win one customer. **CAC payback** is how many months of margin it takes to earn that back; **blended CAC** is the cost averaged across every channel, paid and free.
* **LTV / LTV:CAC** — a customer's lifetime value, and that value set against what they cost to acquire.
* **MRR** — monthly recurring revenue.
* **GMV** — gross merchandise value: the total worth of goods sold through a marketplace (not the same as revenue).
* **ROAS** — return on ad spend: revenue divided by the money spent to earn it.
* **Net revenue retention** — the revenue you keep and expand from existing customers, after churn.
* **Activation rate** — the share of new users who reach first value.
* **North Star metric** — the single metric that best proxies the value users actually get from your product; the number the whole team steers by.
* **Holdout** — a random slice of users deliberately left untouched, so the gap between them and everyone else shows what a program actually added. A **global holdout** does this for a whole lifecycle program at once.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: What a growth capability is worth
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
Running a growth loop end to end — instrument a funnel, find where it leaks, ship a fix
across product and messaging, then prove the fix moved the number honestly — is a rare
skill, and rare skills buy you something specific. Not a salary figure this course won't
invent, but leverage: control over what you work on and how. That's what running the loop
is worth to a career.

The useful frame for it is Cal Newport's **career capital**, from *So Good They Can't
Ignore You* ([calnewport.com](https://calnewport.com)): the rare and valuable skills you
build are the capital you trade for the things people actually want from work — autonomy,
interesting problems, a say in what you do next. His argument runs against the usual
advice, that mastery tends to come *before* passion, not after it. You don't find a growth
role you love and then get good at it. It's the other way round — you get good at running
a loop, and the control that skill earns is what starts to make the work feel like yours.

A growth capability is rare *because* it spans functions. Plenty of people can run one
channel well — write ads, send emails, tune a signup page. Far fewer can hold the whole
loop in their head: read the funnel, pick the leak with the most leverage, build the fix
across product and messaging, and prove it moved with a **holdout** — a slice of users
deliberately kept out of the change, so the gap between them and everyone else is the
change's real effect — rather than a vanity chart that only ever shows the line going up.
That span is exactly what makes the skill hard to hire for, and hard to fake.

It's also a genuine discipline, not a job title someone invented. Andrew Chen's 2012 essay
"Growth Hacker is the new VP of Marketing" ([andrewchen.com](https://andrewchen.com))
named the shift: growth sits between product, engineering, and marketing, and it's driven
by data rather than taste. Product-led growth — a way of running a company where the
product and its usage data are the engine of growth — widened the demand, because such a
company needs people who think in loops and can instrument them, not just run campaigns.
Reforge ([reforge.com](https://www.reforge.com)) built a whole curriculum around treating
growth as a repeatable system rather than a bag of tricks, which is the same premise this
course runs on.

That capability shows up under three main titles — growth PM, growth marketer, growth
engineer — three doors into the same loop, which the piece on reading a growth job
description decodes. What matters here is the shape: a founder or growth leader values the
person who can run the loop end to end over a specialist in any single stage of it, because
one person who can do all of it replaces what would otherwise take a whole committee. A
veteran of that work puts it more sharply than a definition can.

**What to take from it:**

* Verna's whole list is one argument: the growth "hacks" people chase — copy a competitor's
  onboarding, redesign the homepage, hire a head of growth to rescue a slump — fail
  reliably, because growth *amplifies* a loop that already works rather than manufacturing
  one. Knowing that is the scarce skill: loop-thinking, not tactic-collecting.
* Her highest-leverage move is building an *earned* channel you own — virality,
  word-of-mouth, user-generated content, the way Dropbox draws ~50% of its acquisition from
  its own sharing loop — instead of renting attention from an algorithm. Seeing and building
  that loop is the cross-functional capability this chapter calls career capital.
* Copying competitors, she says, is "the fastest way to mediocrity": you can't lead by
  imitating. The operator who reads a funnel and designs the fix, rather than reaching for
  someone else's playbook, is the one who's hard to hire and hard to replace.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Your workbook is your portfolio
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
Most people applying for a growth role have a résumé that *asserts* things — "grew
activation," "owned lifecycle." You have something better: a record of the work itself.
The [workbook](/workbook) you filled in across this course is the raw material for a
portfolio, and a portfolio beats an assertion because it shows judgement, not just
outcomes.

Look at what's already in there. An **activation** sentence — the one line that says when a
new user has actually got value, not merely signed up — that you defined and defended. A
tracking plan: the events you chose to capture, and why those. A dashboard that reads the
funnel at a glance. **Lifecycle journeys** — automated message sequences that fire on what
a user does or doesn't do — that you actually shipped, with holdout results (a holdout is a
slice of users deliberately held back from the change, so the difference shows what it
added) that say whether they moved anything. A view on where traffic should come from and how you'd measure
it. A 30/60/90 plan for the next quarter. That is the full loop, documented, with your
reasoning attached — which is exactly the thing an interviewer can't get from a bullet
point.

The job now is compression. A portfolio nobody opens is worth nothing, so aim for a single
page (or one short doc, or a slide or two) that a hiring manager can read in two minutes
and a curious one can drill into. The shape that works: **the problem you saw in the data →
the fix you shipped → how you knew it worked (or didn't) → what you'd do next.** That last
clause matters more than it looks. Showing what you learned from a journey that *didn't*
move the metric reads as more credible than an unbroken run of wins — because real growth
work is mostly negative results.

One honest caveat: your course workbook is built on your own product, or a practice one.
That's a strength — self-directed work on a real funnel is rarer than it sounds — but say
so plainly rather than dressing it up as something it isn't. The attribution humility this
course keeps circling back to begins right here, in how you frame your own evidence.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Project vs. loop
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
There's a line an experienced interviewer is listening for, usually without saying so out
loud: is this person telling me about a *project* they ran once, or a *loop* they run? That
difference decides a surprising number of growth offers, so it's worth getting straight
before you tell your own story.

**A project** has a start and an end. "We wanted more signups, so we ran a referral push,
and signups went up 20%." It's a fine thing to have done — but it's a one-off, and it
leaves the interviewer with questions the candidate didn't answer: up 20% versus what?
Would they have signed up anyway? What happened after? Did it repeat?

**A loop** is a system that keeps running. Same story, told as a loop: "New users weren't
reaching activation, so I instrumented the drop, shipped an onboarding journey, and held
out 10% of them as a control to measure it. It lifted 30-day retention a few points against
that holdout, so I kept it and moved to the next-biggest leak — that measure-fix-measure
cycle is how I work." Notice what changed. There's a system, a control group, a decision
about where to go next, and a *cadence*. That's the growth team versus the team that ran a
growth project once.

Three things strong interviewers probe for, all of them ways of asking "loop or project?":

* **Systems thinking** — do you see the funnel as connected stages, or as isolated
  tactics? A loop-thinker fixes the leak with the most leverage; a project-thinker fixes
  whatever was asked for.
* **Measurement** — how did you *know* it worked? A number without a baseline is a story.
  A holdout, a control, a before/after with a comparison group is evidence.
* **Incrementality** — did the change cause the outcome, or ride along with it? This is the
  correlation-versus-causation discipline the course drills for your product, turned on your
  own résumé — and it's the single most common place candidates over-claim (the piece on
  attribution humility is about surviving that question honestly).

You don't have to have run a loop for years to tell it as one. You ran one in this course:
measure → keep → grow, with holdouts and a plan. Frame it honestly at that scope and it
still reads as loop-thinking, which is the thing being tested.

A clean way to package one story is **STAR** — Situation, Task, Action, Result — a
long-standing behavioural-interview structure that keeps you concrete and stops you
drifting into generalities. Land the Result on a measured outcome and, if you can, the
decision it led to next. Draft yours here:

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: The 90-day plan as an artifact
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
A large share of growth and product interviews end on the same question, and it's often the
deciding one: *what would your first 90 days here look like?* Sometimes it's a conversation;
sometimes it's a written take-home or a presentation. Either way, the deliverable it wants
is a **30/60/90 plan** — a one-page sketch of what you'd learn in the first thirty days, the
first fix you'd ship by sixty, and the win you'd prove by ninety. This course had you draft
exactly that shape for your own product; the work now is aiming that generic plan at a
specific company.

Why interviewers lean on the question: a 90-day plan reveals in one page how a candidate
thinks. It shows a few things at once — whether you'll spend month one *looking* before you
act, whether you sequence retention before acquisition or reach straight for the acquisition
tap, and whether you set gates you can actually be held to. That's why the plan works as an
interview deliverable almost unchanged — its whole spine is *learn the data first, find
early wins in activation and retention, only then scale acquisition* — activation being the
share of new users who reach first value, not just those who signed up — which is the same
first-90-days shape growth operators run when they land in a new role (Reforge's
first-90-days guide and First Round Review's [founder-led growth
playbook](https://review.firstround.com) both walk this arc).

What turns the generic plan into a *strong* answer is specificity to their business. Do the
homework the plan assumes:

* **Sign up as a real user.** Hit their onboarding, their paywall, their empty states — the
  way the plan's first month is mostly looking before acting. You'll walk in already knowing
  where their funnel probably leaks.
* **Name what you'd instrument first.** You can't promise results on data you haven't seen,
  so frame month one as measurement: "here's the funnel I'd want visible before I commit to
  projects." Restraint reads as maturity, not indecision.
* **Sequence honestly.** Put activation and retention work before paid scaling, and say why —
  the leaky-bucket argument, that filling a bucket faster is pointless while it leaks, is more
  persuasive than a promise to "drive growth."
* **Set gates, not just dates.** "By day 60 I'd want a holdout-measured lift on activation
  before we open the acquisition tap" tells them you measure before you spend — a holdout
  being a slice of new users deliberately left out of the change, so the gap between them
  and everyone else shows what the work actually added.

Say you're interviewing at a scheduling app. You sign up the night before, hit the
empty-calendar first run, and notice there's no nudge to book anything. Now your month-one
answer writes itself: "I'd want the funnel from signup to first booking instrumented, and
I'd look at where new users stall before their first event." It's specific, and it's
grounded in something you actually saw — not a number you promised sight unseen.

The trap to avoid is the confident-stranger plan: bold targets and specific tactics for a
funnel you've never seen. It reads as someone who'll act before they look. A plan that leads
with what you'd *measure* — and admits what you can't know from the outside — consistently
beats one that promises a number on day one.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Interviewing for growth
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
Growth interviews vary by company, but the question types are stable enough to prepare for.
Knowing the shape lets you rehearse the substance instead of being surprised by the format.

What you'll be asked falls into four recurring types, each testing a different muscle:

* **Metrics fluency.** "What metrics would you watch for this product? Which is the North
  Star, and why?" The North Star metric is the single number that best reflects the value
  customers get, and they're checking whether you can pick one that reflects that value
  rather than a vanity number — the value-versus-vanity judgement the course drills, live.
  Being able to explain why revenue is a *lagging* indicator — a scoreboard, not the thing
  you actually pull on — separates real fluency from jargon.
* **A past experiment.** "Walk me through a growth experiment you ran." This is the
  project-vs-loop test in disguise — tell it as a loop, land the result on lift against a
  control.
* **A product teardown.** "Sign up for our product — where's the funnel leaking, and what
  would you fix first?" They want to see you *look* before you prescribe, and prioritise by
  leverage rather than by whatever's most visible.
* **A case study or take-home.** Bigger roles often include a written exercise or a
  presentation — frequently the first-90-days plan itself, or a "how would you grow X"
  prompt. Guides on preparing for PM and growth interviews (Lenny's Newsletter,
  [lennysnewsletter.com](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com)) consistently make the same
  point: judgement and structure beat a pile of tactics.

The throughline: in every one of these, **measurement judgement beats buzzwords**. The
candidate who says "I'd want a holdout before I claim that worked" outscores the one who
lists ten growth hacks, because the first sounds like someone who's been accountable for a
number and the second sounds like someone who's read about it.

The questions you ask them are themselves a signal — they reveal whether you think like an
operator. Strong ones probe the conditions you'd need to actually do the job: *Is the product
instrumented, and will I have access to the data? Who owns the growth loop today? What's been
tried, and how did you know whether it worked? Is growth in the same backlog as product, or a
separate function?* Their answers also tell *you* whether the role is real — reading a growth
job description is the field guide to those signals.

Lenny Rachitsky has asked variations of these questions across 100-plus podcast guests, and
the reel below is the shortlist of the ones strong operators reach for — worth the few
minutes for both sides of the table:

**What to take from it:**

* Almost every favourite question probes *judgement and self-awareness*, not tactics: "the
  hardest thing you've ever done" (agency when it's hard), "a time something went wrong" (how
  you relate to failure), "the work you're most proud of" (your taste, and what "good" looks
  like to you). That's this atom's "measurement judgement beats buzzwords," heard from the
  interviewer's chair.
* Strong operators simplify, asking the highest-information questions. Coda's founder uses a
  teleportation-device test: you get only two questions before you must produce a plan, and
  the good answers cut straight to the decisions that matter ("is it safe for humans?",
  "capex or opex?") instead of piling on detail — the same instinct as the teardown's "look
  before you prescribe."
* The best questions are built to break a rehearsed script. "What conventional wisdom do you
  bet against?" and "three years from now, what's different about you?" are designed to get
  past what the candidate thinks the interviewer wants to hear and surface a genuine answer —
  the person who sounds like they've been accountable for a number, not one who's only read
  about it.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Reading a growth job description
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
"Growth" on a job posting covers at least three different jobs, and telling them apart
before you apply saves you from interviewing for a role you didn't want. The same title can
mean a data-driven loop-owner at one company and a cold-email operator at another, so read
the responsibilities, not the header.

Those three jobs are really three doors into the same loop, each entering from a different
stage and weighted toward it. Work out which stage a posting leans on and you already know
most of what the role is:

* **Growth marketer** — leans on the acquisition and top-of-funnel stages: channels,
  campaigns, messaging, landing pages. The best of these still measure and run experiments.
  The weakest just mean "do marketing tasks," with the word *growth* bolted on to make the
  role sound bigger than it is.
* **Growth PM** — owns a slice of the funnel as a product surface: activation, onboarding,
  retention, monetisation. Runs the experiment backlog, works across engineering and design,
  lives in the metrics. Closest to the whole-loop role.
* **Growth engineer** — builds the machinery: instrumentation, experimentation tooling, the
  pipelines and internal surfaces that let the loop run fast. If you came to this course as
  an engineer, this is often the most natural door.

Whichever door it is, the same handful of signals tell you the loop underneath is real —
that the role genuinely owns a number and is given the means to move it:

* A described loop, not a list of channels — the posting talks about measuring, iterating,
  and retention, not just "drive signups."
* Explicit **data access** and instrumentation. If the role owns a metric, you need to be
  able to see it; a posting that mentions the analytics stack is telling you the loop exists.
* Experimentation as a norm — A/B tests, holdouts, a backlog. It signals a team that knows
  the difference between activity and lift.
* Growth sits with product and engineering, not stranded in a marketing silo.

The absence of those signals is its own tell. A few phrases give away a channel job wearing
a growth costume:

* *Growth = cold outreach only.* If the whole role is outbound volume with no analytics,
  it's a sales-development job relabelled.
* No mention of data, metrics, or measurement anywhere in the responsibilities.
* "Growth hacking" as vibe — a wish-list of tactics with no system, no ownership of a
  number, no instrumentation.
* Everything is acquisition; nothing about activation or retention. That's a leaky-bucket
  team — one losing users about as fast as it wins them — hiring someone to pour water in
  faster instead of plugging the holes.

Reforge's writing on growth roles and org structure
([reforge.com](https://www.reforge.com)) is the fullest map of how these functions differ
in practice, if you want to go deeper on which door fits you. The short version: match the
posting's *verbs* to the stage of the loop you most want to own, and treat data access as
non-negotiable — a growth role without it is a growth role in name only.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Attribution humility
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
There's a temptation, when you're selling yourself, to round your impact up. "I grew
activation 3x." "My campaign drove the quarter." Resist it — because the person across the
table has almost certainly run these numbers themselves, and they know how hard attribution
really is. Over-claiming doesn't read as confidence. It reads as someone who's never had to
stand behind a number and defend it.

This is the same honesty the course argues for throughout, now pointed at your own story.
**Attribution** — pinning a result to the thing that actually caused it — is genuinely hard
on a live product, because several things move at once: a pricing change ships, a seasonal
bump lands, the base rate drifts on its own. A change that *correlates* with a metric didn't
necessarily *cause* it. That's the correlation-versus-causation discipline the course keeps
returning to; if you internalised it for your product, apply it to your résumé too.

The credible move is to pair any claim with **how you'd know**. Compare:

* *Over-claim:* "I increased retention 40%."
* *Honest, and stronger:* "We shipped an onboarding journey and held out 10% of new users.
  30-day retention ran a few points higher in the treated group over two months. I'd want a
  longer window and a repeat before I called it durable, but the holdout gives me confidence
  the journey caused most of it."

The second version is longer and it's *more* persuasive, because everything in it is
checkable. It shows you know what a holdout is for, that you distinguish lift from activity,
and that you know the limits of your own evidence. To a growth leader, that's the whole job —
you're going to be trusted to spend money and report on it, and they're hiring the person
who won't fool them or themselves.

Saying "I don't know for certain, but here's how I'd find out" is not a weakness in a growth
