interview. It's the single clearest signal that you've actually done the work, because only
people who've measured things honestly know how much they don't know. The candidate claiming
a clean 10x with no control group is telling you they haven't.

Take one line from your own story — the one you were tempted to round up — and rewrite it as
the honest version:

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Build your own audience
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
A business is safest when it builds an audience it *owns* — an email list, a community, a
direct line to its users — rather than renting attention from a platform that can change the
rules overnight. This course made that case for products; the same logic points straight at
your own career, and it's a fitting note to close on.

An owned audience for *you* is the people who know what you can do because you've shown
them — through writing, building in public, a newsletter, talks, or just a habit of posting
what you're learning. It's a career flywheel: the work you do generates things worth sharing,
sharing them reaches people, and some of those people bring opportunities — which generate
more work worth sharing. Andrew Chen's own trajectory
([andrewchen.com](https://andrewchen.com)) is a clean example of the pattern — years of
writing about growth became the platform for everything that followed. This isn't a claim
that everyone must become a creator. It's that a small, genuine audience compounds quietly
in the background of a career — and it costs very little to start.

It works for the same reason it worked for your product. You **own the relationship** — it
doesn't depend on a recruiter's inbox or a job board's algorithm. It's **compounding** — a
body of writing keeps working while you sleep, the way a retained user keeps paying back
their acquisition cost. And it's **differentiating** — most candidates are a résumé; someone
who's visibly done and explained the work stands out before the interview starts.

You don't need a large following for this to pay off. A handful of the right people —
founders, growth leaders, peers who'd vouch for you — is worth more than a big anonymous
number, which is exactly the owned-audience-over-vanity-metric lesson from earlier in the
course, applied to you. And you already have the raw material: the portfolio you packaged
earlier in this chapter, the honest write-ups of what worked and what didn't. The first move
is small — publish one thing.

An operator who has built exactly this kind of audience makes the mechanics concrete:

**What to take from it:**

* Robinson's core move is being useful in public: show what's possible, then teach people
  how to build it themselves. Trust compounds over many touches — his sceptical audience,
  developers, tends to adopt around the *tenth* time they hear about something, not the
  first — which is why a small owned audience pays back slowly, then all at once.
* Writing is the compounding asset, and his process makes it repeatable: ideas sit as
  bullets in a notes app until "this needs to be in the world," a rough first draft,
  feedback from people you trust, then a rewrite that moves the conclusion to the top. A
  tweet is the small version that later expands into the post — start smaller than a post.
* Write like you talk: cut jargon and hedging, lead with the bottom line, keep it skimmable.
  The point isn't polish — it's being genuinely useful, which is what earns the handful of
  right people (this atom's owned audience) over a vanity follower count.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Before you move on
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
The loop you ran in this course is career capital, and this closing chapter's one job was
to repackage it into evidence someone will hire on. By now you have a portfolio (your
workbook), a story told as a loop rather than a project
(the STAR draft), a first-90-days plan aimed at a real company, an honest way to talk
about your impact, and a plan to start building an audience of your own. Everything you
wrote is in [your workbook](/workbook), each entry linked back to the piece that produced
it — that page is your growth portfolio now.

Check yourself on the whole chapter:

***

## Go deeper

* [Cal Newport — So Good They Can't Ignore You](https://calnewport.com) — the "career capital" argument this chapter's title borrows: build rare skills first, trade them for control over your work.
* [Reforge](https://www.reforge.com) — the fullest map of growth as a repeatable discipline, and of how growth-marketing, growth-PM, and growth-engineering roles differ in practice.
* [Andrew Chen](https://andrewchen.com) — growth as a cross-functional discipline ("Growth Hacker is the new VP of Marketing"), and a living example of an owned audience compounding into a career.
* [Lenny's Newsletter](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com) — practical guides on growth and product careers, interviewing, and case studies; the reference most operators point new candidates to.
* [First Round Review](https://review.firstround.com) — operator-written pieces on founder-led growth and early growth hiring, useful for calibrating what a first-90-days plan should show.

***

A few books for the long game — tick them off as you read:

***

This is the last chapter of the course. You started by learning to measure a funnel; you
finish able to run the whole loop — measure, keep, grow — and to trade that capability for
the kind of work you want. The machine you built runs on your product now, and the
evidence that you can build it is in [your workbook](/workbook). Go publish one thing.

growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.title: Growth as career capital
growth-with-posthog/14-growth-as-career-.text:
> **In this chapter:** what a growth capability is worth right now, and why people who can
> run the whole loop are scarce. How the workbook you filled in across this course is
> already a portfolio. The difference between "I ran a growth project" and "I run a growth
> loop" — and how to tell your story so it lands as the second one. How to turn your
> 30/60/90 plan into an interview deliverable, the questions you'll be asked (and the ones
> to ask back), how to read a growth job description, and how to talk about your impact
> without over-claiming. And to close, the course's own owned-audience lesson, pointed back
> at your own career. By the end you'll have the artifacts and the language to trade what
> you learned here for a role.

**How this chapter works.** It's broken into nine short pieces — most are a one- to
three-minute read. This is the last chapter of the course, so it's less about new
technique and more about repackaging what you already did into evidence someone will
hire on. Several pieces end with something to write; those save to your workbook next to
the rest of your work. Go in order, or jump to what you need:

1. [What a growth capability is worth](./01-what-its-worth.mdx) — rare skills you can trade for good work.
2. [Your workbook is your portfolio](./02-your-workbook-is-your-portfolio.mdx) — the loop you built is already the evidence.
3. [Project vs. loop](./03-project-vs-loop.mdx) — the one distinction strong interviewers probe for.
4. [The 90-day plan as an artifact](./04-the-90-day-plan-as-artifact.mdx) — turn your 30/60/90 plan into an interview deliverable.
5. [Interviewing for growth](./05-interviewing-for-growth.mdx) — the questions you'll be asked, and the ones to ask.
6. [Reading a growth job description](./06-reading-a-growth-jd.mdx) — green flags, red flags, and what titles really mean.
7. [Attribution humility](./07-attribution-humility.mdx) — why the honest candidate beats the one claiming 10x.
8. [Build your own audience](./08-build-your-own-audience.mdx) — the owned-audience lesson, applied to you.
9. [Before you move on](./09-before-you-move-on.mdx) — a recap, a quiz on the whole chapter, and where to read more.

**Words you'll meet in this chapter**

* **Career capital** — Cal Newport's term for the rare, valuable skills you accumulate and trade for autonomy, interesting problems, and a real say in what you work on.
* **Activation** — the moment a new user first gets genuine value from a product (say, `project_created` fired within seven days of signup), not merely the moment they sign up.
* **Lifecycle journey** — an automated sequence of messages triggered by what a user does or doesn't do as they move through a product: onboarding nudges, re-engagement, dunning.
* **Holdout** — a slice of users deliberately kept out of a change, so the gap between them and everyone else measures the change's true effect rather than a coincidence.
* **Incrementality** — whether a change actually *caused* an outcome, rather than merely moving alongside it.
